questions.specification: A delivery drone follows a predetermined route over a city: The drone starts from point $O$ and flies $10$ km north to point $A$. From point $A$, it changes direction and flies $6$ km due west to point $B$. After reaching point $B$, the drone flies $8$ km south to point $C$. Finally, the drone flies $5$ km east back to point $D$.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic A - Space, time and motion > A.1 Kinematics > A.1.1 Describing motion quantitatively and qualitatively
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.1.1 Describing motion quantitatively and qualitatively
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question focuses on interpreting a 2D journey using vectors: total distance vs displacement, magnitude and direction of displacement between points, and comparing displacement direction to due north—core qualitative/quantitative description of motion.

parts.content: Determine the total distance the drone travels during the entire journey.
parts.markscheme:
Calculate the sum of all segments: $10 + 6 + 8 + 5 = 29$ km <marks>A1</marks>

[Total: 1 mark]

steps.title: Define distance as scalar
steps.content: In physics, specifically within **Topic A.1: Kinematics**, we distinguish between distance and displacement. Distance is a **scalar quantity**, meaning it represents the total ground covered by the object during its motion, regardless of direction. To find the total distance, we simply sum the lengths of every part of the journey.

steps.title: Identify journey segments
steps.content:
From the problem description, we can identify four distinct segments of the drone's flight:

1.  **Segment 1 ($OA$):** $10$ km
2.  **Segment 2 ($AB$):** $6$ km
3.  **Segment 3 ($BC$):** $8$ km
4.  **Segment 4 ($CD$):** $5$ km

steps.title: Sum the lengths
steps.content:
Using the **Direct Arithmetic Summation** approach, we add the magnitude of each segment together to find the total distance traveled:

$$\text{Total Distance} = 10 + 6 + 8 + 5$$

gdc.instructions: On the main calculation screen, input the addition of the four distances.

ti84.instructions: Enter the sum of the four segment lengths directly on the home screen and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Type the sum of the segments into the expression bar.

calculator.description: Calculate the sum of all journey segments to find the total distance.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
Performing the addition gives us the final result:

$$\text{Total Distance} = 29 \text{ km}$$

In an IB exam, showing this simple sum is sufficient to earn the **A1** mark for a correct numerical answer with appropriate units.

parts.content: Calculate the drone’s net displacement from point $O$ to point $D$, both in magnitude and direction.
parts.markscheme:
Determine net displacement components: $x = -6 + 5 = -1$ km (West) and $y = 10 - 8 = 2$ km (North)

Substitute into Pythagorean theorem for magnitude: $\sqrt{(-1)^2 + 2^2}$ <marks>M1</marks>

Magnitude $= \sqrt{5} \approx 2.24$ km <marks>A1</marks>

Direction $= \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{2}{1}\right) \approx 63.4^\circ$ north of west <marks>A1</marks>

[Total: 3 marks]

parts.content: What is the displacement from point $A$ to point $C$ in terms of both magnitude and direction?
parts.markscheme:
Magnitude: $\sqrt{6^2 + 8^2} = \sqrt{36 + 64} = 10$ km <marks>A1</marks>

Direction: $\tan^{-1}\left(\frac{8}{6}\right) \approx 53.1^\circ$ south of west <marks>A1</marks>

[Total: 2 marks]

parts.content: If the drone completes the route in $1$ hour and $15$ minutes, calculate its average speed and average velocity.
parts.markscheme:
Average speed $= \frac{\text{distance}}{\text{time}} = \frac{29 \text{ km}}{1.25 \text{ h}} = 23.2$ km/h <marks>A1</marks>

Average velocity $= \frac{\text{displacement}}{\text{time}} = \frac{2.24 \text{ km}}{1.25 \text{ h}} \approx 1.79$ km/h <marks>A1</marks>

Direction of average velocity $= 63.4^\circ$ north of west <marks>A1</marks>

<note>Accept answers rounded to 2 or 3 significant figures.</note>

[Total: 3 marks]

parts.content: Compare the direction of the net displacement from $O$ to $D$ to due north.
parts.markscheme:
Identify that net displacement is $63.4^\circ$ north of west <marks>A1</marks>

Angle relative to due north is $90^\circ - 63.4^\circ = 26.6^\circ$ west of north <marks>A1</marks>

[Total: 2 marks]

steps.title: Define the coordinate system
steps.content: To use the **Component Summation** approach, we first assign directions to a Cartesian grid. Let's define North as the positive $y$-direction ($+y$) and East as the positive $x$-direction ($+x$). Conversely, South is $-y$ and West is $-x$.

steps.title: Sum the vertical components
steps.content:
First, we calculate the total North-South displacement ($\Delta y$) by adding the individual segments:

$$\Delta y = (+10 \text{ km North}) + (-8 \text{ km South})$$
$$\Delta y = +2 \text{ km (North)}$$

ti84.instructions: Enter the calculation on the home screen.

calculator.description: Calculate the net North-South displacement.

steps.title: Sum the horizontal components
steps.content:
Next, we calculate the total East-West displacement ($\Delta x$):

$$\Delta x = (-6 \text{ km West}) + (+5 \text{ km East})$$
$$\Delta x = -1 \text{ km (West)}$$

ti84.instructions: Enter the calculation on the home screen.

calculator.description: Calculate the net East-West displacement.

steps.title: Calculate angle North of West
steps.content:
The drone is now $1$ km West and $2$ km North of its starting position. We can find the angle $\theta$ relative to the West axis using the tangent function:

$$\tan(\theta) = \frac{\text{Opposite (North)}}{\text{Adjacent (West)}} = \frac{2}{1}$$
$$\theta = \arctan(2) \approx 63.4^\circ$$

This indicates the displacement is $63.4^\circ$ North of West.

gdc.instructions: Set the angle unit to degrees and calculate the inverse tangent.

ti84.instructions: Ensure the calculator is in DEGREE mode. Press [2nd] [TAN] 2 [ENTER].

desmos.instructions: Click the wrench icon to set to degrees, then type the expression.

calculator.description: Find the angle in degrees using the inverse tangent.

steps.title: Find angle relative to North
steps.content:
The question asks to compare the direction to **due north**. Since North and West are $90^\circ$ apart, we subtract our calculated angle from $90^\circ$:

$$\text{Angle from North} = 90^\circ - 63.4^\circ = 26.6^\circ$$

Because the drone is to the West of the North axis, the direction is $26.6^\circ$ West of North.

calculator.description: Subtract the result from 90 to find the angle relative to North.

steps.title: Map the coordinates
steps.content:
To find the direction of the net displacement, we first need to determine the final position of point $D$ relative to the start point $O(0,0)$ on a coordinate plane.

$$\begin{aligned} O &= (0, 0) \\ A &= (0, 10) & \text{(10 km North)} \\ B &= (-6, 10) & \text{(6 km West)} \\ C &= (-6, 2) & \text{(8 km South: } 10 - 8 = 2) \\ D &= (-1, 2) & \text{(5 km East: } -6 + 5 = -1) \end{aligned}$$

Point $D$ is located $1$ km West and $2$ km North of the origin.

steps.title: Form the right-angled triangle
steps.content:
By connecting $O(0,0)$ to $D(-1,2)$, we form a right-angled triangle. 

- The horizontal leg (West) has a length of $1$ km.
- The vertical leg (North) has a length of $2$ km.

We can calculate the angle $\alpha$ north of the West axis using the tangent ratio:

$$\tan(\alpha) = \frac{\text{opposite}}{\text{adjacent}} = \frac{\text{North component}}{\text{West component}} = \frac{2}{1}$$
$$\alpha = \arctan(2) \approx 63.4^\circ$$

steps.title: Use calculator for angle
steps.content: Using your GDC, ensure the mode is set to **Degrees** to find the inverse tangent.

gdc.instructions:
1. Go to the Calculation app.
2. Ensure the angle unit is set to Degree.
3. Enter arctan(2) or tan⁻¹(2).

ti84.instructions:
1. Press [MODE], ensure 'DEGREE' is highlighted.
2. Press [2nd] [TAN] (tan⁻¹).
3. Type '2'.
4. Press [ENTER].

desmos.instructions:
1. Click the wrench icon (Settings) and select 'Degrees'.
2. Type 'arctan(2)'.

calculator.description: Calculate the angle North of West using the inverse tangent function.

steps.title: Convert to North-relative angle
steps.content:
The question asks for the direction relative to **due north**. Since North and West are $90^\circ$ apart, we subtract our previous angle from $90^\circ$:

$$\text{Angle West of North} = 90^\circ - 63.4^\circ = 26.6^\circ$$

Alternatively, we could have calculated this directly by taking the North leg as the adjacent side:
$$\tan(\theta) = \frac{\text{West component}}{\text{North component}} = \frac{1}{2}$$
$$\theta = \arctan(0.5) = 26.6^\circ$$

This confirms the displacement is $26.6^\circ$ West of North.

parts.content: Explain how the distance travelled and the net displacement provide different information about the drone’s journey.
parts.markscheme:
State that distance measures the total length of the route taken ($29$ km), which is a scalar quantity <marks>R1</marks>

State that displacement is a vector quantity representing the straight-line change in position from start ($O$) to finish ($D$), which is smaller than the distance due to direction changes ($2.24$ km) <marks>R1</marks>

[Total: 2 marks]

parts.content: If the drone needed to return directly to point $O$ from point $D$, what would be the required distance and direction?
parts.markscheme:
Distance $= 2.24$ km <marks>A1</marks>

Direction $= 63.4^\circ$ south of east (or $26.6^\circ$ east of south) <marks>A1</marks>

<note>Accept equivalent direction descriptions like $153.4^\circ$ bearing or equivalent.</note>

[Total: 2 marks]

steps.title: Define the coordinate system
steps.content:
To solve this using component summation, we first establish a coordinate system where North and East are positive, and South and West are negative. This is a fundamental concept in **Topic A.1 Kinematics** for describing motion in two dimensions.

- **North-South (y-axis):** North is $+$, South is $-$.
- **East-West (x-axis):** East is $+$, West is $-$.

steps.title: Sum the horizontal components
steps.content:
First, we sum the East-West (horizontal) displacements to find the final x-coordinate of point $D$ relative to the start $O$:

$$\begin{aligned} \Delta x &= 0 \text{ (North)} - 6 \text{ (West)} + 0 \text{ (South)} + 5 \text{ (East)} \\ \Delta x &= -1 \text{ km} \end{aligned}$$

This means point $D$ is $1$ km West of point $O$.

steps.title: Sum the vertical components
steps.content:
Next, we sum the North-South (vertical) displacements to find the final y-coordinate of point $D$ relative to $O$:

$$\begin{aligned} \Delta y &= +10 \text{ (North)} + 0 \text{ (West)} - 8 \text{ (South)} + 0 \text{ (East)} \\ \Delta y &= +2 \text{ km} \end{aligned}$$

This means point $D$ is $2$ km North of point $O$.

steps.title: Determine the return vector
steps.content:
To return directly from $D$ to $O$, the drone must reverse the net displacement. 

Since point $D$ is at $(-1, +2)$, the return path to the origin $(0,0)$ must be:
- **East-West component:** $+1$ km (East)
- **North-South component:** $-2$ km (South)

steps.title: Calculate the return distance
steps.content:
We find the total distance using the Pythagorean theorem, which is the magnitude of the displacement vector:

$$\text{Distance} = \sqrt{(1)^2 + (-2)^2} = \sqrt{1 + 4} = \sqrt{5} \approx 2.24 \text{ km}$$

gdc.instructions: Use the square root function and input 5.

calculator.description: Calculate the square root of 5 to find the distance.

steps.title: Determine the return direction
steps.content:
We use trigonometry to find the angle $\theta$ relative to the East-West axis:

$$\tan \theta = \frac{\text{Opposite (South)}}{\text{Adjacent (East)}} = \frac{2}{1}$$
$$\theta = \arctan(2) \approx 63.4^\circ$$

Since the components are $1$ km East and $2$ km South, the direction is $63.4^\circ$ South of East.

gdc.instructions: Use the atan or tan⁻¹ function with the calculator in degree mode.

ti84.instructions: Ensure the mode is set to DEGREES. Press [2nd] [tan] (tan⁻¹), type 2, and press [ENTER].

desmos.instructions: Click the wrench icon to ensure 'Degrees' is selected. Type 'arctan(2)'.

calculator.description: Calculate the arctangent of 2 to find the direction angle.

steps.title: Final Result
steps.content: The drone needs to travel **$2.24$ km** at a direction of **$63.4^\circ$ south of east** (or $26.6^\circ$ east of south).

steps.title: Define the coordinate system
steps.content:
To solve this using coordinate geometry, let's place the starting point $O$ at the origin $(0,0)$. We will define North as the positive $y$-direction and East as the positive $x$-direction.

$$\begin{aligned} \text{North} &= +y \\ \text{South} &= -y \\ \text{East} &= +x \\ \text{West} &= -x \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Determine coordinates of Point D
steps.content:
Let's track the drone's position step-by-step to find the coordinates of point $D$:

1.  **Start at $O$**: $(0, 0)$
2.  **Point $A$** (10 km North): $(0, 10)$
3.  **Point $B$** (6 km West): $(-6, 10)$
4.  **Point $C$** (8 km South): $(-6, 10 - 8) = (-6, 2)$
5.  **Point $D$** (5 km East): $(-6 + 5, 2) = (-1, 2)$

So, the coordinates of point $D$ are $(-1, 2)$.

highlights.text: 6 km due west to point B
5 km east back to point D

steps.title: Calculate the return vector
steps.content:
To return from $D(-1, 2)$ back to $O(0, 0)$, we need to calculate the change in position ($Δx$ and $Δy$):

$$\begin{aligned} \Delta x &= x_{\text{final}} - x_{\text{initial}} = 0 - (-1) = +1 \text{ km (East)} \\ \Delta y &= y_{\text{final}} - y_{\text{initial}} = 0 - 2 = -2 \text{ km (South)} \end{aligned}$$

This means to go from $D$ to $O$, the drone must fly 1 km East and 2 km South.

steps.title: Calculate the direct distance
steps.content:
Using the Pythagorean theorem (or the distance formula), we can find the direct distance $d$ from $D$ to $O$:

$$\begin{aligned} d &= \sqrt{(\Delta x)^2 + (\Delta y)^2} \\ d &= \sqrt{1^2 + (-2)^2} \\ d &= \sqrt{1 + 4} = \sqrt{5} \end{aligned}$$

Using a calculator, $\sqrt{5} \approx 2.24$ km.

ti84.instructions: Press [2nd] [x²] (square root), then type 5 and press [ENTER].

desmos.instructions: Type sqrt(5) into the input bar.

calculator.description: Calculate the square root of 5 to find the distance.

steps.title: Determine the direction
steps.content:
We can find the angle $\theta$ south of east using the tangent ratio:

$$\begin{aligned} \tan \theta &= \frac{|\Delta y|}{|\Delta x|} = \frac{2}{1} \\ \theta &= \arctan(2) \end{aligned}$$

Using a calculator, we find $\theta \approx 63.4^\circ$.

Since we are moving in the $+x$ direction (East) and $-y$ direction (South), the direction is **$63.4^\circ$ south of east**.

gdc.instructions: Set angle mode to degrees. Enter arctan(2) or tan⁻¹(2) and press execute.

ti84.instructions: Ensure the mode is in DEGREES. Press [2nd] [TAN] (tan⁻¹), type 2, and press [ENTER].

desmos.instructions: Set settings to Degrees. Type arctan(2) or tan^-1(2).

calculator.description: Calculate the inverse tangent to find the direction angle in degrees.

steps.title: Final Answer
steps.content: The drone must fly a distance of **2.24 km** at a direction of **$63.4^\circ$ south of east**.

steps.title: Establish a scale
steps.content:
To solve this using a scale drawing, we first need to choose a convenient scale. Let's use:

$$1 \text{ cm} = 1 \text{ km}$$

This will allow the $10$ km flight to fit easily on a standard piece of graph paper.

steps.title: Construct the path
steps.content:
Using a ruler and a protractor (or grid lines for cardinal directions), draw the journey starting from the origin $O$:
1.  **$O \rightarrow A$**: Draw a $10$ cm line straight up (North).
2.  **$A \rightarrow B$**: From $A$, draw a $6$ cm line to the left (West).
3.  **$B \rightarrow C$**: From $B$, draw an $8$ cm line straight down (South).
4.  **$C \rightarrow D$**: From $C$, draw a $5$ cm line to the right (East).

steps.title: Locate return vector
steps.content: Now, identify point $D$ and the starting point $O$. To return directly, the drone must travel from $D$ back to $O$. Draw a straight line connecting $D$ to $O$. This line represents the displacement vector we need to find.

steps.title: Measure return distance
steps.content:
Using your ruler, measure the length of the line segment $DO$. 

You should find that the length is approximately $2.24$ cm. 

Since our scale is $1 \text{ cm} = 1 \text{ km}$, the required distance is:
$$\text{Distance} = 2.24 \text{ km}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the expected magnitude of the vector from D(-1, 2) to O(0, 0).

steps.title: Measure return direction
steps.content:
To find the direction, place the center of your protractor at point $D$ and align the baseline with the East-West line. 

Measuring the angle from the East line downwards toward $O$, you will find:
$$\theta \approx 63.4^\circ$$

This direction is described as $63.4^\circ$ South of East.

gdc.instructions: Use atan to find the angle in degrees.

calculator.description: Verify the angle using trigonometry (tan θ = opposite/adjacent).

steps.title: Final summary
steps.content:
By accurately drawing and measuring, we determine the drone must fly:
- **Distance:** $2.24$ km
- **Direction:** $63.4^\circ$ south of east (or a bearing of $153.4^\circ$)

questions.specification: A rock is projected at an angle of $30^\circ$ above the horizontal with a speed of $20.0\ \text{m s}^{-1}$. Assume air resistance is negligible.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic A - Space, time and motion > A.1 Kinematics > A.1.3 Projectile motion
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.1.3 Projectile motion
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: All parts (time to highest point, maximum height, range, and independence of horizontal/vertical components) are standard projectile motion with negligible air resistance.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic A - Space, time and motion > A.1 Kinematics > A.1.2 Equations of motion
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.1.2 Equations of motion
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Requires using constant-acceleration SUVAT equations in vertical motion and uniform motion horizontally to compute time, height, and range.

parts.content: Calculate the time taken for the rock to reach the highest point of its trajectory.
parts.markscheme:
- Vertical component: $v_y = 20.0 \sin(30^\circ) = 10.0 \text{ m s}^{-1}$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Time to top: $t = \frac{v_y}{g} = \frac{10.0}{9.81} = 1.02 \text{ s}$ <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Analyze the vertical motion
steps.content:
In projectile motion, we treat the horizontal and vertical components of motion independently. To find the time taken to reach the peak, we focus solely on the vertical direction. This concept is covered in **Topic A.1.3 Projectile motion**.

At the highest point of its trajectory, the rock's vertical velocity becomes momentarily zero before it begins to descend.

steps.title: Calculate initial vertical velocity
steps.content:
First, we find the vertical component of the initial velocity ($u_y$) using the launch speed and angle:

$$u_y = u \sin(\theta)$$

Substituting the given values:

$$u_y = 20.0 \cdot \sin(30^\circ) = 10.0 \text{ m s}^{-1}$$

This calculation earns the first mark.

steps.title: Apply the kinematic equation
steps.content:
We use the kinematic equation for constant acceleration from the data booklet:

$$\text{Vertical motion: } v_y = u_y + a_y t$$

Where:
- $v_y = 0 \text{ m s}^{-1}$ (at the highest point)
- $u_y = 10.0 \text{ m s}^{-1}$
- $a_y = -g = -9.8 \text{ m s}^{-2}$ (acceleration due to gravity acting downwards)

steps.title: Solve for time
steps.content:
Now, we substitute the values into the equation and solve for $t$:

$$\begin{aligned} 0 &= 10.0 + (-9.8)t \\ 9.8t &= 10.0 \\ t &= \frac{10.0}{9.8} \end{aligned}$$

Performing the calculation gives us the time to reach the top.

gdc.instructions: Perform the division on the calculation screen.

ti84.instructions: Enter the division calculation on the main screen.

desmos.instructions: Enter the expression into the calculator.

calculator.description: Calculate the time taken by dividing the initial vertical velocity by gravity.

steps.title: Final answer
steps.content:
The calculated time is:

$$t \approx 1.02 \text{ s}$$

(Note: Using $g = 9.81 \text{ m s}^{-2}$ as commonly seen in IB markschemes results in the same value of $1.02 \text{ s}$ when rounded to three significant figures).

steps.title: Calculate vertical initial velocity
steps.content:
To analyze the vertical motion, we first find the vertical component of the initial velocity ($u_y$). This is done by taking the sine of the launch angle. 

$$\begin{aligned} u_y &= u \sin \theta \\ u_y &= 20.0 \sin(30^\circ) \\ u_y &= 10.0 \text{ m s}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

This calculation earns you the first mark (A1) in the markscheme.

steps.title: Set up displacement equation
steps.content:
Using the **symmetry of projectile motion**, we can calculate the total time of flight by finding when the rock returns to its original height. This means the total vertical displacement ($s$) is zero. 

We use the kinematic equation from the **Mechanics** section of your data booklet:

$$s = ut + \frac{1}{2}at^2$$

Substituting our values ($s = 0$, $u_y = 10.0$, and $a = -g = -9.81 \text{ m s}^{-2}$):

$$0 = (10.0)T + \frac{1}{2}(-9.81)T^2$$

steps.title: Solve for total time
steps.content:
Now, we solve for the total time $T$. We can factor out one $T$ (since $T=0$ is the start time, we want the other solution):

$$\begin{aligned} 4.905T^2 &= 10.0T \\ 4.905T &= 10.0 \\ T &= \frac{10.0}{4.905} \approx 2.0387 \text{ s} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Use the solver or simply calculate the fraction on the main screen.

desmos.instructions: Type the expression into a new cell.

calculator.description: Calculate the total time of flight T.

steps.title: Determine time to peak
steps.content:
In a symmetrical trajectory (where the launch and landing heights are the same), the time taken to reach the highest point is exactly half of the total flight time.

$$\begin{aligned} t_{peak} &= \frac{T}{2} \\ t_{peak} &= \frac{2.0387}{2} \\ t_{peak} &\approx 1.02 \text{ s} \end{aligned}$$

This final answer of $1.02 \text{ s}$ matches the second mark (A1) in the markscheme.

steps.title: Calculate vertical velocity component
steps.content:
To analyze the motion at the highest point, we first need to find the initial vertical velocity component ($u_y$). This is found using trigonometry based on the projection angle and speed.

$$u_y = u \sin(\theta)$$

$$u_y = 20.0 \times \sin(30^\circ) = 10.0 \text{ m s}^{-1}$$

This marks the first step in solving any projectile motion problem—separating the horizontal and vertical components.

steps.title: Find the maximum height
steps.content:
Using the **Indirect Displacement Approach**, we first find the maximum vertical displacement ($s$). At the highest point, the vertical velocity ($v_y$) is $0$. We use the velocity-displacement formula from the data booklet:

$$v^2 = u^2 + 2as$$

Substituting our values ($u = 10.0$, $v = 0$, and $a = -9.81 \text{ m s}^{-2}$ for gravity):

$$0^2 = 10.0^2 + 2(-9.81)s$$
$$19.62s = 100$$
$$s \approx 5.097 \text{ m}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the maximum height reached by the rock.

steps.title: Calculate time using average velocity
steps.content:
Now, we use the average velocity formula to solve for time ($t$). The average vertical velocity is the mean of the initial and final vertical velocities:

$$\text{Average velocity } (v_{avg}) = \frac{u + v}{2} = \frac{10.0 + 0}{2} = 5.0 \text{ m s}^{-1}$$

According to the data booklet, displacement is average velocity multiplied by time:

$$s = \frac{u+v}{2}t$$

Rearranging for $t$:

$$t = \frac{s}{v_{avg}} = \frac{5.097}{5.0} \approx 1.02 \text{ s}$$

calculator.description: Divide the calculated displacement by the average velocity to find the time.

steps.title: Final answer and markscheme check
steps.content:
The time taken to reach the highest point is **$1.02 \text{ s}$**. 

In the IB markscheme, you earn **1 mark** for correctly identifying the vertical component ($10.0 \text{ m s}^{-1}$) and **1 mark** for the final calculated time. Both $9.8$ or $9.81$ for $g$ are usually acceptable, but $9.81$ provides the specific precision seen in the official markscheme.

parts.content: Determine the maximum height reached above the point of launch.
parts.markscheme:
- Use $v_y = 10.0 \text{ m s}^{-1}$ in kinematic equation <marks>M1</marks>

- Maximum height: $h = \frac{v_y^2}{2g} = \frac{10.0^2}{2 \times 9.81} = 5.10 \text{ m}$ <marks>A1</marks>

parts.content: Calculate the total horizontal distance travelled by the rock before it hits the ground.
parts.markscheme:
- Horizontal component: $v_x = 20.0 \cos(30^{\circ}) = 17.3 \text{ m s}^{-1}$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Total flight time: $2 \times 1.02 = 2.04 \text{ s}$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Horizontal distance: $v_x \times \text{total time} = 17.3 \times 2.04 = 35.3 \text{ m}$ <marks>A1</marks>

<note>Award maximum 2 marks</note>

parts.content: Explain why the vertical and horizontal motions of the rock can be treated independently.
parts.markscheme:
- The horizontal and vertical components are affected by different factors: gravity only acts vertically <marks>B1</marks>

- They are resolved independently since they are perpendicular and do not influence each other <marks>B1</marks>

steps.title: Identify the acting forces
steps.content:
To understand why we can separate the motions, we first look at the forces involved. This concept is covered in **Topic A.2: Forces and momentum**. 

Since the question states that air resistance is negligible, the only force acting on the rock once it is in flight is gravity (its weight). According to Newton's Second Law, acceleration only occurs in the direction of the resultant force:

$$F = ma$$

steps.title: Determine the force direction
steps.content:
Gravity is a vector that acts exclusively in the downward **vertical** direction. 

Because there are no horizontal forces acting on the rock (as we are ignoring air resistance), there is no horizontal acceleration. This means the factors affecting the vertical motion (gravity) are completely different from those affecting the horizontal motion (which has a constant velocity).

steps.title: Apply vector independence principles
steps.content:
In vector analysis, components that are **perpendicular** ($90^\circ$ to each other) do not influence one another. 

Because the vertical force of gravity has no component in the horizontal direction ($F \cos(90^\circ) = 0$), it cannot change the rock's horizontal velocity. Since they do not interact or "leak" into each other, the two dimensions of motion are mathematically independent.

steps.title: Final Explanation
steps.content:
To earn the marks on an IB exam, you should clearly state:
1. Gravity is the only force acting, and it acts only in the **vertical direction**.
2. The horizontal and vertical components are **perpendicular**, meaning they do not influence each other and can be resolved independently.

questions.specification: A cyclist travels along a straight road. She accelerates uniformly from rest to a speed of $6.0\text{ m s}^{-1}$ in $4.0\text{ s}$.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic A - Space, time and motion > A.1 Kinematics > A.1.2 Equations of motion
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.1.2 Equations of motion
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Uses constant-acceleration kinematics equations to find acceleration and distance during uniform acceleration, plus total distance with a subsequent constant-speed interval.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic A - Space, time and motion > A.1 Kinematics > A.1.1 Describing motion quantitatively and qualitatively
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.1.1 Describing motion quantitatively and qualitatively
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Requires interpreting and sketching a speed–time graph and using its features/area to describe motion and distance travelled.

parts.content: Calculate the cyclist's acceleration.
parts.markscheme:
- Calculate the acceleration: $a = \frac{\Delta v}{\Delta t} = \frac{6.0}{4.0}$ <marks>M1</marks>
- $1.5\text{ m s}^{-2}$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

parts.content: Calculate the distance travelled by the cyclist during this acceleration.
parts.markscheme:
- Substitute into distance formula or use area of triangle: $s = \frac{1}{2}(u + v)t = \frac{1}{2}(0 + 6.0)(4.0)$ <marks>M1</marks>
- $12\text{ m}$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify given variables
steps.content:
To solve this problem using kinematics, we first need to identify our 'SUVAT' variables from the question. 

*   **Initial velocity ($u$):** The cyclist starts **from rest**, which means $u = 0 \text{ m s}^{-1}$.
*   **Final velocity ($v$):** The speed reached is **$6.0\text{ m s}^{-1}$**.
*   **Time ($t$):** The acceleration occurs over **$4.0\text{ s}$**.
*   **Displacement ($s$):** This is what we are trying to find.

steps.title: Select the kinematic equation
steps.content:
Since the cyclist **accelerates uniformly**, we can use the equations of motion found in **Topic A.1.2** of your syllabus. We need an equation that relates $u$, $v$, $t$, and $s$ without needing the acceleration ($a$). 

The most direct formula from the data booklet is:

$$s = \frac{u+v}{2}t$$

steps.title: Calculate the distance
steps.content:
Now, substitute the known values into the equation to find the distance:

$$\begin{aligned} s &= \frac{0 + 6.0}{2} \times 4.0 \\ s &= 3.0 \times 4.0 \\ s &= 12 \text{ m} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter the calculation into the run-matrix or main calculation mode.

ti84.instructions: Enter the expression into the main screen.

desmos.instructions: Type the expression directly into an empty cell.

calculator.description: Calculate the displacement using the average velocity formula.

steps.title: Final result
steps.content:
The distance travelled by the cyclist during the acceleration is $12 \text{ m}$. 

In an IB exam, you earn one mark for the correct substitution into the formula and one mark for the final answer with correct units.

steps.title: Identify the given information
steps.content:
First, let's list the values given in the problem. Since the cyclist starts "from rest", we know the initial velocity is zero. These concepts are found in **Topic A.1 Kinematics**.

*   Initial velocity ($u$) = $0\text{ m s}^{-1}$
*   Final velocity ($v$) = $6.0\text{ m s}^{-1}$
*   Time ($t$) = $4.0\text{ s}$

steps.title: Calculate the constant acceleration
steps.content:
In the Two-Step Acceleration Method, we first find the acceleration ($a$). We can rearrange the kinematic equation $$v = u + at$$ from the data booklet to solve for $a$:

$$\begin{aligned} a &= \frac{v - u}{t} \\ a &= \frac{6.0 - 0}{4.0} \\ a &= 1.5\text{ m s}^{-2} \end{aligned}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the acceleration by dividing the change in velocity by time.

steps.title: Calculate the distance travelled
steps.content:
Now that we have the acceleration, we can substitute it into the displacement formula $$s = ut + \frac{1}{2}at^2$$ to find the distance ($s$):

$$\begin{aligned} s &= (0)(4.0) + \frac{1}{2}(1.5)(4.0)^2 \\ s &= 0 + 0.5 \times 1.5 \times 16 \\ s &= 12\text{ m} \end{aligned}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the final distance using the acceleration found in the previous step.

steps.title: State final answer
steps.content:
The distance travelled by the cyclist during the acceleration phase is $12\text{ m}$. 

In an IB exam, you would earn one mark for the correct substitution into a valid kinematic equation (even if done in two steps) and one mark for the correct final answer with units.

steps.title: Visualize the motion
steps.content:
In physics, the area under a **velocity-time ($v-t$) graph** represents the total distance (or displacement) travelled. 

Because the cyclist accelerates uniformly from rest, the $v-t$ graph is a straight line starting at $(0, 0)$ and ending at $6.0\text{ m s}^{-1}$ after $4.0\text{ s}$. This shape forms a right-angled triangle.

steps.title: Identify triangle dimensions
steps.content:
From the question, we can identify the base and the height of our triangle:

*   **Base (time):** $4.0\text{ s}$
*   **Height (velocity):** $6.0\text{ m s}^{-1}$

steps.title: Calculate the area
steps.content:
To find the distance ($s$), we use the formula for the area of a triangle:

$$\text{Area} = \frac{1}{2} \times \text{base} \times \text{height}$$

Substituting our values:

$$s = \frac{1}{2} \times 4.0 \times 6.0$$

This calculation is equivalent to the kinematic equation found in your data booklet under **Mechanics**:

$$s = \frac{u+v}{2}t$$

ti84.instructions: Enter 0.5 * 4.0 * 6.0 on the home screen and press ENTER.

calculator.description: Calculate the total distance using the area of the triangle.

steps.title: State final answer
steps.content:
The total distance travelled is:

$$s = 12\text{ m}$$

In the IB markscheme, you would earn **1 mark** for showing the correct substitution into the formula and **1 mark** for the correct final answer with units.

parts.content: The cyclist continues at a constant speed of $6.0\text{ m s}^{-1}$ for $10.0\text{ s}$. Calculate the total distance travelled from the start to the end of this constant speed period.
parts.markscheme:
- Calculate distance at constant speed: $s = vt = 6.0 \times 10.0 = 60\text{ m}$ seen <marks>M1</marks>
- Add distances to find total: $12 + 60 = 72\text{ m}$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Calculate distance during acceleration
steps.content:
First, we calculate the distance covered during the initial acceleration phase. The cyclist starts from rest ($u = 0\text{ m s}^{-1}$) and reaches $v = 6.0\text{ m s}^{-1}$ in $t = 4.0\text{ s}$. 

Using the kinematic equation from the data booklet:

$$s = \frac{u+v}{2}t$$

Substituting the values:

$$s_1 = \frac{0 + 6.0}{2} \times 4.0 = 12\text{ m}$$

highlights.text: accelerates uniformly from rest to a speed of 6.0 m s−1 in 4.0 s

steps.title: Calculate distance at constant speed
steps.content:
Next, we calculate the distance traveled during the second phase, where the cyclist moves at a constant speed of $6.0\text{ m s}^{-1}$ for $10.0\text{ s}$. 

For constant speed, we use:

$$s = vt$$

$$s_2 = 6.0 \times 10.0 = 60\text{ m}$$

gdc.instructions: Multiply the velocity by the time.

ti84.instructions: Multiply the velocity by the time.

desmos.instructions: Multiply the velocity by the time.

calculator.description: Calculate the distance during the constant speed phase.

highlights.text: constant speed of 6.0 m s−1 for 10.0 s

steps.title: Find the total distance
steps.content:
Finally, we sum the distances from both phases to find the total distance traveled from the start:

$$\begin{aligned} s_{total} &= s_1 + s_2 \\ s_{total} &= 12 + 60 \\ s_{total} &= 72\text{ m} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Visualize the motion graph
steps.content:
To find the total distance using the **Geometric Area Method**, we need to calculate the area under the velocity-time ($v-t$) graph. 

This motion consists of two distinct shapes:
1.  **A triangle**: Representing the uniform acceleration from rest to $6.0\text{ m s}^{-1}$ over $4.0\text{ s}$.
2.  **A rectangle**: Representing the constant speed of $6.0\text{ m s}^{-1}$ for the next $10.0\text{ s}$.

The total distance is simply the sum of these two areas.

steps.title: Calculate the acceleration area
steps.content:
First, we calculate the area of the triangle formed during the first $4.0\text{ s}$. 

The area of a triangle is given by:
$$\text{Area}_1 = \frac{1}{2} \times \text{base} \times \text{height}$$

Substituting our values:
$$\text{Area}_1 = \frac{1}{2} \times 4.0\text{ s} \times 6.0\text{ m s}^{-1} = 12\text{ m}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the area of the triangle for the acceleration phase.

highlights.text: accelerates uniformly from rest to a speed of 6.0 m s−1 in 4.0 s

steps.title: Calculate the constant-speed area
steps.content:
Next, we calculate the area of the rectangle formed during the $10.0\text{ s}$ constant speed period. 

The area of a rectangle is:
$$\text{Area}_2 = \text{base} \times \text{height}$$

Substituting our values:
$$\text{Area}_2 = 10.0\text{ s} \times 6.0\text{ m s}^{-1} = 60\text{ m}$$

This calculation earns the first mark ($M1$).

calculator.description: Calculate the area of the rectangle for the constant speed phase.

highlights.text: continues at a constant speed of 6.0 m s−1 for 10.0 s

steps.title: Determine the total distance
steps.content:
Finally, we add the two areas together to find the total distance traveled from the start:

$$\text{Total Distance} = \text{Area}_1 + \text{Area}_2$$
$$\text{Total Distance} = 12\text{ m} + 60\text{ m} = 72\text{ m}$$

This final sum earns the second mark ($A1$).

calculator.description: Sum the two calculated areas.

parts.content: Sketch a speed–time graph of the motion from the start until the end of the constant speed period.
parts.markscheme:
- Sketch graph shape: Straight line from origin to $(4.0, 6.0)$ AND horizontal line from $(4.0, 6.0)$ to $(14.0, 6.0)$ <marks>A1</marks>
- Label axes with quantities (speed/v and time/t) AND key values ($4.0$, $14.0$, $6.0$) shown <marks>A1</marks>

questions.specification: A car accelerates uniformly from rest along a straight road. After $5.0\ \text{s}$, it reaches a speed of $15.0\ \text{m s}^{-1}$. It then continues at this speed for $10.0\ \text{s}$ before decelerating uniformly to a stop in $4.0\ \text{s}$.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic A - Space, time and motion > A.1 Kinematics > A.1.2 Equations of motion
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.1.2 Equations of motion
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Uses constant-acceleration kinematics (accelerate from rest, uniform deceleration) and distance via area under velocity–time graph / kinematics equations.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic A - Space, time and motion > A.1 Kinematics > A.1.1 Describing motion quantitatively and qualitatively
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.1.1 Describing motion quantitatively and qualitatively
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Interpreting and sketching acceleration–time and velocity–time graphs, and explaining why v–t graphs give displacement (area under curve).

parts.content: Calculate the car's acceleration during the first $5.0\ \text{s}$.
parts.markscheme:
- Use definition of acceleration: $a = \frac{\Delta v}{\Delta t}$

- Substitute values: $a = \frac{15.0 - 0}{5.0}$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Calculate final answer: $3.0\ \text{m s}^{-2}$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify known kinematic variables
steps.content:
To solve this problem using the kinematic equation approach, we first need to identify the variables provided in the question for the first $5.0\text{ s}$ of motion. This is a typical **Topic A.1 (Kinematics)** problem.

*   The car starts **from rest**, which means the initial velocity $u = 0\text{ m s}^{-1}$.
*   The final speed reached is $v = 15.0\text{ m s}^{-1}$.
*   The time interval for this acceleration is $t = 5.0\text{ s}$.
*   We need to find the acceleration, $a$.

highlights.text: reaches a speed of 15.0 m s−1

steps.title: Select the appropriate formula
steps.content: We need an equation that relates $u$, $v$, $t$, and $a$. Looking at the **Mechanics** section of your IB Data Booklet, we find the SUVAT equations.

steps.title: Substitute and solve
steps.content:
Now, we substitute our known values into the equation to solve for $a$:

$$\begin{aligned} v &= u + at \\ 15.0 &= 0 + a(5.0) \\ a &= \frac{15.0}{5.0} \end{aligned}$$

This substitution step is crucial for earning the first mark (**M1**) in an IB exam.

ti84.instructions: Enter 15.0 divided by 5.0 on the main screen.

desmos.instructions: Type 15.0 / 5.0 into a new expression line.

calculator.description: Calculate the acceleration by dividing the change in velocity by time.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
Calculating the final value, we get:

$$a = 3.0\text{ m s}^{-2}$$

Notice that we provide the answer to two significant figures to match the precision given for the time ($5.0\text{ s}$). This earns the final mark (**A1**).

steps.title: Identify the gradient approach
steps.content:
To calculate acceleration using a velocity-time graph, we interpret the acceleration as the **gradient** (slope) of the line. 

In physics, the gradient of a velocity ($v$) against time ($t$) graph represents the rate of change of velocity, which is the definition of acceleration ($a$). This is a key concept in **Topic A.1: Kinematics**.

steps.title: Determine coordinates from rest
steps.content:
We need to find two points on the graph for the first 5.0 seconds of motion:

1.  **Starting Point:** The car starts **from rest**, which means at $t = 0\text{ s}$, the velocity $v = 0\text{ m s}^{-1}$. This gives us the coordinate $(0, 0)$.
2.  **Ending Point:** After **5.0 s**, it reaches a speed of **15.0 m s⁻¹**. This gives us the coordinate $(5.0, 15.0)$.

steps.title: Calculate the gradient
steps.content:
The formula for the gradient of a straight line is:

$$\text{gradient} = \frac{\text{change in } y}{\text{change in } x} = \frac{v_2 - v_1}{t_2 - t_1}$$

Substituting our values from the first 5.0 seconds:

$$a = \frac{15.0 - 0}{5.0 - 0}$$

This calculation is equivalent to the kinematic equation found in the data booklet:

$$v = u + at$$

rearranged to solve for $a$.

gdc.instructions: Divide the change in velocity by the change in time.

ti84.instructions: Enter the division to find the gradient.

calculator.description: Calculate the final acceleration value.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
Performing the calculation:

$$a = 3.0\text{ m s}^{-2}$$

According to the markscheme, you receive one mark (**M1**) for showing the substitution into the acceleration definition and one mark (**A1**) for the correct final answer with units.

parts.content: Determine the total distance travelled during the entire motion.
parts.markscheme:
- Attempt to calculate distance for each stage or area under velocity-time graph <marks>M1</marks>

- Calculate distance for first phase: $s_1 = \frac{1}{2}(15.0)(5.0) = 37.5\ \text{m}$
- Calculate distance for constant speed phase: $s_2 = 15.0 \cdot 10.0 = 150.0\ \text{m}$
- Calculate distance for deceleration phase: $s_3 = \frac{1}{2}(15.0)(4.0) = 30.0\ \text{m}$ (or via average speed $7.5\ \text{m s}^{-1}$) <marks>A1</marks>

- Calculate total distance: $37.5 + 150.0 + 30.0 = 217.5\ \text{m}$ <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

steps.title: Analyze the motion segments
steps.content:
To find the total distance, we will treat the motion as three distinct segments: acceleration, constant speed, and deceleration. This approach uses concepts from **Topic A.1: Kinematics**. We can find the distance ($s$) for each segment using the kinematic equation for average velocity from your data booklet:

$$s = \frac{u+v}{2}t$$

steps.title: Calculate distance for Phase 1
steps.content:
In the first phase, the car accelerates from rest ($u = 0$) to a final speed of $15.0\text{ m s}^{-1}$ over $5.0\text{ s}$.

$$\begin{aligned} s_1 &= \frac{0 + 15.0}{2} \times 5.0 \\ s_1 &= 7.5 \times 5.0 \\ s_1 &= 37.5\text{ m} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: In the main calculation menu, enter (0 + 15) / 2 * 5.

ti84.instructions: Enter (0 + 15) / 2 * 5 on the home screen and press ENTER.

calculator.description: Calculate the distance for the first phase.

steps.title: Calculate distance for Phase 2
steps.content:
Next, the car travels at a constant speed of $15.0\text{ m s}^{-1}$ for $10.0\text{ s}$. For constant speed ($u = v = 15.0$):

$$\begin{aligned} s_2 &= 15.0 \times 10.0 \\ s_2 &= 150.0\text{ m} \end{aligned}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the distance for the constant speed phase.

highlights.text: continues at this speed for 10.0 s

steps.title: Calculate distance for Phase 3
steps.content:
In the final phase, the car decelerates from $15.0\text{ m s}^{-1}$ back to a stop ($v = 0$) in $4.0\text{ s}$.

$$\begin{aligned} s_3 &= \frac{15.0 + 0}{2} \times 4.0 \\ s_3 &= 7.5 \times 4.0 \\ s_3 &= 30.0\text{ m} \end{aligned}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the distance for the deceleration phase.

highlights.text: decelerating uniformly to a stop in 4.0 s

steps.title: Find the total distance
steps.content:
Finally, we sum the distances from each segment to find the total distance travelled:

$$\begin{aligned} s_{\text{total}} &= s_1 + s_2 + s_3 \\ s_{\text{total}} &= 37.5 + 150.0 + 30.0 \\ s_{\text{total}} &= 217.5\text{ m} \end{aligned}$$

calculator.description: Sum the distances from all phases.

steps.title: Visualize the velocity-time graph
steps.content:
To find the total distance, we can calculate the area under a velocity-time ($v$-$t$) graph. The motion consists of three distinct stages:

1.  **Uniform acceleration**: A straight line from $v = 0$ to $v = 15.0\text{ m s}^{-1}$ over $5.0\text{ s}$ (forming a triangle).
2.  **Constant speed**: A horizontal line at $v = 15.0\text{ m s}^{-1}$ for $10.0\text{ s}$ (forming a rectangle).
3.  **Uniform deceleration**: A straight line from $v = 15.0\text{ m s}^{-1}$ to $v = 0$ over $4.0\text{ s}$ (forming another triangle).

Calculating the area of these geometric shapes gives us the distance for each stage.

highlights.text: continues at this speed for 10.0 s
decelerating uniformly to a stop in 4.0 s

steps.title: Calculate acceleration phase distance
steps.content:
The first stage is a triangle with a base of $5.0\text{ s}$ and a height of $15.0\text{ m s}^{-1}$. 

Using the area of a triangle formula $\text{Area} = \frac{1}{2} \times \text{base} \times \text{height}$:

$$s_1 = \frac{1}{2} \times 5.0\text{ s} \times 15.0\text{ m s}^{-1} = 37.5\text{ m}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the area of the first triangle.

highlights.text: After 5.0 s, it reaches a speed of 15.0 m s−1

steps.title: Calculate constant speed distance
steps.content:
The second stage is a rectangle with a width of $10.0\text{ s}$ and a height of $15.0\text{ m s}^{-1}$.

Using the area of a rectangle formula $\text{Area} = \text{width} \times \text{height}$:

$$s_2 = 10.0\text{ s} \times 15.0\text{ m s}^{-1} = 150.0\text{ m}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the area of the rectangle.

highlights.text: continues at this speed for 10.0 s

steps.title: Calculate deceleration phase distance
steps.content:
The final stage is a triangle with a base of $4.0\text{ s}$ and a height of $15.0\text{ m s}^{-1}$.

Using the area of a triangle formula:

$$s_3 = \frac{1}{2} \times 4.0\text{ s} \times 15.0\text{ m s}^{-1} = 30.0\text{ m}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the area of the second triangle.

highlights.text: decelerating uniformly to a stop in 4.0 s

steps.title: Determine total distance
steps.content:
To find the total distance, we sum the distances from all three phases:

$$\text{Total Distance} = s_1 + s_2 + s_3$$
$$\text{Total Distance} = 37.5 + 150.0 + 30.0 = 217.5\text{ m}$$

Alternatively, you could treat the entire graph as one large trapezoid with parallel sides of length $10.0$ and $19.0$ ($5+10+4$), and a height of $15.0$, which leads to the same result.

calculator.description: Sum the three distances.

- Explain that this allows distances for each motion segment to be calculated visually or geometrically <marks>A1</marks>

labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Requires interpreting and sketching a velocity–time graph and using its area to verify total distance and compute average velocity.

parts.content: Calculate the final velocity of the car at the end of the 6-second acceleration period.
parts.markscheme:
- Use of kinematic equation $v = u + at$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Correct substitution and calculation: $v = 0 + 3 \times 6 = 18 \text{ m/s}$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

<examTip>Remember to include units in your final answer.</examTip>

steps.title: Identify the given variables
steps.content:
To solve this problem using the Kinematic (SUVAT) approach, we first need to list the information given for the first 6 seconds of the car's motion:

*   **Initial velocity ($u$):** The car "starts from rest", so $u = 0 \text{ m/s}$.
*   **Acceleration ($a$):** Given as $3 \text{ m/s}^2$.
*   **Time ($t$):** The period of acceleration is $6 \text{ s}$.
*   **Final velocity ($v$):** This is what we need to calculate.

steps.title: Select the appropriate equation
steps.content:
From the **Mechanics** section of your IB Data Booklet, we can find the kinematic equation that relates final velocity, initial velocity, acceleration, and time:

$$v = u + at$$

This is the most direct way to find $v$ when you know $u$, $a$, and $t$. In an IB exam, simply stating or showing the use of this formula earns you the first mark (**M1**).

steps.title: Substitute and solve
steps.content:
Now, substitute the known values into the equation:

$$\begin{aligned} v &= u + at \\ v &= 0 + (3 \times 6) \\ v &= 18 \text{ m/s} \end{aligned}$$

Correct substitution and the final result earn the second mark (**A1**). Remember to always include units ($ \text{m/s} $) in your final answer!

ti84.instructions: Type the product of acceleration and time

calculator.description: Calculate the final velocity

steps.title: Identify the motion variables
steps.content:
To find the final velocity, let's first list what we know from the problem description for the first 6 seconds:

*   Initial velocity ($u$): $0\text{ m/s}$ (because the car "starts from rest")
*   Acceleration ($a$): $3\text{ m/s}^2$
*   Time interval ($t$): $6\text{ s}$

steps.title: Link acceleration to graph gradient
steps.content:
In a velocity-time graph, the **acceleration is the gradient** (slope) of the line. Since the car starts from rest at $t = 0$, the graph starts at the origin $(0,0)$. 

For uniform acceleration, the relationship between final velocity ($v$), acceleration ($a$), and time ($t$) is represented by the linear equation from the **Mechanics** section of your data booklet:

$$v = u + at$$

steps.title: Calculate the final velocity
steps.content:
Since the car starts from the origin ($u = 0$), the formula simplifies to $v = at$. This is essentially calculating the vertical change on our velocity-time graph after 6 seconds.

$$\begin{aligned} v &= 0 + (3 \times 6) \\ v &= 18 \text{ m/s} \end{aligned}$$

This calculation earns you the second mark for the correct substitution and final value.

desmos.instructions: Type 3 * 6 in a new expression line.

calculator.description: Calculate the final velocity by multiplying acceleration and time.

steps.title: Final Answer
steps.content: The final velocity of the car at the end of the 6-second period is **$18 \text{ m/s}$**.

parts.content: Determine the total distance travelled by the car during the acceleration phase.
parts.markscheme:
- Use of kinematic equation $s = ut + \frac{1}{2}at^2$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Correct substitution and calculation: $s = 0 \times 6 + \frac{1}{2} \times 3 \times 6^2 = 54 \text{ m}$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify known variables
steps.content:
To find the distance travelled during the acceleration phase, we first list the known values from the question:
- Initial velocity ($u$): $0 \text{ m/s}$ (because the car starts from rest)
- Acceleration ($a$): $3 \text{ m/s}^2$
- Time interval ($t$): $6 \text{ s}$

steps.title: Select the SUVAT equation
steps.content:
We need to find the displacement ($s$). In **Topic A.1 Kinematics**, we learn about the equations of motion (SUVAT). Looking at our data booklet in the Mechanics section, we find the equation that links $s, u, a,$ and $t$:

$$s = ut + \frac{1}{2}at^2$$

steps.title: Substitute and solve
steps.content:
Now, we substitute the known values into the equation:

$$\begin{aligned} s &= (0 \times 6) + \frac{1}{2} \times 3 \times 6^2 \\ s &= 0 + \frac{1}{2} \times 3 \times 36 \\ s &= 1.5 \times 36 \\ s &= 54 \text{ m} \end{aligned}$$

Identifying the correct equation and performing the substitution earns you the first mark (**M1**).

gdc.instructions: Enter the expression into the calculator's main computation mode.

ti84.instructions: Enter the expression directly on the home screen.

desmos.instructions: Type the expression into an empty cell.

calculator.description: Calculate the total distance using the SUVAT equation.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content: The total distance travelled by the car during the acceleration phase is $54 \text{ m}$. Providing the correct final value with units earns the second mark (**A1**).

steps.title: Identify known variables
steps.content:
To find the distance travelled during the acceleration phase, we first list the information given in the question for this specific interval:

- Initial velocity ($u$) = $0 \text{ m/s}$ (because the car starts from rest)
- Acceleration ($a$) = $3 \text{ m/s}^2$
- Time ($t$) = $6 \text{ s}$

This problem involves constant acceleration, so we can use the kinematic equations found in **Topic A.1: Kinematics**.

steps.title: Calculate final velocity
steps.content:
First, we calculate the final velocity ($v$) reached at the end of the $6 \text{ s}$ acceleration phase using the formula for velocity in the data booklet:

$$\begin{aligned} v &= u + at \\ v &= 0 + (3 \times 6) \\ v &= 18 \text{ m/s} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter the sum of the initial velocity and the product of acceleration and time.

ti84.instructions: Type the expression and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Type the calculation directly into the input bar.

calculator.description: Calculate the final velocity.

steps.title: Calculate distance travelled
steps.content:
Now that we have the final velocity, we can find the distance ($s$) using the average velocity formula. This method considers that with constant acceleration, the average speed is simply the midpoint between the starting and ending speeds.

$$\begin{aligned} s &= \frac{u+v}{2}t \\ s &= \frac{0 + 18}{2} \times 6 \\ s &= 9 \times 6 \\ s &= 54 \text{ m} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Multiply the average velocity by the time.

desmos.instructions: Enter the average velocity formula with the known values.

calculator.description: Calculate the total distance using average velocity.

steps.title: State final answer
steps.content:
The total distance travelled by the car during the acceleration phase is $54 \text{ m}$.

In an IB exam, you would earn one mark for showing a correct kinematic formula and one mark for the correct final answer with units.

steps.title: Identify the goal
steps.content:
To find the distance travelled using the **Velocity-Time Graph Area** approach, we first need to determine the maximum velocity reached during the acceleration phase. 

This problem falls under **Topic A.1 Kinematics**. Recall that for a velocity-time graph, the area under the curve represents the total displacement (or distance in a straight line).

highlights.text: accelerates uniformly at 3  m/s^2 for 6 seconds

steps.title: Calculate final velocity
steps.content:
First, we find the velocity at $t = 6$ seconds using the kinematic equation from the data booklet:

$$v = u + at$$

Given:
- Initial velocity $u = 0 \text{ m/s}$
- Acceleration $a = 3 \text{ m/s}^2$
- Time $t = 6 \text{ s}$

$$\begin{aligned} v &= 0 + (3 \times 6) \\ v &= 18 \text{ m/s} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Calculate the graph area
steps.content:
On a velocity-time graph, uniform acceleration from rest is represented by a straight line starting at $(0,0)$ and ending at $(6, 18)$. This forms a **right-angled triangle** with:
- Base ($t$) $= 6 \text{ s}$
- Height ($v$) $= 18 \text{ m/s}$

The distance $s$ is equal to the area of this triangle:

$$\text{Area} = \frac{1}{2} \times \text{base} \times \text{height}$$

$$\begin{aligned} s &= \frac{1}{2} \times 6 \times 18 \\ s &= 54 \text{ m} \end{aligned}$$

desmos.instructions: Type the expression directly into the input bar.

calculator.description: Calculate the distance using the triangle area formula.

steps.title: Summary of marks
steps.content:
In an IB exam, you would earn marks as follows:
- **M1**: For correctly identifying the method, such as showing the calculation for $v$ or the triangle area formula.
- **A1**: For the correct final answer of $54 \text{ m}$ with appropriate units.

Final Distance: $54 \text{ m}$

parts.content: Calculate the total distance covered while the car is moving at a constant velocity.
parts.markscheme:
- Use of correct formula $s = vt$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Correct substitution and calculation: $s = 18 \times 10 = 180 \text{ m}$ <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Analyze the motion phases
steps.content: To find the distance covered during the constant velocity phase, we first need to determine exactly what that velocity is. The car reaches this velocity at the end of its initial acceleration phase. This problem uses concepts from **Topic A.1 Kinematics**.

steps.title: Find the constant velocity
steps.content:
The car starts from rest ($u = 0$) and accelerates at $3 \text{ m/s}^2$ for $6 \text{ s}$. We use the kinematic equation from the **Mechanics** section of your data booklet to find the final velocity ($v$) of this first phase:

$$v = u + at$$

Substituting our values:
$$\begin{aligned} v &= 0 + (3 \times 6) \\ v &= 18 \text{ m/s} \end{aligned}$$

This $18 \text{ m/s}$ is the constant velocity the car maintains for the next phase.

gdc.instructions: Enter the multiplication on the main calculation screen.

ti84.instructions: Enter the multiplication on the home screen.

desmos.instructions: Type the expression into the input bar.

calculator.description: Calculate the velocity at the end of the acceleration phase.

steps.title: Calculate the distance covered
steps.content:
Now that we know the velocity is $18 \text{ m/s}$, we can calculate the distance ($s$) covered during the $10 \text{ s}$ constant velocity phase. For constant velocity, the formula is:

$$s = vt$$

Substituting the values:
$$\begin{aligned} s &= 18 \times 10 \\ s &= 180 \text{ m} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Multiply the velocity by the time.

ti84.instructions: Multiply the velocity by the time.

steps.content:
The total distance covered while the car is moving at a constant velocity is $180 \text{ m}$.

In an IB exam, you earn **M1** for showing the correct formula ($s = vt$) or the substitution, and **A1** for the final correct answer with units.

steps.title: Find the constant velocity
steps.content:
To calculate the distance during the constant velocity phase, we first need to find the velocity the car reached. The car starts from rest and accelerates for $6$ seconds. 

Using the kinematic equation from the **Mechanics** section of your data booklet:

$$v = u + at$$

Substituting the values:
$$\begin{aligned} v &= 0 + (3 \times 6) \\ v &= 18 \text{ m/s} \end{aligned}$$

highlights.text: accelerates uniformly at $3 \text{ m/s}^2$ for 6 seconds

steps.title: Visualize the graph area
steps.content: In a velocity-time graph, the **area under the curve** represents the displacement (distance). During the constant velocity phase, the graph is a horizontal line at $v = 18 \text{ m/s}$ for a duration of $10$ seconds. This forms a rectangle.

highlights.text: moves at a constant velocity for 10 seconds

steps.title: Calculate the rectangle area
steps.content:
The distance $s$ is the area of this rectangle, calculated as:

$$\text{Area} = \text{height} \times \text{width}$$
$$s = v \times t$$

Substituting our values:
$$\begin{aligned} s &= 18 \times 10 \\ s &= 180 \text{ m} \end{aligned}$$

This calculation earns you the final marks: one for the method of multiplying velocity by time ($s = vt$) and one for the correct result.

parts.content: Determine the time taken for the car to come to a complete stop during the deceleration phase.
parts.markscheme:
- Use of kinematic equation $v = u + at$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Substituting values: $0 = 18 - 2t$

- Solving for $t$: $t = 9 \text{ s}$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Calculate the peak velocity
steps.content:
To find the time taken to stop, we first need to know how fast the car was going when it started to decelerate. The car starts from rest ($u = 0$) and accelerates at $3 \text{ m/s}^2$ for $6 \text{ s}$. 

Using the kinematic equation from the **Mechanics** section of your data booklet:

$$v = u + at$$

Substituting the values:

$$\begin{aligned} v &= 0 + (3 \times 6) \\ v &= 18 \text{ m/s} \end{aligned}$$

highlights.text: accelerates uniformly at $3 \text{ m/s}^2$ for 6 seconds

steps.title: Define deceleration phase variables
steps.content:
After the acceleration phase, the car moves at this constant velocity ($18 \text{ m/s}$) for 10 seconds. This means the initial velocity ($u$) for the final deceleration phase is $18 \text{ m/s}$. We are told it decelerates at $2 \text{ m/s}^2$ until it reaches a complete stop ($v = 0$).

Note: Because it is a deceleration, the acceleration $a$ is negative relative to the direction of motion: $a = -2 \text{ m/s}^2$.

highlights.text: decelerates uniformly at $2 \text{ m/s}^2$ until it comes to a complete stop

steps.title: Solve for time
steps.content:
Now we apply the same kinematic equation to the deceleration phase to find the time ($t$):

$$v = u + at$$

Substituting our known values:

$$\begin{aligned} 0 &= 18 + (-2)t \\ 2t &= 18 \\ t &= 9 \text{ s} \end{aligned}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the time by dividing the change in velocity by the deceleration rate.

highlights.text: Use of kinematic equation $v = u + at$

steps.title: Identify the peak velocity
steps.content:
To analyze the deceleration phase, we first need to know the car's velocity before it starts slowing down. On a velocity-time graph, the gradient represents acceleration. 

Starting from rest ($u = 0$), the car accelerates at $3 \text{ m/s}^2$ for $6 \text{ s}$. We can find the peak velocity ($v_{\text{max}}$) using the gradient definition of acceleration:

$$\text{gradient} = a = \frac{\Delta v}{\Delta t}$$

$$\begin{aligned} 3 &= \frac{v_{\text{max}} - 0}{6} \\ v_{\text{max}} &= 18 \text{ m/s} \end{aligned}$$

highlights.text: accelerates uniformly at $3 \text{ m/s}^2$ for 6 seconds

steps.title: Analyze the deceleration gradient
steps.content:
In the final phase, the car's velocity drops from $18 \text{ m/s}$ to $0 \text{ m/s}$. The gradient of this section of the graph corresponds to the deceleration. 

We are given the deceleration is $2 \text{ m/s}^2$, which means the gradient $a = -2 \text{ m/s}^2$. We can use the kinematic equation for a straight line on the $v-t$ graph from the **Topic A.1 Kinematics** section of the data booklet:

$$v = u + at$$

steps.title: Solve for time
steps.content:
Substitute our known values into the equation to find the time ($t$) taken for this phase:

$$\begin{aligned} 0 &= 18 + (-2)t \\ 2t &= 18 \\ t &= 9 \text{ s} \end{aligned}$$

The car takes $9$ seconds to come to a complete stop.

calculator.description: Calculate the time by dividing the change in velocity by the acceleration magnitude.

steps.title: Understand the velocity balance
steps.content:
To solve this using the **Acceleration-Time Proportionality** approach, we recognize that the car starts from rest and ends at a complete stop. 

This means the total change in velocity gained during the acceleration phase must be exactly equal to the total change in velocity lost during the deceleration phase. In mathematical terms, using the formula $$v = u + at$$ from the IB Data Booklet (where $u=0$ for the gain and $v=0$ for the loss), we can say:

$$\Delta v_{\text{gain}} = \Delta v_{\text{loss}}$$
$$a_1 t_1 = a_2 t_2$$

steps.title: Calculate the velocity gain
steps.content:
First, let's find the maximum velocity the car reached. The question states it accelerated at $$3 \text{ m/s}^2$$ for $$6 \text{ s}$$.

$$\Delta v_{\text{gain}} = 3 \text{ m/s}^2 \times 6 \text{ s} = 18 \text{ m/s}$$

This is the velocity the car maintains during the constant motion phase and the initial velocity ($u$) for the deceleration phase.

ti84.instructions: Enter the multiplication of acceleration and time.

calculator.description: Calculate the change in velocity during the acceleration phase.

highlights.text: accelerates uniformly at $3 \text{ m/s}^2$ for 6 seconds

steps.title: Solve for deceleration time
steps.content:
Now, we apply the proportionality principle. The velocity lost during deceleration must equal the $$18 \text{ m/s}$$ gained earlier. We know the deceleration rate is $$2 \text{ m/s}^2$$.

$$\begin{aligned} \Delta v_{\text{loss}} &= a_2 \times t_2 \\ 18 &= 2 \times t_2 \\ t_2 &= \frac{18}{2} \\ t_2 &= 9 \text{ s} \end{aligned}$$

The car takes 9 seconds to come to a stop.

calculator.description: Divide the velocity change by the deceleration rate to find the time.

steps.title: Final Result and Marks
steps.content:
The time taken for the car to come to a complete stop is **9 seconds**.

In an IB exam, you earn marks as follows:
- **1 mark (M1)**: For using the kinematic relationship (setting up the equation $$18 = 2t$$ or similar).
- **1 mark (A1)**: For the correct final answer of $$9 \text{ s}$$.

parts.content: Calculate the distance travelled by the car during the deceleration phase.
parts.markscheme:
- Use of kinematic equation $s = ut + \frac{1}{2}at^2$ (or equivalent) <marks>M1</marks>

- Correct substitution and calculation: $s = 18 \times 9 + \frac{1}{2} \times (-2) \times 9^2 = 162 - 81 = 81 \text{ m}$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify the initial velocity
steps.content:
To find the distance during the deceleration phase, we first need the velocity of the car right before it starts braking. The car starts from rest and accelerates for 6 seconds. This velocity is then held constant before the car begins to slow down.

From the data booklet, we use the kinematic equation:
$$v = u + at$$

Substituting the values for the first phase:
$$\begin{aligned} v &= 0 + (3)(6) \\ v &= 18 \text{ m/s} \end{aligned}$$

This velocity of $18 \text{ m/s}$ is the initial velocity ($u$) for our deceleration phase.

highlights.text: accelerates uniformly at $3 \text{ m/s}^2$ for 6 seconds

steps.title: Analyze the deceleration phase
steps.content:
Now we focus on the final phase where the car slows down. We know the following:
- Initial velocity ($u$) = $18 \text{ m/s}$
- Acceleration ($a$) = $-2 \text{ m/s}^2$ (since it is decelerating)
- Final velocity ($v$) = $0 \text{ m/s}$ (as it comes to a complete stop)

We need to find the displacement ($s$). This topic is covered in **Topic A.1: Kinematics**.

steps.title: Apply the kinematic equation
steps.content:
Since we have $u$, $v$, and $a$, we can use the following kinematic equation from the data booklet to solve for $s$ directly:

$$v^{2}=u^{2}+2as$$

Rearranging for $s$:
$$\begin{aligned} 0^2 &= (18)^2 + 2(-2)s \\ 0 &= 324 - 4s \\ 4s &= 324 \\ s &= \frac{324}{4} \end{aligned}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the final distance by dividing the squared velocity by four.

steps.title: State final result
steps.content:
After performing the calculation, we find:

$$s = 81 \text{ m}$$

In an IB exam, you earn **M1** for identifying the correct kinematic relationship and **A1** for the final answer with correct units.

steps.title: Determine the starting velocity
steps.content:
To find the distance during the deceleration phase, we first need the car's velocity just before it starts to slow down. The car accelerates from rest ($u=0$) at $3\text{ m/s}^2$ for $6\text{ s}$.

Using the kinematic equation from **Topic A.1.2**:
$$v = u + at$$

$$\begin{aligned} v &= 0 + (3 \times 6) \\ v &= 18\text{ m/s} \end{aligned}$$

Since the car maintains a constant velocity for the next 10 seconds, it enters the deceleration phase with an initial velocity ($u$) of $18\text{ m/s}$.

highlights.text: accelerates uniformly at $3 \text{ m/s}^2$ for 6 seconds

steps.title: Calculate time to stop
steps.content:
Next, we find how long it takes for the car to come to a complete stop ($v=0$) with a deceleration of $2\text{ m/s}^2$. Since it is slowing down, the acceleration $a = -2\text{ m/s}^2$.

Rearranging $$v = u + at$$ to solve for time ($t$):

$$\begin{aligned} 0 &= 18 + (-2)t \\ 2t &= 18 \\ t &= 9\text{ s} \end{aligned}$$

calculator.description: Calculate time by dividing the velocity change by the acceleration magnitude.

highlights.text: decelerates uniformly at $2 \text{ m/s}^2$ until it comes to a complete stop

steps.title: Calculate the deceleration distance
steps.content:
Now we can calculate the distance ($s$) travelled during these $9\text{ seconds}$ using the displacement formula from the data booklet:

$$s = ut + \frac{1}{2}at^2$$

Substituting our values ($u=18$, $t=9$, $a=-2$):

$$\begin{aligned} s &= (18 \times 9) + \frac{1}{2}(-2)(9)^2 \\ s &= 162 - 81 \\ s &= 81\text{ m} \end{aligned}$$

In an IB exam, you would earn one mark for choosing the correct kinematic equation and one mark for the final correct value.

calculator.description: Compute the final displacement calculation.

highlights.text: Use of kinematic equation $s = ut + \frac{1}{2}at^2$ (or equivalent)

steps.title: Find the maximum velocity
steps.content:
To analyze the motion using a velocity-time ($v$-$t$) graph, we first need to determine the maximum velocity ($v_{max}$) the car reaches. The car starts from rest and accelerates at $3 \text{ m/s}^2$ for 6 seconds.

Using the kinematic equation from the **Mechanics** section of your data booklet:

$$v = u + at$$

Substituting the values:
$$\begin{aligned} v_{max} &= 0 + (3 \times 6) \\ v_{max} &= 18 \text{ m/s} \end{aligned}$$

highlights.text: accelerates uniformly at 3 m/s^2 for 6 seconds

steps.title: Determine deceleration phase duration
steps.content:
Next, we find how long the deceleration phase lasts. The car starts this phase at $18 \text{ m/s}$ and decelerates at $2 \text{ m/s}^2$ until it stops ($v = 0$).

Using $$v = u + at$$ again:

$$\begin{aligned} 0 &= 18 + (-2) \times t \\ 2t &= 18 \\ t &= 9 \text{ s} \end{aligned}$$

The deceleration phase lasts for 9 seconds.

highlights.text: decelerates uniformly at 2 m/s^2 until it comes to a complete stop

steps.title: Analyze the velocity-time graph
steps.content:
In a $v$-$t$ graph, the area under the line represents the distance travelled. For the deceleration phase, the graph shows a straight line decreasing from $18 \text{ m/s}$ to $0 \text{ m/s}$ over a period of $9$ seconds.

This segment forms a **right-angled triangle** with:
- Base (time) $= 9 \text{ s}$
- Height (velocity) $= 18 \text{ m/s}$

steps.title: Calculate the area (distance)
steps.content:
The distance travelled during this phase is the area of this triangle:

$$\text{Distance} = \frac{1}{2} \times \text{base} \times \text{height}$$

$$\begin{aligned} s &= \frac{1}{2} \times 9 \times 18 \\ s &= 9 \times 9 \\ s &= 81 \text{ m} \end{aligned}$$

This matches the result we would get using the kinematic equations directly, as shown in the markscheme.

gdc.instructions: Calculate 0.5 * 9 * 18 in the main calculation menu.

ti84.instructions: Enter 0.5 * 9 * 18 on the home screen and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Type the expression into a new cell.

parts.content: Sketch a velocity-time graph for the car’s motion and use it to verify the total distance travelled.
parts.markscheme:
- Correct axes with labels ($t$/s, $v$/m/s) and general shape (trapezoid) <marks>A1</marks>

- Correct key points shown: linear increase to $(6, 18)$, constant to $(16, 18)$, linear decrease to $(25, 0)$ <marks>A1</marks>

![Velocity-time graph](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/plots/a1d8500a-fba0-4689-8df1-604ebbfc1b2e.png)

- Method to calculate area (sum of areas of triangle, rectangle, triangle) <marks>M1</marks>
  $\text{Area} = \frac{1}{2} \times 6 \times 18 + 18 \times 10 + \frac{1}{2} \times 9 \times 18$

- Correct verification of total distance: $54 + 180 + 81 = 315 \text{ m}$ <marks>A1</marks>

4 marks total

<examTip>Draw the graph carefully with a ruler and clearly show your area calculations.</examTip>

steps.title: Calculate maximum velocity
steps.content:
To plot the graph, we first find the velocity reached after the acceleration phase. Using the kinematics equation from the data booklet:

$$v = u + at$$

Given the car starts from rest ($u=0$), has an acceleration of $3 \text{ m/s}^2$, and moves for 6 seconds:

$$v = 0 + (3 \times 6) = 18 \text{ m/s}$$

This $18 \text{ m/s}$ represents the peak velocity and the height of our graph.

highlights.text: accelerates uniformly at $3 \text{ m/s}^2$ for 6 seconds

steps.title: Calculate deceleration time
steps.content:
Next, we find how long it takes to stop from $18 \text{ m/s}$ at a deceleration rate of $2 \text{ m/s}^2$. Again, using the kinematics equation:

$$v = u + at$$

Setting final velocity $v=0$ and acceleration $a=-2 \text{ m/s}^2$:

$$\begin{aligned} 0 &= 18 + (-2)t_{3} \\ 2t_{3} &= 18 \\ t_{3} &= 9 \text{ s} \end{aligned}$$

The total time on the x-axis will be $6 + 10 + 9 = 25 \text{ seconds}$.

gdc.instructions: Divide the velocity by the deceleration magnitude.

ti84.instructions: Divide the velocity by the deceleration magnitude.

desmos.instructions: Divide the velocity by the deceleration magnitude.

calculator.description: Calculate deceleration time by dividing velocity by the magnitude of deceleration.

highlights.text: decelerates uniformly at $2 \text{ m/s}^2$ until it comes to a complete stop

steps.title: Decompose the graph area
steps.content:
The velocity-time graph forms a trapezoid. Using geometric decomposition, we split this area into three parts:
1. A **triangle** for the acceleration phase ($t=0$ to $t=6$)
2. A **rectangle** for the constant velocity phase ($t=6$ to $t=16$)
3. A **triangle** for the deceleration phase ($t=16$ to $t=25$)

The distance traveled is equal to the total area under this graph.

steps.title: Calculate phase areas
steps.content:
Now we calculate the area of each section using the formulas for triangles and rectangles:

- **Triangle 1 (Acceleration):** 
$$\text{Area}_1 = \frac{1}{2} \times \text{base} \times \text{height} = \frac{1}{2} \times 6 \times 18 = 54 \text{ m}$$

- **Rectangle (Constant Velocity):** 
$$\text{Area}_2 = \text{base} \times \text{height} = 10 \times 18 = 180 \text{ m}$$

- **Triangle 2 (Deceleration):** 
$$\text{Area}_3 = \frac{1}{2} \times \text{base} \times \text{height} = \frac{1}{2} \times 9 \times 18 = 81 \text{ m}$$

ti84.instructions: Calculate the area of the two triangles and one rectangle.

desmos.instructions: Calculate each area to find the total distance later.

steps.title: Verify total distance
steps.content:
Summing the areas of the decomposed shapes gives us the total distance traveled:

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Total distance} &= 54 + 180 + 81 \\ &= 315 \text{ m} \end{aligned}$$

This verify's the car's motion profile on the graph. A correct sketch should include axes labeled $t$/s and $v$/m/s with linear segments connecting the points $(0,0), (6,18), (16,18),$ and $(25,0)$.

ti84.instructions: Add the areas calculated in the previous step.

steps.title: Calculate the peak velocity
steps.content:
To sketch the graph, we first need to find the maximum velocity reached. The car starts from rest ($u = 0$) and accelerates for 6 seconds. Using the kinematic equation from the data booklet:

$$v = u + at$$

Substituting the given values:

$$\begin{aligned} v &= 0 + (3 \text{ m/s}^2 \times 6 \text{ s}) \\ v &= 18 \text{ m/s} \end{aligned}$$

This is the height of our trapezoid.

highlights.text: accelerates uniformly at $3 \text{ m/s}^2$ for 6 seconds

steps.title: Determine the total time
steps.content:
Next, we find the time taken to stop during the deceleration phase. The car decelerates from $18 \text{ m/s}$ to $0 \text{ m/s}$ at $2 \text{ m/s}^2$:

$$\begin{aligned} v &= u + at \\ 0 &= 18 + (-2)t \\ 2t &= 18 \\ t &= 9 \text{ s} \end{aligned}$$

The total time for the trip is the sum of the three phases:
1. Acceleration: $6 \text{ s}$
2. Constant velocity: $10 \text{ s}$
3. Deceleration: $9 \text{ s}$

Total time = $6 + 10 + 9 = 25 \text{ s}$.

highlights.text: moves at a constant velocity for 10 seconds

steps.title: Identify trapezoid dimensions
steps.content:
On a velocity-time graph, the distance is the area under the curve. This motion forms a **trapezoid** with the following dimensions:
- **Base $b$ (total time):** $25 \text{ s}$
- **Base $a$ (constant velocity duration):** $10 \text{ s}$
- **Height $h$ (maximum velocity):** $18 \text{ m/s}$

The graph starts at $(0,0)$, climbs to $(6,18)$, remains flat until $(16,18)$, and returns to the axis at $(25,0)$. This is covered in **Topic A.1: Kinematics**.

steps.title: Calculate total distance
steps.content:
We can calculate the total distance in a single step using the area of a trapezoid formula:

$$\text{Distance} = \frac{1}{2}(a + b)h$$

Substituting our values:

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Distance} &= \frac{1}{2}(10 + 25) \times 18 \\ \text{Distance} &= \frac{1}{2}(35) \times 18 \\ \text{Distance} &= 315 \text{ m} \end{aligned}$$

This verifies the total distance calculation required for the final mark.

desmos.instructions: Enter the formula into a calculation cell.
