parts.content: Consider the graph showing the variation with time $t$ of the speed $v$ of the ball from the instant it is released until it impacts the ground. State the value of the area under that curve.
parts.markscheme:
- State the value: 28 «m» <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Identify the physical meaning of the area
steps.content:
In kinematics, specifically within **Topic A.1: Kinematics**, the area under a speed-time graph represents the total distance travelled by the object during that time interval.

Since the graph shows the speed from release until impact, the total area corresponds to the total distance the ball falls.

steps.title: Relate distance to height
steps.content: The question specifies that the ball is released from a specific height and travels until it hits the ground. Therefore, the total distance travelled is equal to this initial height.

highlights.text: released from rest from a height of 28 m

steps.title: State the final value
steps.content:
Since the drop height is 28 m, the area under the curve is simply:

$$\text{Area} = 28$$

steps.title: Analyze the graph axes
steps.content:
First, we look at the variables plotted on the graph axes to understand what the area represents. 

- The vertical axis represents **speed** ($v$), measured in meters per second ($$\text{m s}^{-1}$$).
- The horizontal axis represents **time** ($t$), measured in seconds ($$\text{s}$$).

steps.title: Perform dimensional analysis
steps.content:
The area under a graph is the product of the quantities on the vertical and horizontal axes. We can determine the physical quantity this represents by multiplying their units:

$$ \text{m s}^{-1} \times \text{s} = \text{m} $$

Since the resulting unit is meters ($$\text{m}$$), the area under the speed-time graph represents the total **distance** traveled by the ball.

steps.title: Identify the distance from data
steps.content: The graph covers the motion from release until impact. Therefore, the total distance traveled is equal to the drop height given in the question specification.

steps.title: State the value
steps.content:
The value of the area corresponds to this height.

$$ \text{Area} = 28 $$

parts.content: Given that terminal velocity is $v=9.5 \, \mathrm{m\,s^{-1}}$, determine $k$.
parts.markscheme:
- Substitute values into equation: $m g=k v^2$ with $v=9.5 \, \mathrm{m\,s}^{-1}$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Calculate $k$: $k= \frac{m g}{v^2}=\frac{2.7 \times 10^{-3} \times 9.81}{9.5^2}=2.9 \times 10^{-4} \, \mathrm{kg\,m^{-1}}$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Understand terminal velocity
steps.content:
When a ball reaches terminal velocity, it is no longer accelerating. This happens when the downward force of gravity (weight) is exactly balanced by the upward force of air resistance (drag).

According to Newton's Second Law, if the net force is zero, the acceleration is zero and the velocity remains constant. This is covered in **Topic A.2: Forces and momentum**.

steps.title: Set up force balance
steps.content:
At terminal velocity, we equate the weight of the ball to the air resistance force:

$$mg = kv^2$$

To solve for $k$, we rearrange the formula:

$$k = \frac{mg}{v^2}$$

steps.title: Convert units and values
steps.content:
Before calculating, ensure all units are in SI standard (kg, m, s):
- Mass: $m = 2.7 \text{ g} = 2.7 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg}$
- Gravity: $g = 9.81 \text{ m s}^{-2}$ (standard IB value)
- Terminal velocity: $v = 9.5 \text{ m s}^{-1}$

steps.title: Calculate the constant k
steps.content:
Substitute the values into our rearranged equation:

$$k = \frac{2.7 \times 10^{-3} \times 9.81}{9.5^2}$$

$$k \approx 2.9 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg m}^{-1}$$

Rounding to two significant figures (as per the input data), we get $2.9 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg m}^{-1}$. This earns both marks: one for the correct substitution and one for the final value.

The rocket's engine provides a constant upward acceleration of $12.0 \text{ m s}^{-2}$ for the first $4.0 \text{ s}$ of the flight. After this time, the engine shuts off and the rocket enters free fall, eventually returning to the surface. Air resistance is negligible. Let the acceleration due to gravity $g = 9.81 \text{ m s}^{-2}$.

parts.content: Calculate the vertical velocity of the rocket at the moment the engine shuts off.
parts.markscheme: - Substitute values into velocity formula: $v = at = 12.0 \times 4.0 = 48 \text{ m s}^{-1}$  <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Determine the altitude of the rocket at $t = 4.0 \text{ s}$.
parts.markscheme: - Substitute values into displacement formula: $s = \frac{1}{2}at^{2} = 0.5 \times 12.0 \times 4.0^{2} = 96 \text{ m}$  <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Calculate the time taken for the rocket to reach its maximum height after the engine has shut off.
parts.markscheme: - Use $v = u + at$ with $v = 0$: $0 = 48 - 9.81t \Rightarrow t = \frac{48}{9.81} = 4.89 \text{ s}$  <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Determine the maximum altitude reached by the rocket above the ground.
parts.markscheme: - Calculate total height: $H = 96 + \frac{48^{2}}{2 \times 9.81} = 96 + 117.4 = 213 \text{ m}$  <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Calculate the total time $T$ from the moment of launch until the rocket strikes the ground.
parts.markscheme:
- Determine the time taken to fall from maximum height: $t_{fall} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 213.4}{9.81}} = 6.60 \text{ s}$  <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Calculate the total time: $T = 4.0 + 4.89 + 6.60 = 15.5 \text{ s}$  <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Calculate the time it takes for car B to catch up with car A.
parts.markscheme:
When the cars meet, their displacements from their respective starting positions must be equal: $s_A = s_B$

- State displacement for car A: $s_A = 20.0t$ <marks>A1</marks>
- State displacement for car B: $s_B = \frac{1}{2} \times 2.50 \times t^2$
- Equate displacements: $20.0t = \frac{1}{2} \times 2.50 \times t^2$
- Simplify: $20.0t = 1.25t^2$
- Solve for $t$: $t = \frac{20.0}{1.25} = 16.0 \text{ s}$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Define the Catching-Up Condition
steps.content:
For car B to "catch up" with car A, both cars must be at the exact same position at the same time. Since they started from the same point, this means their displacements ($s$) must be equal at time $t$.

$$s_A = s_B$$

steps.title: Displacement of Car A
steps.content:
Car A is traveling at a **constant speed**. This means its acceleration is zero. We can use the kinematic equation for displacement from the data booklet:

$$s = ut + \frac{1}{2}at^{2}$$

Since $a = 0$ and $u = 20.0 \text{ m s}^{-1}$, the equation simplifies to:

$$s_A = 20.0t$$

steps.title: Displacement of Car B
steps.content:
Car B starts from rest and accelerates uniformly. Looking at the data booklet again:

$$s = ut + \frac{1}{2}at^{2}$$

Given that car B starts from rest ($u = 0$) and accelerates at $2.50 \text{ m s}^{-2}$, we substitute these values:

$$\begin{aligned} s_B &= (0)t + \frac{1}{2}(2.50)t^2 \\ s_B &= 1.25t^2 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Equate and Solve
steps.content:
Now, we set the two displacements equal to each other to find the time $t$:

$$20.0t = 1.25t^2$$

To solve for $t$, we can divide both sides by $t$ (noting that $t=0$ is the starting time, but we want the time when they meet later):

$$\begin{aligned} 20.0 &= 1.25t \\ t &= \frac{20.0}{1.25} \\ t &= 16.0 \text{ s} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: In the Run-Matrix menu, enter 20.0 / 1.25 and press EXE.

ti84.instructions: Enter 20.0 / 1.25 on the home screen and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Type 20 / 1.25 into a new expression line.

calculator.description: Calculate the final time value by dividing the speed of Car A by the simplified acceleration coefficient of Car B.

steps.title: Final Answer and Marks
steps.content:
The time it takes for car B to catch up with car A is **16.0 seconds**. 

In an IB exam, you earn marks for:
1.  Setting up the correct expressions for displacement ($s_A$ and $s_B$).
2.  Providing the final correct answer with the appropriate unit (s).

steps.title: Equate average velocities
steps.content:
For car B to catch car A, both cars must travel the same distance in the same amount of time. This means their **average velocities** over that time interval must be equal. 

Car A travels at a constant speed, so its average velocity is simply its constant speed:
$$v_{avg, A} = 20.0 \text{ m s}^{-1}$$

To catch up, car B's average velocity ($v_{avg, B}$) must also be $20.0 \text{ m s}^{-1}$. This concept is part of **Topic A.1: Kinematics**.

steps.title: Find final velocity of B
steps.content:
For an object with uniform acceleration, the average velocity is the arithmetic mean of the initial ($u$) and final ($v$) velocities:

$$v_{avg} = \frac{u + v}{2}$$

Since car B starts from rest ($u = 0$), we can find the final velocity ($v_B$) it needs to reach to have an average velocity of $20.0 \text{ m s}^{-1}$:

$$\begin{aligned} 20.0 &= \frac{0 + v_B}{2} \\ v_B &= 20.0 \times 2 \\ v_B &= 40.0 \text{ m s}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Calculate catch-up time
steps.content:
Now that we know car B's final velocity ($v_B = 40.0 \text{ m s}^{-1}$) and its acceleration, we use the kinematic equation for velocity to solve for time ($t$):

$$v = u + at$$

Substituting the known values:
$$\begin{aligned} 40.0 &= 0 + (2.50 \times t) \\ t &= \frac{40.0}{2.50} \\ t &= 16.0 \text{ s} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: In the calculation menu, divide 40 by 2.5.

calculator.description: Calculate the time by dividing the required final velocity by the acceleration.

steps.title: State final answer
steps.content:
The time it takes for car B to catch up with car A is **16.0 seconds**. 

In an IB exam, you would earn 1 mark for equating the motion of both cars (effectively setting $s_A = s_B$) and 1 mark for the correct final answer with units.

steps.title: Define the Catch-up Condition
steps.content:
To solve this using a graphical approach, we first need to understand what it means for one car to "catch up" to another. When Car B catches up with Car A, they are at the exact same position at the same time. 

In physics terms, their **displacements ($s$)** from the starting point must be equal at time $t$:
$$s_A = s_B$$

steps.title: Model Car A's Displacement
steps.content:
Car A moves at a **constant speed**. We can model its displacement ($s_A$) as a linear function of time ($t$):

$$s_A = 20.0t$$

steps.title: Model Car B's Displacement
steps.content:
Car B starts from rest and accelerates uniformly. To find its displacement function ($s_B$), we use the kinematic equation from the **Mechanics** section of your data booklet:

$$\text{From data booklet: } s = ut + \frac{1}{2}at^2$$

Since Car B starts from rest ($u = 0$) and has an acceleration of $2.50 \text{ m s}^{-2}$:

$$\begin{aligned} s_B &= (0)t + \frac{1}{2}(2.50)t^2 \\ s_B &= 1.25t^2 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Find the Intersection Point
steps.content:
Now, we can treat these two equations as functions on a graph where the $y$-axis is displacement ($s$) and the $x$-axis is time ($t$). We are looking for the point where the line $y = 20x$ intersects the parabola $y = 1.25x^2$.

While they both start at $(0,0)$, we want the **second** intersection point where $t > 0$.

gdc.instructions:
1. Go to the Graphing menu.
2. Input f1(x) = 20x and f2(x) = 1.25x².
3. Adjust the viewing window to see the intersection (e.g., x: [0, 20], y: [0, 400]).
4. Use the 'Intersection' tool (usually under Analyze Graph) to find the point where the curves meet.

ti84.instructions:
1. Press [Y=].
2. Enter Y1 = 20X.
3. Enter Y2 = 1.25X².
4. Press [WINDOW] and set Xmax = 20 and Ymax = 400.
5. Press [GRAPH].
6. Press [2nd] [TRACE] (CALC) and select '5: intersect'.
7. Move the cursor near the second intersection point and press [ENTER] three times.

desmos.instructions:
1. Type y = 20x in the first row.
2. Type y = 1.25x^2 in the second row.
3. Click on the point where the two lines intersect to reveal the coordinates.

calculator.description: Find the intersection of the two displacement functions to determine the catch-up time.

steps.title: State the Final Result
steps.content:
The calculator shows the intersection occurs at $x = 16$. In our physics model, $x$ represents time $t$.

Thus, Car B catches up with Car A at:
$$t = 16.0 \text{ s}$$

This matches the requirement to equate the two expressions and solve for $t$:
$$\begin{aligned} 20.0t &= 1.25t^2 \\ 20.0 &= 1.25t \\ t &= \frac{20.0}{1.25} = 16.0 \text{ s} \end{aligned}$$

parts.content: Determine the distance each car has travelled by the time car B catches up.
parts.markscheme:
- Calculate distance for car A: $s_A = 20.0 \times 16.0 = 320 \text{ m}$ <marks>A1</marks>
- Calculate distance for car B: $s_B = \frac{1}{2} \times 2.50 \times (16.0)^2 = 320 \text{ m}$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

parts.content: Calculate the speed of car B at the moment it catches up with car A.
parts.markscheme:
- Substitute into velocity equation: $v = u + at = 0 + 2.50 \times 16.0$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Calculate final speed: $v = 40.0 \text{ m s}^{-1}$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

parts.content: Discuss one assumption made in this problem and its possible impact on the result.
parts.markscheme:
- State assumption: Assumes both cars move along the same straight line without any hindrance such as friction or traffic. <marks>R1</marks>
- Explain impact: In reality, factors like delayed reaction time or road conditions could cause deviations from predicted values. <marks>R1</marks>

2 marks total

questions.specification: A swimmer in a pool follows a specific path while training: The swimmer starts at point X and swims 25 meters east to point Y. From point Y, the swimmer then turns and swims 40 meters north to point Z. After reaching point Z, the swimmer makes a turn and swims 30 meters southwest, arriving at point W.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic A - Space, time and motion > A.1 Kinematics > A.1.1 Describing motion quantitatively and qualitatively
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.1.1 Describing motion quantitatively and qualitatively
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: All parts focus on kinematics quantities: describing a multi-segment path with distances and directions, calculating resultant displacement vectors (magnitude/direction), average speed from total distance/time, and explaining distance vs displacement.

parts.content: Describe the swimmer’s total path in terms of distance and direction for each segment.
parts.markscheme:
- Segment 1: Swims from X to Y, 25 meters east <marks>A1</marks>

- Segment 2: Swims from Y to Z, 40 meters north <marks>A1</marks>

- Segment 3: Swims from Z to W, 30 meters southwest <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

steps.title: Analyze the motion
steps.content: To describe the swimmer's path, we break it down into distinct legs. In physics, specifically in **Topic A.1: Kinematics**, we describe motion by identifying the distance (a scalar quantity) and the direction of travel for each part of the journey.

steps.title: Identify the first segment
steps.content:
The journey begins at point $X$. The swimmer moves directly to point $Y$. 

**Segment 1:** Swims from $X$ to $Y$, a distance of $25\text{ meters}$ in the direction of **east**.

highlights.text: swims 25 meters east to point Y

steps.title: Identify the second segment
steps.content:
From point $Y$, the swimmer changes direction to reach point $Z$. 

**Segment 2:** Swims from $Y$ to $Z$, a distance of $40\text{ meters}$ in the direction of **north**.

highlights.text: swims 40 meters north to point Z

steps.title: Identify the third segment
steps.content:
After reaching point $Z$, the swimmer makes a final turn to reach point $W$.

**Segment 3:** Swims from $Z$ to $W$, a distance of $30\text{ meters}$ in the direction of **southwest**.

highlights.text: swims 30 meters southwest, arriving at point W

parts.content: Determine the net displacement from point X to point W in terms of both magnitude and direction.
parts.markscheme:
- Calculate net x-component: $25 - 21.2 = 3.8$ m (east) <marks>A1</marks>

- Calculate net y-component: $40 - 21.2 = 18.8$ m (north) <marks>A1</marks>

- Find magnitude using Pythagorean theorem: 
$$\text{magnitude} = \sqrt{3.8^2 + 18.8^2} = 19.2 \text{ meters}$$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Calculate direction using inverse tangent:
$$\theta = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{18.8}{3.8}\right) = 78.6^\circ \text{ north of east}$$ <marks>A1</marks>

4 marks total

steps.title: Define the Coordinate System
steps.content:
To solve this using component resolution, we first set up a standard Cartesian coordinate system:
- **East** is the positive $x$-direction ($+x$).
- **North** is the positive $y$-direction ($+y$).

This makes **West** negative $x$ and **South** negative $y$. We will break each leg of the journey into these $x$ and $y$ components.

steps.title: Resolve the First Two Legs
steps.content:
The first two displacements are simple because they align perfectly with our axes:

1. **X to Y:** $25\text{ m}$ East
   - $x_1 = +25.0\text{ m}$
   - $y_1 = 0\text{ m}$

2. **Y to Z:** $40\text{ m}$ North
   - $x_2 = 0\text{ m}$
   - $y_2 = +40.0\text{ m}$

steps.title: Resolve the Third Leg
steps.content:
The third leg is $30\text{ m}$ **Southwest**. In navigation, Southwest means exactly halfway between South and West, which is an angle of $45^\circ$.

Since it is West and South, both components will be negative:
- $x_3 = -30 \cos(45^\circ)$
- $y_3 = -30 \sin(45^\circ)$

Using $\cos(45^\circ) = \sin(45^\circ) \approx 0.707$:

$$x_3 = -21.2\text{ m}$$
$$y_3 = -21.2\text{ m}$$

gdc.instructions: Set angle mode to degrees. Input 30 * cos(45).

ti84.instructions: Ensure the calculator is in DEGREE mode. Calculate 30 * cos(45) and 30 * sin(45).

desmos.instructions: Type the expression. Make sure the toggle is set to 'deg'.

calculator.description: Calculate the components for the 30m Southwest vector.

steps.title: Sum the Net Components
steps.content:
Now, we add all the $x$ and $y$ parts together to find the resultant vector components from X to W:

**Net $x$-component ($R_x$):**
$$R_x = 25.0 + 0 - 21.2 = 3.8\text{ m (East)}$$

**Net $y$-component ($R_y$):**
$$R_y = 0 + 40.0 - 21.2 = 18.8\text{ m (North)}$$

steps.title: Calculate the Resultant Magnitude
steps.content:
To find the total magnitude of the displacement, we use the Pythagorean theorem:

$$\text{Magnitude} = \sqrt{R_x^2 + R_y^2}$$
$$\text{Magnitude} = \sqrt{3.8^2 + 18.8^2}$$
$$\text{Magnitude} \approx 19.2\text{ m}$$

gdc.instructions: Input the square root of the sum of the squares of the components.

desmos.instructions: Type sqrt(3.8^2 + 18.8^2) into the expression bar.

calculator.description: Calculate the resultant magnitude using the Pythagorean theorem.

steps.title: Calculate the Final Direction
steps.content:
Finally, we find the direction $\theta$ using the inverse tangent function:

$$\theta = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{\text{opposite}}{\text{adjacent}}\right) = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{R_y}{R_x}\right)$$
$$\theta = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{18.8}{3.8}\right)$$
$$\theta \approx 78.6^\circ$$

Since both components are positive, the direction is **$78.6^\circ$ North of East**.

gdc.instructions: Use the inverse tangent function atan(18.8 / 3.8). Ensure you are in degree mode.

calculator.description: Find the angle of the displacement vector.

steps.title: Calculate the intermediate displacement
steps.content:
First, we find the resultant displacement for the first two legs of the journey: 25 m East ($X$ to $Y$) and 40 m North ($Y$ to $Z$). Since these legs are perpendicular, we can use the Pythagorean theorem to find the magnitude of the displacement vector $XZ$.

$$XZ = \sqrt{25^2 + 40^2} \approx 47.17\text{ m}$$

Next, we find the angle $\theta_1$ of this vector relative to the East (the positive x-axis):

$$\theta_1 = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{40}{25}\right) \approx 57.99^\circ\text{ North of East}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter sqrt(25^2+40^2) for magnitude and atan(40/25) for angle. Ensure result is in degrees.

ti84.instructions: To find the magnitude, press [sqrt] 25^2 + 40^2 [ENTER]. To find the angle, press [2nd] [tan^-1] (40 / 25) [ENTER]. Ensure the calculator is in Degree mode.

desmos.instructions: Type sqrt(25^2+40^2) and arctan(40/25). Ensure degree mode is active.

calculator.description: Calculate magnitude and angle of XZ

steps.title: Determine the interior angle
steps.content:
Now consider the final leg: 30 m Southwest from $Z$ to $W$. "Southwest" means an angle of $45^\circ$ South of West (or $225^\circ$ relative to East). 

To use the Law of Cosines in triangle $XZW$, we need the interior angle at vertex $Z$. 
- The line $ZX$ is $57.99^\circ$ South of West (opposite to $XZ$).
- The line $ZW$ is $45.00^\circ$ South of West.

The interior angle $\gamma$ between these two lines at point $Z$ is the difference between these angles:

$$\gamma = 57.99^\circ - 45.00^\circ = 12.99^\circ$$

steps.title: Apply Law of Cosines
steps.content:
We now have a triangle $XZW$ with two sides ($XZ \approx 47.17\text{ m}$ and $ZW = 30\text{ m}$) and the included angle ($\gamma = 12.99^\circ$). We use the Law of Cosines to find the magnitude of the net displacement $XW$:

$$XW^2 = XZ^2 + ZW^2 - 2(XZ)(ZW)\cos(\gamma)$$
$$XW^2 = 47.17^2 + 30^2 - 2(47.17)(30)\cos(12.99^\circ)$$
$$XW^2 \approx 2225 + 900 - 2757.77 \approx 367.23$$
$$XW = \sqrt{367.23} \approx 19.16\text{ m}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter the formula for XW using the cos function. Ensure the angle is in degrees.

ti84.instructions: Enter sqrt(47.17^2 + 30^2 - 2 * 47.17 * 30 * cos(12.99)). Ensure you are in Degree mode.

desmos.instructions: Type the expression for XW using sqrt and cos in degree mode.

calculator.description: Calculate XW magnitude

steps.title: Determine the direction
steps.content:
To find the direction of the net displacement, we use the Law of Sines to find the angle $\alpha$ (angle $\angle ZXW$) inside the triangle:

$$\frac{\sin(\alpha)}{ZW} = \frac{\sin(\gamma)}{XW}$$
$$\sin(\alpha) = \frac{30 \cdot \sin(12.99^\circ)}{19.16} \approx 0.352$$
$$\alpha = \sin^{-1}(0.352) \approx 20.61^\circ$$

The vector $XZ$ was at $57.99^\circ$ North of East. Looking at the geometry, $XW$ is further North than $XZ$, so we add this internal angle to the initial angle:

$$\text{Direction} = 57.99^\circ + 20.61^\circ = 78.60^\circ\text{ North of East}$$

**Final Answer:** Magnitude is $19.2\text{ m}$, direction is $78.6^\circ$ North of East.

gdc.instructions: Enter asin(30 * sin(12.99) / 19.16) + 57.99 in degree mode.

desmos.instructions: Type arcsin(30 * sin(12.99) / 19.16) + 57.99. Check degree mode.

calculator.description: Calculate direction angle

steps.title: Establish a Scale
steps.content:
To solve this problem graphically, we first need to choose an appropriate scale so that the diagram fits on a page while remaining precise. A good choice is $1\text{ cm} : 5\text{ m}$. 

We convert our real-world distances into drawing lengths:
- Vector XY: $25\text{ m} \div 5 = 5\text{ cm}$
- Vector YZ: $40\text{ m} \div 5 = 8\text{ cm}$
- Vector ZW: $30\text{ m} \div 5 = 6\text{ cm}$

This follows the principles of **Topic A.1: Kinematics**, specifically describing motion quantitatively.

steps.title: Draw Vectors XY and YZ
steps.content:
Using a ruler and a protractor, construct the first two legs of the journey:
1.  **Point X to Y**: Start at point X. Draw a horizontal line exactly $5\text{ cm}$ long to the right (East) to reach point Y.
2.  **Point Y to Z**: From point Y, use a protractor to measure a $90^{\circ}$ angle and draw a vertical line $8\text{ cm}$ long upwards (North) to reach point Z.

steps.title: Draw Vector ZW (Southwest)
steps.content:
From point Z, we need to draw the final leg. "Southwest" means exactly $45^{\circ}$ between South and West.

Place your protractor at point Z and measure an angle $45^{\circ}$ below the horizontal (to the left). Draw a line $6\text{ cm}$ long ($30\text{ m}$) in this direction to reach point W.

steps.title: Measure the Resultant Displacement
steps.content:
The net displacement is represented by the vector starting at X and ending at W. 

1.  **Magnitude**: Use your ruler to measure the straight-line distance from point X to point W. You should find it is approximately $3.84\text{ cm}$.
2.  **Direction**: Use your protractor at point X to measure the angle between the horizontal (East) line and the line XW. You should find an angle of approximately $78.6^{\circ}$ North of East.

steps.title: Convert Back to Meters
steps.content:
Finally, convert your measured drawing length back into real-world units using your scale ($1\text{ cm} = 5\text{ m}$):

$$\text{Net Displacement Magnitude} = 3.84\text{ cm} \times 5\text{ m/cm} = 19.2\text{ m}$$

According to the markscheme, the net x-component is $3.8\text{ m}$ and the net y-component is $18.8\text{ m}$. We can verify our drawing's magnitude using the Pythagorean theorem:

$$\text{magnitude} = \sqrt{3.8^2 + 18.8^2} \approx 19.2\text{ m}$$

gdc.instructions: Use the square root function to calculate the magnitude from the x and y components.

desmos.instructions: Type the expression into the input bar to find the magnitude.

calculator.description: Calculate the magnitude of the displacement from its components.

steps.title: State Final Answer
steps.content:
The net displacement from point X to point W is **$19.2\text{ m}$** at an angle of **$78.6^{\circ}$ North of East** (or a bearing of $011.4^{\circ}$).

In an IB exam, marks are awarded for:
- Correct magnitude ($19.2\text{ m}$)
- Correct angle ($78.6^{\circ}$)
- Clear indication of direction (North of East)

parts.content: Calculate the swimmer’s displacement from point Y to point W.
parts.markscheme:
- Identify x-component of displacement $Y \to W$: $-21.2$ m (west) <marks>A1</marks>

- Identify y-component of displacement $Y \to W$: $18.8$ m (north) <marks>A1</marks>

- Calculate magnitude and direction:
$$\text{displacement} = \sqrt{(-21.2)^2 + 18.8^2} = 28.3 \text{ m at } 41.6^\circ \text{ north of west}$$ <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

<note>Accept answers between 28.2 and 28.4 meters due to rounding</note>

steps.title: Define the Reference Point
steps.content:
To find the displacement from point Y to point W, we treat point Y as our origin $(0, 0)$. 

We are given that point Z is 40 meters north of point Y. In a coordinate system, this means point Z is at $(0, 40)$. To find W, we need to analyze the movement from Z to W.

steps.title: Decompose Vector ZW
steps.content:
The swimmer moves 30 meters southwest from Z. "Southwest" implies an angle of $45^\circ$ south of west. We can break this displacement into horizontal (westward) and vertical (southward) components using trigonometry:

$$\begin{aligned} \Delta x_{ZW} &= 30 \cos(45^\circ) \approx 21.2 \text{ m (West)} \\ \Delta y_{ZW} &= 30 \sin(45^\circ) \approx 21.2 \text{ m (South)} \end{aligned}$$

This decomposition is a key part of **Topic A.1 Kinematics** when dealing with vectors in two dimensions.

gdc.instructions: Use trig functions in degree mode to find components.

ti84.instructions: Ensure the calculator is in DEGREE mode. Calculate 30*cos(45) and 30*sin(45).

desmos.instructions: Enter the expressions in degree mode.

calculator.description: Calculate the components of the 30m southwest vector.

steps.title: Find Net Components from Y
steps.content:
Now we calculate the total horizontal and vertical displacement starting from point Y:

1.  **Horizontal ($x$):** There was no horizontal movement from Y to Z, so the net horizontal displacement is simply the westward component from Z to W.
    $$x = -21.2 \text{ m (where negative is West)}$$

2.  **Vertical ($y$):** The swimmer went 40 m North then 21.2 m South.
    $$y = 40 - 21.2 = 18.8 \text{ m (North)}$$

steps.title: Calculate Resultant Magnitude
steps.content:
The magnitude of the displacement from Y to W is found using the Pythagorean theorem:

$$s = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2}$$
$$s = \sqrt{(-21.2)^2 + 18.8^2}$$
$$s \approx 28.3 \text{ m}$$

This calculation would earn you the final mark for magnitude.

ti84.instructions: Use the square root and squaring functions.

desmos.instructions: Evaluate the square root of the sum of squares.

calculator.description: Calculate the resultant magnitude.

steps.title: Calculate Resultant Direction
steps.content:
To find the direction, we find the angle $\theta$ relative to the West axis (since our $x$ component is West and $y$ is North):

$$\begin{aligned} \theta &= \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{\text{Opposite}}{\text{Adjacent}}\right) \\ \theta &= \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{18.8}{21.2}\right) \approx 41.6^\circ \end{aligned}$$

Since the vertical component is North and the horizontal is West, the direction is **$41.6^\circ$ North of West**.

gdc.instructions: Find the inverse tangent of the ratio of components.

ti84.instructions: Use the inverse tangent function in DEGREE mode.

desmos.instructions: Enter the arctan function in degree mode.

calculator.description: Calculate the angle in degrees.

steps.title: Final Answer Statement
steps.content:
The swimmer's displacement from point Y to point W is **$28.3 \text{ m}$ at an angle of $41.6^\circ$ North of West**.

In the IB markscheme:
- 1 mark for the x-component ($21.2$ m West).
- 1 mark for the y-component ($18.8$ m North).
- 1 mark for the final magnitude and direction.

steps.title: Visualize the vector path
steps.content: To calculate the displacement from point Y to point W, we treat the segments $YZ$ and $ZW$ as sides of a triangle. Point Y is our origin for this displacement, point Z is 40 meters North, and point W is reached by traveling 30 meters Southwest from Z.

steps.title: Identify the interior angle
steps.content: The path $YZ$ is North, so the reverse direction $ZY$ is exactly South. From point Z, the swimmer travels Southwest to point W. Since Southwest is $45^\circ$ from South, the interior angle $\angle YZW$ of our triangle is $45^\circ$.

steps.title: Apply Law of Cosines
steps.content:
We use the Law of Cosines to find the magnitude of side $YW$ ($c$), where $a = 40\text{ m}$, $b = 30\text{ m}$, and $C = 45^\circ$:

$$c^2 = a^2 + b^2 - 2ab \cos(C)$$

$$\begin{aligned} YW^2 &= 40^2 + 30^2 - 2(40)(30) \cos(45^\circ) \\ YW^2 &= 1600 + 900 - 2400(0.7071) \\ YW^2 &\approx 802.96 \end{aligned}$$

$$YW = \sqrt{802.96} \approx 28.3 \text{ m}$$

gdc.instructions: Use the square root and cosine functions. Check that the angle unit is set to degrees.

ti84.instructions: Type the expression into the home screen. Ensure your calculator is in DEGREE mode.

desmos.instructions: Enter the formula directly. Select 'Degrees' in the graph settings (wrench icon).

calculator.description: Calculate the magnitude of YW using the Law of Cosines.

steps.title: Determine the direction
steps.content:
To find the direction, we can use the Law of Sines to find the interior angle at $Y$ (let's call it $\alpha$):

$$\frac{\sin(\alpha)}{30} = \frac{\sin(45^\circ)}{28.3}$$

$$\alpha = \arcsin\left(\frac{30 \sin(45^\circ)}{28.3}\right) \approx 48.4^\circ$$

Since the side $YZ$ points North, this angle is $48.4^\circ$ West of North. Converting this to North of West:

$$90^\circ - 48.4^\circ = 41.6^\circ$$

parts.content: If the swimmer completes the path in 5 minutes, what is the swimmer’s average speed in meters per second?
parts.markscheme:
- Calculate total distance: $25 + 40 + 30 = 95$ meters <marks>M1</marks>

- Convert time to seconds: $5$ minutes $= 300$ seconds and calculate average speed:
$$v_{avg} = \frac{95}{300} = 0.317 \text{ m/s}$$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

<examTip>Remember to include units in your final answer</examTip>

parts.content: Explain why the magnitude of the total distance covered differs from the net displacement.
parts.markscheme:
- Distance measures total path length/actual path covered (95 meters) <marks>A1</marks>

- Displacement is straight-line distance between start and end points (19.2 meters) / vector quantity measuring direct change in position <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

<note>Accept clear explanation that distance includes all segments while displacement is direct path</note>

steps.title: Define distance as scalar
steps.content:
In IB Physics, **distance** is a scalar quantity that refers to the "how much ground an object has covered" during its motion. To find the total distance, we simply sum up every segment of the swimmer's path regardless of direction:

$$\text{Total Distance} = 25\text{ m} + 40\text{ m} + 30\text{ m} = 95\text{ m}$$

steps.title: Define displacement as vector
steps.content:
**Displacement** is a vector quantity that represents the overall change in position. It is the straight-line distance from the starting point (X) to the final point (W). 

Because the swimmer changes direction multiple times, the final point (W) is much closer to the start (X) than the total path length suggests. The magnitude of this direct vector is approximately $19.2\text{ m}$.

steps.title: Explain the difference
steps.content:
The magnitude of the total distance differs from the net displacement because the swimmer does not travel in a single straight line. 

Since the path includes turns (East, then North, then Southwest), the **total path length (distance)** accounts for every meter swum, whereas the **displacement** only measures the net gap between the start and end positions. This concept is covered in **Topic A.1.1: Describing motion**.

steps.title: Define Total Distance
steps.content:
In kinematics, **distance** is a scalar quantity that measures the actual ground covered by an object during its motion. To find the total distance, we simply add the lengths of every segment of the swimmer's path regardless of direction:

$$\text{Total Distance} = 25\text{ m} + 40\text{ m} + 30\text{ m} = 95\text{ m}$$

This represents the cumulative length of the journey from point X through Y and Z, ending at W.

steps.title: Define Net Displacement
steps.content:
Unlike distance, **displacement** is a vector quantity that represents the direct change in position. It is defined as the shortest straight-line distance from the starting point (X) to the final point (W).

While the swimmer's total path length is $95\text{ m}$, the straight-line "shortcut" from X to W is much shorter (calculated in previous parts as approximately $19.2\text{ m}$ in a specific direction).

steps.title: Analyze the Geometrical Path
steps.content:
The reason these two values differ is due to the **geometry of the path**. 

Because the swimmer makes turns (moving East, then North, then Southwest) rather than traveling in a single, unwavering straight line from X to W, the total path length must exceed the displacement. In any geometric shape, the sum of the individual sides (the path) is always greater than the direct resultant vector (the shortcut) unless the motion is in one constant direction. 

This distinction is a fundamental concept in **Topic A.1: Kinematics** regarding how we describe motion qualitatively.

steps.title: Define Distance and Displacement
steps.content:
To understand the difference, we must look at the definitions from **Topic A.1: Kinematics**. 

*   **Total Distance** is a scalar quantity representing the total length of the path traveled.
*   **Net Displacement** is a vector quantity representing the straight-line change in position from the starting point to the final point.

steps.title: Calculate Total Distance
steps.content:
The total distance is simply the sum of the magnitudes of every segment the swimmer covered. We add the lengths of the three segments together:

$$\text{Distance} = 25\text{ m} + 40\text{ m} + 30\text{ m}$$
$$\text{Distance} = 95\text{ m}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter the sum on the calculation screen.

ti84.instructions: Enter the sum directly on the home screen.

calculator.description: Calculate the sum of all path segments.

steps.title: Calculate Net Displacement Magnitude
steps.content:
Displacement only cares about the start and end points. We calculate the net change in the $x$ (East-West) and $y$ (North-South) directions:

1.  **Horizontal ($x$):** $25 - 30\cos(45^\circ) \approx 3.79\text{ m}$
2.  **Vertical ($y$):** $40 - 30\sin(45^\circ) \approx 18.79\text{ m}$

Using the Pythagorean theorem, the magnitude of the displacement is:

$$\text{Displacement} = \sqrt{3.79^2 + 18.79^2} \approx 19.2\text{ m}$$

ti84.instructions: Use the square root and trig functions. Ensure mode is in DEGREES.

desmos.instructions: Enter the formula for the magnitude of the displacement vector.

steps.title: Compare and Conclude
steps.content:
By comparing the two values ($95\text{ m}$ vs $19.2\text{ m}$), we see a significant difference. 

This occurs because the swimmer did not travel in a single straight line. Each turn (to the North and then back Southwest) added to the **total distance** but actually brought the swimmer closer to the starting point, resulting in a much smaller **net displacement**.

parts.content: Calculate by what percentage the magnitude of the net displacement is less than the total distance travelled.
parts.markscheme:
- Calculate difference between total distance and displacement: $95 - 19.2 = 75.8$ m <marks>M1</marks>

- Calculate percentage difference: $\frac{75.8}{95} \times 100 = 79.8\%$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Calculate total distance
steps.content:
To start, we need to find the total distance travelled by the swimmer. Distance is a scalar quantity, so we simply add the lengths of each segment of the path:

$$\text{Total Distance} = 25\text{ m} + 40\text{ m} + 30\text{ m} = 95\text{ m}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the sum of the three legs of the journey.

steps.title: Resolve displacement components
steps.content:
Next, we find the net displacement using vector resolution. We'll set East as the $+x$ direction and North as the $+y$ direction. 

1. **25 m East**: $(+25, 0)$
2. **40 m North**: $(0, +40)$
3. **30 m Southwest**: Since Southwest is exactly between South and West ($225^{\circ}$ from East), both components are negative and use $\cos(45^{\circ})$ or $\sin(45^{\circ})$:
   - $x$-component: $-30 \cos(45^{\circ})$
   - $y$-component: $-30 \sin(45^{\circ})$

Now, sum the components to find the resultant vector $\vec{R}$:
$$\begin{aligned} R_x &= 25 + 0 - 30 \cos(45^{\circ}) \approx 3.79\text{ m} \\ R_y &= 0 + 40 - 30 \sin(45^{\circ}) \approx 18.79\text{ m} \end{aligned}$$

Using the Pythagorean theorem from **Topic A.1 Kinematics**:
$$\text{Magnitude} = \sqrt{R_x^2 + R_y^2} = \sqrt{3.79^2 + 18.79^2} \approx 19.2\text{ m}$$

gdc.instructions: Use the square root and trig functions to compute the magnitude.

ti84.instructions: Ensure mode is in DEGREES. Enter: √( (25 - 30*cos(45))^2 + (40 - 30*sin(45))^2 )

desmos.instructions: Set to Degree mode. Calculate the square root of the sum of the squared components.

steps.title: Find the difference
steps.content:
The question asks for the percentage by which the displacement is *less than* the distance. First, find the absolute difference between the total distance and the displacement magnitude:

$$\text{Difference} = 95\text{ m} - 19.2\text{ m} = 75.8\text{ m}$$

steps.title: Calculate the percentage
steps.content:
Finally, we calculate the percentage difference relative to the total distance travelled:

$$\text{Percentage} = \frac{\text{Difference}}{\text{Total Distance}} \times 100$$

$$\text{Percentage} = \frac{75.8}{95} \times 100 = 79.8\%$$

parts.content: Calculate the time taken for the stone to reach the maximum height.
parts.markscheme:
- $u_y = 25.0 \sin(40.0^\circ) = 16.1\ \text{m s}^{-1}$
- $t_{\text{up}} = \frac{u_y}{g} = \frac{16.1}{9.81} = 1.64\ \text{s}$
- Correct vertical component <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Correct time to apex with unit <marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Find the vertical velocity component
steps.content:
To find the time taken to reach the maximum height, we focus only on the vertical motion. First, we need to calculate the initial vertical velocity ($u_y$) using the initial speed and launch angle.

$$u_y = u \sin(\theta)$$

$$u_y = 25.0 \times \sin(40.0^\circ)$$

$$u_y \approx 16.1\ \text{m s}^{-1}$$

gdc.instructions: Set calculator to Degree mode. Input the expression.

ti84.instructions: Ensure the calculator is in Degree mode. Press [MODE], select 'DEGREE'. Then press [25] [SIN] [40] [ENTER].

desmos.instructions: Set the calculator to Degrees. Input the expression.

calculator.description: Calculate the initial vertical velocity component.

steps.title: Apply the kinematic equation
steps.content:
At the maximum height (the apex), the vertical velocity of the stone is exactly zero ($v_y = 0$). We can use the kinematic equation for constant acceleration from the data booklet:

$$v = u + at$$

In the vertical direction, the acceleration is due to gravity ($a = -g$). Taking 'up' as the positive direction:

$$0 = u_y - gt$$

Rearranging for $t$:

$$t = \frac{u_y}{g}$$

steps.title: Calculate the time to apex
steps.content:
Now we substitute our values ($u_y = 16.1\ \text{m s}^{-1}$ and $g = 9.81\ \text{m s}^{-2}$) into the equation to find the time:

$$t = \frac{16.1}{9.81}$$

$$t \approx 1.64\ \text{s}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the time taken to reach the maximum height.

steps.title: Final answer and marks
steps.content:
The stone takes **1.64 seconds** to reach its maximum height. 

In the markscheme, you earn **1 mark** for correctly identifying the vertical component ($16.1\ \text{m s}^{-1}$) and **1 mark** for the final calculated time with the correct units (s).

steps.title: Calculate initial vertical velocity
steps.content:
To find the time taken to reach maximum height, we first need to determine the vertical component of the initial velocity ($u_y$). 

Using trigonometry for the given launch speed and angle:
$$u_y = u \sin(\theta)$$
$$u_y = 25.0 \cdot \sin(40.0^\circ) \approx 16.1 \text{ m s}^{-1}$$

gdc.instructions: Set calculator to Degree mode. Input 25 * sin(40).

ti84.instructions: Ensure the mode is set to Degrees. Enter 25 * sin(40).

desmos.instructions: Set to Degrees in settings. Type 25 * sin(40).

calculator.description: Calculate the initial vertical velocity component.

steps.title: Find maximum vertical displacement
steps.content:
At the maximum height (the apex), the vertical velocity ($v_y$) is exactly $0$. We can use the kinematic equation relating velocity and displacement to find the height $s$ relative to the launch point.

Taking upwards as positive, the acceleration is $a = -g = -9.81 \text{ m s}^{-2}$. Using the formula:
$$v^2 = u^2 + 2as$$

Substituting our values:
$$0^2 = (16.1)^2 + 2(-9.81)s$$
$$s = \frac{16.1^2}{2 \cdot 9.81} \approx 13.2 \text{ m}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the maximum vertical displacement (height).

steps.title: Solve for time
steps.content:
Now that we have the maximum displacement ($s \approx 13.2 \text{ m}$), we can use the average velocity kinematic equation to find the time ($t$):

$$s = \left(\frac{u + v}{2}\right)t$$

Substituting the vertical components:
$$13.2 = \left(\frac{16.1 + 0}{2}\right)t$$
$$t = \frac{2 \cdot 13.2}{16.1} \approx 1.64 \text{ s}$$

calculator.description: Solve for time using the displacement and vertical velocity components.

steps.title: State final answer
steps.content:
Rounding to three significant figures, the time taken to reach the maximum height is:

$$t = 1.64 \text{ s}$$

highlights.text: Calculate the time taken for the stone to reach the maximum height.

steps.title: Identify known variables
steps.content:
To solve this problem, we first need to identify the initial conditions of the stone's motion. The stone is launched from a cliff, but to find the time to reach the **maximum height**, we only need to focus on its vertical motion.

From the question, we have:
- Initial speed: $u = 25.0\text{ m s}^{-1}$
- Launch angle: $\theta = 40.0^\circ$
- Acceleration due to gravity: $g = 9.81\text{ m s}^{-2}$ (downwards)

steps.title: Find vertical velocity component
steps.content:
The first step in any projectile motion problem is to split the initial velocity into its horizontal and vertical components. Since we are looking for the maximum height (a vertical peak), we calculate the vertical component, $u_y$:

$$u_y = u \sin(\theta)$$

Substituting our values:
$$u_y = 25.0 \cdot \sin(40.0^\circ)$$

This component represents the initial upward speed of the stone.

gdc.instructions: Set angle units to degrees. Calculate 25 * sin(40).

ti84.instructions: Ensure the calculator is in DEGREE mode. Press [25] [*] [SIN] [40] [ENTER].

desmos.instructions: Switch to degree mode in settings and type the expression.

calculator.description: Calculate the vertical component of the initial velocity.

steps.title: Use symmetrical flight time
steps.content:
Using the **Symmetrical Flight Time** approach, we recall that for a projectile returning to its original launch height, the total time of flight $T$ is given by:

$$T = \frac{2u \sin\theta}{g}$$

Because the parabolic path is symmetrical, the time taken to reach the maximum height (the apex) is exactly half of the total time for a level-ground flight. Therefore:

$$t_{\text{apex}} = \frac{T}{2} = \frac{u \sin\theta}{g}$$

This formula comes from the kinematic equation $$v = u + at$$, where at the peak, vertical velocity $v_y = 0$.

steps.title: Calculate the final time
steps.content:
Now we substitute the values into our simplified formula to find the time to reach the apex:

$$\begin{aligned} t_{\text{apex}} &= \frac{25.0 \cdot \sin(40.0^\circ)}{9.81} \\ t_{\text{apex}} &= \frac{16.07}{9.81} \\ t_{\text{apex}} &\approx 1.64\text{ s} \end{aligned}$$

This matches the markscheme requirement for calculating the vertical component and the final time correctly.

desmos.instructions: Type the expression to find the time.

calculator.description: Calculate the time to reach the maximum height.

parts.content: Determine the total time the stone is in the air before hitting the ground.
parts.markscheme:
- Use $s = u_y t + \frac{1}{2} a t^2$ with downward displacement $s = -50.0\ \text{m}$.
- $u_y = 16.1\ \text{m s}^{-1}$, $a = -9.81\ \text{m s}^{-2}$
- $-50.0 = 16.1 t - 4.905 t^2$
- Rewriting: $4.905 t^2 - 16.1 t - 50.0 = 0$
- Use quadratic formula:
$$t = \frac{16.1 \pm \sqrt{(-16.1)^2 - 4(4.905)(-50.0)}}{2(4.905)}$$
- $t = 5.23\ \text{s}$ (positive root)
- Correct setup and substitution into quadratic <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Correct use of formula and calculation of discriminant <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Correct final time with unit <marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Calculate vertical initial velocity
steps.content:
To solve for time using vertical motion, we first need the initial vertical component of the velocity ($u_y$). Since the stone is thrown at an angle of $40.0^\circ$ above the horizontal:

$$u_y = u \sin \theta$$
$$u_y = 25.0 \sin(40.0^\circ) \approx 16.1 \text{ m s}^{-1}$$

steps.title: Set up kinematic equation
steps.content:
We use the kinematic equation for displacement. For the vertical direction, we'll take **upwards as positive** and **downwards as negative**. 

- The displacement ($s$) is $-50.0 \text{ m}$ (since it ends up $50 \text{ m}$ below the starting point).
- The acceleration ($a$) is $-9.81 \text{ m s}^{-2}$.

Using the formula from the data booklet:

$$s = ut + \frac{1}{2}at^2$$

steps.title: Formulate the quadratic equation
steps.content:
Substitute the known values into the equation:

$$-50.0 = 16.1t + \frac{1}{2}(-9.81)t^2$$
$$-50.0 = 16.1t - 4.905t^2$$

Rearrange this into the standard quadratic form $at^2 + bt + c = 0$:

$$4.905t^2 - 16.1t - 50.0 = 0$$

steps.title: Solve for time
steps.content:
Using the quadratic formula $t = \frac{-b \pm \sqrt{b^2 - 4ac}}{2a}$:

$$t = \frac{16.1 \pm \sqrt{(-16.1)^2 - 4(4.905)(-50.0)}}{2(4.905)}$$

Calculating the discriminant:
$$(-16.1)^2 - 4(4.905)(-50.0) = 259.21 + 981 = 1240.21$$

$$t = \frac{16.1 \pm \sqrt{1240.21}}{9.81}$$

This gives two solutions:
- $t \approx 5.23 \text{ s}$
- $t \approx -1.95 \text{ s}$ (ignore as time cannot be negative)

gdc.instructions: Use the equation solver (Poly Root Finder) or graph Y1 = 4.905x^2 - 16.1x - 50 and find the positive x-intercept.

ti84.instructions: Go to [APPS], select 'PlySmlt2', then 'Polynomial Root Finder'. Set Order to 2. Enter a2=4.905, a1=-16.1, a0=-50. Press [SOLVE].

desmos.instructions: Type the equation into the input bar and identify the x-coordinate of the positive intercept.

calculator.description: Solve the quadratic equation using a graphing calculator.

steps.title: Final Result
steps.content:
The total time the stone is in the air is **$5.23 \text{ s}$**.

In the IB markscheme, marks are awarded for:
1. Substituting correctly into the kinematic equation.
2. Correct calculation of the discriminant/quadratic formula.
3. Providing the final answer to 3 significant figures with units.

steps.title: Resolve initial vertical velocity
steps.content:
To find the total time, we first focus only on the vertical motion. We need to find the initial vertical component of the velocity ($u_y$) using trigonometry.

$$u_y = u \sin(\theta)$$
$$u_y = 25.0 \times \sin(40.0^\circ)$$

Using the calculator:
$$u_y \approx 16.07 \text{ m s}^{-1}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the initial vertical velocity component.

steps.title: Find final vertical velocity
steps.content:
Using the **Velocity-First Method**, we calculate the vertical velocity of the stone just before it hits the ground ($v_y$). We use the kinematic equation for motion with constant acceleration from the data booklet:

$$v^2 = u^2 + 2as$$

Here, the displacement $s$ is $-50.0 \text{ m}$ (downward from the launch point) and the acceleration $a$ is $-9.8 \text{ m s}^{-2}$.

$$\begin{aligned} v_y^2 &= (16.07)^2 + 2(-9.8)(-50.0) \\ v_y^2 &= 258.24 + 980 \\ v_y^2 &= 1238.24 \\ v_y &= -\sqrt{1238.24} \approx -35.19 \text{ m s}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

*Note: We take the negative square root because the stone is moving downwards upon impact.*

calculator.description: Calculate the final vertical velocity squared and then its root.

steps.title: Calculate total time
steps.content:
Now that we have the initial and final vertical velocities, we can solve for the total time ($t$) using the simplest kinematic equation:

$$v = u + at$$

Rearranging for $t$:

$$\begin{aligned} t &= \frac{v_y - u_y}{a} \\ t &= \frac{-35.19 - 16.07}{-9.8} \\ t &= \frac{-51.26}{-9.8} \\ t &\approx 5.23 \text{ s} \end{aligned}$$

This multi-step approach avoids the need for the quadratic formula while arriving at the same final result required by the markscheme.

To find the total time, we first need the vertical component of the initial velocity ($u_y$). The stone is thrown at an angle, so we use trigonometry to find the part of the velocity that points upwards.

$$\begin{aligned} u_y &= u \sin\theta \\ u_y &= 25.0 \times \sin(40.0^\circ) \\ u_y &\approx 16.1 \text{ m s}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the initial vertical velocity component.

steps.title: Time to maximum height
steps.content:
In Stage 1, we find how long it takes for the stone to reach its highest point. At this point, the vertical velocity is zero ($v_y = 0$). We use the kinematic equation:

$$v = u + at$$

Substituting our values ($a = -9.81 \text{ m s}^{-2}$ for upward motion):

$$\begin{aligned} 0 &= 16.1 + (-9.81)t_1 \\ t_1 &= \frac{16.1}{9.81} \\ t_1 &\approx 1.64 \text{ s} \end{aligned}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the time to reach maximum height.

steps.title: Calculate maximum height
steps.content:
Next, we find the distance the stone travels upwards from the cliff edge to calculate the total height above the ground. We use:

$$v^2 = u^2 + 2as$$

$$\begin{aligned} 0^2 &= (16.1)^2 + 2(-9.81)s_{up} \\ s_{up} &= \frac{16.1^2}{2 \times 9.81} \\ s_{up} &\approx 13.2 \text{ m} \end{aligned}$$

The total height ($H$) from the ground is the cliff height plus this upward distance:

$$H = 50.0 + 13.2 = 63.2 \text{ m}$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate the vertical displacement and add 50.

ti84.instructions: Enter 16.07^2 / (2 * 9.81). Then add 50 to the result.

calculator.description: Calculate the height gained above the cliff and the total height.

steps.title: Time to fall down
steps.content:
In Stage 2, the stone falls from rest ($u=0$) at the maximum height ($63.2 \text{ m}$) down to the ground. We use the displacement equation:

$$s = ut + \frac{1}{2}at^2$$

Taking downward as positive for this part ($s = 63.2 \text{ m}$, $a = 9.81 \text{ m s}^{-2}$):

$$\begin{aligned} 63.2 &= 0(t_2) + \frac{1}{2}(9.81)t_2^2 \\ t_2^2 &= \frac{2 \times 63.2}{9.81} \\ t_2 &= \sqrt{\frac{126.4}{9.81}} \\ t_2 &\approx 3.59 \text{ s} \end{aligned}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the time to fall from maximum height.

steps.title: Calculate total time
steps.content:
Finally, we add the time taken to go up ($t_1$) and the time taken to fall down ($t_2$) to find the total time in the air.

$$\begin{aligned} t_{total} &= t_1 + t_2 \\ t_{total} &= 1.64 + 3.59 \\ t_{total} &= 5.23 \text{ s} \end{aligned}$$

This multi-stage method verifies the positive root found using the quadratic approach shown in the markscheme.

calculator.description: Sum the two time stages.

parts.content: Calculate the horizontal range of the stone from the base of the cliff.
parts.markscheme:
- $u_x = 25.0 \cos(40.0^\circ) = 19.2\ \text{m s}^{-1}$
- $\text{Range} = u_x \cdot t = 19.2 \cdot 5.23 = 100\ \text{m}$
- Correct horizontal velocity <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Correct final answer with unit <marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Resolve initial velocity components
steps.content:
To solve projectile motion problems, we first resolve the initial velocity into horizontal ($u_x$) and vertical ($u_y$) components using trigonometry:

$$\begin{aligned} u_x &= u \cos \theta = 25.0 \cos(40.0^\circ) \approx 19.2 \text{ m s}^{-1} \\ u_y &= u \sin \theta = 25.0 \sin(40.0^\circ) \approx 16.1 \text{ m s}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

Finding the correct horizontal velocity is the first step toward earning marks.

steps.title: Set up vertical displacement equation
steps.content:
We need to find the total time the stone is in the air. Since the stone lands $50.0 \text{ m}$ below its starting point, the vertical displacement is $s = -50.0 \text{ m}$ (taking upwards as positive). 

We use the kinematic equation from the **Mechanics** section of the data booklet:

$$s = ut + \frac{1}{2}at^2$$

Substituting our vertical values ($u = 16.07 \text{ m s}^{-1}$ and $a = -g = -9.8 \text{ m s}^{-2}$):

$$-50.0 = 16.07t - 4.9t^2$$

Rearranging into a standard quadratic form ($at^2 + bt + c = 0$):

$$4.9t^2 - 16.07t - 50.0 = 0$$

steps.title: Solve for time of flight
steps.content:
Using the quadratic formula or your GDC, solve for $t$. 

$$t = \frac{-b \pm \sqrt{b^2 - 4ac}}{2a}$$

Since time must be positive, we find:

$$t \approx 5.23 \text{ s}$$

gdc.instructions: Go to the 'Equation' or 'Solver' menu. Select 'Polynomial' then 'Degree 2'. Enter the coefficients a=4.9, b=-16.07, c=-50. Solve for roots.

ti84.instructions: Press [APPS], select 'PlySmlt2', then 'Polynomial Root Finder'. Set Order to 2. Enter a2=4.9, a1=-16.07, a0=-50. Press [SOLVE].

desmos.instructions: Type the quadratic equation and look for the positive x-intercept.

calculator.description: Solve the quadratic equation 4.9t² - 16.07t - 50 = 0 to find the time of flight.

steps.title: Calculate horizontal range
steps.content:
In the horizontal direction, acceleration is zero, so the range is simply the horizontal velocity multiplied by the total time:

$$\text{Range} = u_x \cdot t$$
$$\text{Range} = 19.15 \times 5.23 \approx 100 \text{ m}$$

This final value, provided with the correct units, earns the final mark.

steps.title: Resolve initial velocity components
steps.content:
To solve this projectile motion problem, we first split the initial velocity into its horizontal ($u_x$) and vertical ($u_y$) components. 

$$\begin{aligned} u_x &= 25.0 \cos(40.0^\circ) \approx 19.15\ \text{m s}^{-1} \\ u_y &= 25.0 \sin(40.0^\circ) \approx 16.07\ \text{m s}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

This uses concepts from **Topic A.1.3 Projectile motion**. Note that the horizontal velocity remains constant throughout the flight since air resistance is negligible.

gdc.instructions: Set to degrees. Enter 25 cos(40) and 25 sin(40).

ti84.instructions: Ensure the calculator is in Degree mode (MODE > Degree). Calculate 25*cos(40) and 25*sin(40).

desmos.instructions: Set to degrees. Type 25 cos(40) and 25 sin(40).

calculator.description: Calculate initial velocity components.

steps.title: Calculate impact vertical velocity
steps.content:
Using the **Impact Velocity Approach**, we first find the vertical velocity just before the stone hits the ground. We use the kinematic equation from the data booklet:

$$v^2 = u^2 + 2as$$

Taking upwards as positive, the displacement ($s$) is $-50.0\ \text{m}$ (downwards) and the acceleration ($a$) is $-9.8\ \text{m s}^{-2}$.

$$\begin{aligned} v_y^2 &= (16.07)^2 + 2(-9.8)(-50.0) \\ v_y^2 &= 258.2 + 980 = 1238.2 \\ v_y &= -\sqrt{1238.2} \approx -35.19\ \text{m s}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

The velocity is negative because the stone is moving downwards at impact.

ti84.instructions: Enter (16.07)^2 + 2*(-9.8)*(-50). Then find the square root of the result.

calculator.description: Calculate final vertical velocity squared and then its square root.

steps.title: Determine total flight time
steps.content:
Now we use the vertical velocity-time relation to find the total time of flight ($t$):

$$v = u + at$$

Substitute the known vertical values:

$$\begin{aligned} -35.19 &= 16.07 + (-9.8)t \\ -51.26 &= -9.8t \\ t &= \frac{-51.26}{-9.8} \approx 5.23\ \text{s} \end{aligned}$$

calculator.description: Solve for time t.

steps.title: Calculate horizontal range
steps.content:
Finally, the horizontal range is the product of the constant horizontal velocity and the total flight time:

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Range} &= u_x \cdot t \\ \text{Range} &= 19.15 \cdot 5.23 \approx 100.15\ \text{m} \end{aligned}$$

Rounding to three significant figures (matching the precision of the inputs), the range is **$100\ \text{m}$**.

In an IB exam, you would earn one mark for the correct horizontal velocity calculation and one mark for the final answer with units.

calculator.description: Calculate range.

steps.title: Resolve velocity components
steps.content:
To calculate the range, we first need the horizontal and vertical components of the initial velocity. The stone is thrown at a speed of $25.0\text{ m s}^{-1}$ at an angle of $40.0^\circ$.

$$\begin{aligned} u_x &= 25.0 \cos(40.0^\circ) \approx 19.15\text{ m s}^{-1} \\ u_y &= 25.0 \sin(40.0^\circ) \approx 16.07\text{ m s}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate the sine and cosine components.

ti84.instructions: Ensure mode is in DEGREES. Calculate 25*cos(40) and 25*sin(40).

desmos.instructions: Set to degrees mode and enter the equations.

calculator.description: Calculate the velocity components.

steps.title: Calculate time to peak
steps.content:
We calculate the time $t_1$ taken to reach the maximum height. At the peak, the vertical velocity is $v_y = 0$. Using the kinematic equation from the data booklet:

$$v = u + at$$

Substituting $v_y = 0$, $u_y = 16.07\text{ m s}^{-1}$, and $a = -9.8\text{ m s}^{-2}$:

$$\begin{aligned} 0 &= 16.07 - 9.8t_1 \\ t_1 &= \frac{16.07}{9.8} \approx 1.64\text{ s} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Find peak height
steps.content:
Next, we find the maximum height above the cliff level ($s_y$). Using another kinematic equation:

$$v^2 = u^2 + 2as$$

$$\begin{aligned} 0 &= (16.07)^2 + 2(-9.8)s_y \\ s_y &= \frac{(16.07)^2}{2 \times 9.8} \approx 13.18\text{ m} \end{aligned}$$

The total height $H$ from the base of the cliff is the height of the cliff plus this distance:

$$H = 50.0\text{ m} + 13.18\text{ m} = 63.18\text{ m}$$

steps.title: Calculate time to fall
steps.content:
Now, we find the time $t_2$ for the stone to fall from the peak ($63.18\text{ m}$) to the ground. In this second phase, the initial vertical velocity is $u_{y,peak} = 0$. Using the displacement formula:

$$s = ut + \frac{1}{2}at^2$$

Taking downward as positive for this part:

$$\begin{aligned} 63.18 &= 0 + \frac{1}{2}(9.8)t_2^2 \\ t_2^2 &= \frac{2 \times 63.18}{9.8} \\ t_2 &= \sqrt{12.89} \approx 3.59\text{ s} \end{aligned}$$

desmos.instructions: Calculate the second part of the flight time.

calculator.description: Calculate the time to fall from the peak.

steps.title: Determine total range
steps.content:
The total time of flight $T$ is the sum of the time to reach the peak and the time to fall from the peak:

$$T = t_1 + t_2 = 1.64 + 3.59 = 5.23\text{ s}$$

The horizontal range is calculated by multiplying the constant horizontal velocity by the total time:

$$\text{Range} = u_x \times T = 19.15 \times 5.23 \approx 100.15\text{ m}$$

Rounded to three significant figures, the horizontal range is $100\text{ m}$. This confirms the markscheme's result.

gdc.instructions: Multiply horizontal velocity by total time.

ti84.instructions: Multiply horizontal velocity by total time.

calculator.description: Calculate final range.

parts.content: Suggest why the actual maximum height reached by the stone is less than the theoretical maximum height.
parts.markscheme: Because air resistance would reduce the vertical velocity more than predicted, lowering the actual maximum height. <marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Identify the opposing force
steps.content: In the theoretical model, we assume that **air resistance is negligible**. However, in a real-world scenario, the stone must move through the air, which exerts a resistive force (drag) on the stone. This concept is covered in **Topic A.1.4 Fluid resistance**.

steps.title: Determine the net force
steps.content: As the stone moves upwards, air resistance acts in the direction opposite to its motion—meaning it acts **downwards**. This adds to the force of gravity, increasing the total net downward force acting on the stone.

steps.title: Link force to acceleration
steps.content:
According to Newton’s Second Law, the acceleration of an object is proportional to the net force acting on it:

$$F = ma$$

Because the net downward force is now greater than the weight alone, the stone undergoes a **greater downward acceleration** than it would in a vacuum. This causes the vertical velocity to decrease to zero more rapidly.

steps.title: Evaluate the maximum height
steps.content:
We can see the relationship between acceleration ($a$) and displacement ($s$) using the kinematic equation:

$$v^2 = u^2 + 2as$$

At the maximum height, the final vertical velocity ($v$) is $0$. With a larger downward acceleration ($a$), the stone reaches $v=0$ over a **smaller vertical displacement**, resulting in a lower maximum height than the theoretical prediction.

steps.title: Recall conservation of energy
steps.content:
In the theoretical model where we assume air resistance is negligible, the stone follows the principle of conservation of energy. As the stone rises, its initial kinetic energy ($E_k$) is converted into gravitational potential energy ($\Delta E_p$). At the maximum height, the vertical component of kinetic energy has been fully converted into potential energy.

This relationship is described by the equations found in the **Mechanics** section of your data booklet:

$$\Delta E_p = mg\Delta h$$ 

and 

$$E_k = \frac{1}{2}mv^2$$

steps.title: Account for air resistance
steps.content:
In reality, air resistance is a resistive force that opposes the motion of the stone. As the stone moves through the air, it must do work against this force. This concept is covered in **Topic A.3: Work, energy and power**.

The work done against air resistance is calculated using:

$$W = Fs\cos\theta$$

steps.title: Analyze energy transfer
steps.content:
When work is done against air resistance, some of the stone's initial mechanical energy is converted into **thermal energy** (heat) transferred to the stone and the surrounding air. 

Because energy is "lost" to the environment as heat, the total mechanical energy available to be converted into gravitational potential energy decreases.

steps.title: Connect to maximum height
steps.content:
Since there is less energy available to be stored as gravitational potential energy ($mgh$), the stone cannot reach the same vertical displacement ($h$) as it would in a vacuum. 

Therefore, the actual maximum height reached is less than the theoretical height because work done against air resistance reduces the energy available for height gain.

questions.specification: A ball is projected upwards on Earth's surface. It reaches the same height at $t=1.0\text{ s}$ and $t=3.0\text{ s}$. What is the initial velocity at which it was thrown? Take $g=10\text{ m s}^{-2}$.

options.markscheme:
Time to reach maximum height is the average of the two times due to symmetry:
$$t_{\text{peak}} = \frac{1.0 + 3.0}{2} = 2.0\text{ s}$$
At maximum height, $v = 0$. Using $v = u - gt$:
$$0 = u - 10(2.0)$$
$$u = 20\text{ m s}^{-1}$$

questions.specification: A car is initially traveling at a speed of ${33.2 \text{ m/s}}$. The driver applies the brakes, causing the car to decelerate uniformly at a rate of ${2 \text{ m/s}^2}$ until it comes to a complete stop.

labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.1.2 Equations of motion
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Uses constant (uniform) deceleration to find stopping distance and stopping time via SUVAT/kinematics equations; also validates via v–t graph area which relies on these relations.
