parts.content: Find the deceleration required for the cyclist to stop in 6 seconds.
parts.markscheme:
- Use of the equation $v = u + at$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Substitute values ($v=0, u=18, t=6$): $0 = 18 + a \times 6$ 

- Solve for $a$: $a = -\frac{18}{6} = -3 \, \text{m/s}^2$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

<note>The negative sign indicates deceleration.</note>

steps.title: Strategy overview
steps.content:
To find the deceleration in the final phase, we first need to know the cyclist's speed just before they started breaking. 

We will find this by calculating the maximum speed reached during the initial acceleration phase. Since the cyclist maintains a constant speed in the middle phase, this maximum speed becomes the **initial velocity ($u$)** for the final deceleration phase.

steps.title: Calculate maximum speed
steps.content:
First, we find the speed reached after the first 12 seconds using the kinematic equation for velocity.

From the question, we know:
*   Initial velocity $u = 0 \text{ m/s}$ (starts from rest)
*   Acceleration $a = 1.5 \text{ m/s}^2$
*   Time $t = 12 \text{ s}$

Using the formula:
$$v = u + at$$

Substituting the values:
$$\begin{aligned} v &= 0 + (1.5)(12) \\ v &= 18 \text{ m/s} \end{aligned}$$

This speed of $18 \text{ m/s}$ is maintained during the middle phase and serves as the starting speed for the final stop.

calculator.description: Calculate the velocity after the acceleration phase

highlights.text: accelerates uniformly at ${1.5 	ext{ m/s}^2}$ for the first 12 seconds

steps.title: Identify deceleration variables
steps.content:
Now we analyze the final phase where the cyclist comes to a halt.

We define the variables for this 6-second interval:
*   Initial velocity $u = 18 \text{ m/s}$ (calculated in previous step)
*   Final velocity $v = 0 \text{ m/s}$ (comes to a stop)
*   Time $t = 6 \text{ s}$

steps.title: Solve for deceleration
steps.content:
We use the same kinematic equation to solve for the acceleration $a$.

$$v = u + at$$

Substitute the values ($v=0, u=18, t=6$):
$$0 = 18 + a(6)$$

Rearranging to solve for $a$:
$$\begin{aligned} -18 &= 6a \\ a &= \frac{-18}{6} \\ a &= -3 \text{ m/s}^2 \end{aligned}$$

calculator.description: Solve for acceleration

steps.title: State final answer
steps.content:
The calculated acceleration is $-3 \text{ m/s}^2$. The negative sign indicates the direction is opposite to the motion (deceleration).

The deceleration required is:
$$3 \text{ m/s}^2$$

steps.title: Analyze the velocity change
steps.content:
The cyclist accelerates from rest to a maximum speed and later decelerates from that same speed back to a stop. This means the magnitude of the velocity gained during the first phase must equal the magnitude of the velocity lost during the final phase.

$$|\Delta v_{\text{acceleration}}| = |\Delta v_{\text{deceleration}}|$$

steps.title: Set up the proportionality
steps.content:
From the equations of motion in **Topic A.1**, we know that for motion starting or ending at rest, the change in velocity is given by the product of acceleration and time:

$$v = u + at \implies |\Delta v| = |a| \times t$$

Since the velocity change $|\Delta v|$ is the same for both phases, the acceleration magnitude $|a|$ is inversely proportional to the time duration $t$.

steps.title: Compare the time durations
steps.content:
We compare the time taken to accelerate with the time taken to stop:
- Acceleration time: $t_{\text{acc}} = 12 \text{ s}$
- Deceleration time: $t_{\text{dec}} = 6 \text{ s}$

The stopping time is exactly half of the acceleration time:

$$\frac{t_{\text{acc}}}{t_{\text{dec}}} = \frac{12}{6} = 2$$

calculator.description: Calculate the ratio of acceleration time to deceleration time

steps.title: Calculate the deceleration
steps.content:
Since acceleration is inversely proportional to time, if the time is halved, the acceleration magnitude must double to achieve the same change in velocity.

$$|a_{\text{dec}}| = 2 \times |a_{\text{acc}}|$$

Substituting the given acceleration of $1.5 \text{ m/s}^2$:

$$|a_{\text{dec}}| = 2 \times 1.5$$
$$|a_{\text{dec}}| = 3 \text{ m/s}^2$$

calculator.description: Calculate the final deceleration value

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The deceleration required is $3 \text{ m/s}^2$.

(Note: In kinematic equations, this would appear as an acceleration of $-3 \text{ m/s}^2$, as indicated in the markscheme, because it opposes the direction of motion.)

steps.title: Visualize the velocity-time graph
steps.content:
To solve this using **Velocity-Time Graph Analysis**, visualize the motion as a graph with velocity on the y-axis and time on the x-axis:

1. **Phase 1 (0-12s)**: An upward sloping line starting from the origin (accelerating).
2. **Phase 2 (12-20s)**: A horizontal line (constant speed).
3. **Phase 3 (20-26s)**: A downward sloping line returning to the x-axis (decelerating).

We need to find the gradient of the line in **Phase 3**, which represents the deceleration. To do this, we first need the "height" of the graph (the peak velocity).

steps.title: Calculate peak velocity
steps.content:
The peak velocity is reached at the end of the first phase. The gradient of the velocity-time graph represents acceleration. We can calculate the change in velocity (height) using the gradient ($a$) and the time interval ($t$).

Using the kinematic definition from **Topic A.1**:

$$v = u + at$$

Since the cyclist starts from rest ($u=0$), the peak velocity is:

$$v_{peak} = 1.5 \text{ m/s}^2 \times 12 \text{ s}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the peak velocity attained after the first 12 seconds.

highlights.text: accelerates uniformly at ${1.5 	ext{ m/s}^2}$ for the first 12 seconds

steps.title: State peak velocity
steps.content:
Perform the calculation:

$$v_{peak} = 18 \text{ m/s}$$

This is the velocity at the start of the deceleration phase.

steps.title: Determine gradient for final phase
steps.content:
Now look at the final 6 seconds on our graph. The line goes from a height of $v = 18 \text{ m/s}$ down to $v = 0 \text{ m/s}$ (since it stops).

The deceleration is the gradient (slope) of this line:

$$\text{gradient} = \frac{\text{change in velocity}}{\text{change in time}} = \frac{v_{final} - v_{initial}}{\Delta t}$$

Substitute the values:

$$a = \frac{0 - 18}{6}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the acceleration (gradient) for the final phase.

highlights.text: decelerates uniformly to a stop in 6 seconds

steps.title: Calculate final answer
steps.content:
Solving for $a$:

$$a = -3 \text{ m/s}^2$$

The negative sign indicates the direction is opposite to motion. The question asks for deceleration, which refers to the magnitude of this negative acceleration.

**Answer:** The deceleration is $3 \text{ m/s}^2$.

parts.content: Determine the total distance covered by the cyclist from start to stop.
parts.markscheme:
- Calculate distance for deceleration phase using $s = ut + \frac{1}{2}at^2$ (or alternative method) <marks>M1</marks>

- Substitute values: $s = 18 \times 6 + \frac{1}{2} \times (-3) \times (6^2) = 108 - 54 = 54 \, \text{m}$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Calculate total distance: distance during acceleration + distance at constant speed + distance during deceleration = $108 + 144 + 54 = 306 \, \text{m}$ <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

steps.title: Break down the motion
steps.content:
The cyclist's motion consists of three distinct phases. We need to calculate the distance traveled in each phase separately and sum them to find the total distance.

1.  **Phase 1:** Uniform acceleration from rest ($t = 12 \text{ s}$). 
2.  **Phase 2:** Constant speed ($t = 8 \text{ s}$). 
3.  **Phase 3:** Uniform deceleration to a stop ($t = 6 \text{ s}$).

steps.title: Phase 1: Acceleration distance
steps.content:
First, we calculate the maximum speed reached and the distance covered ($s_1$) during the acceleration phase. We use the kinematic equations for constant acceleration starting from rest ($u=0$, $a=1.5$, $t=12$).

Find the final velocity ($v$):
$$v = u + at$$
$$v = 0 + (1.5)(12) = 18 \text{ m/s}$$

Find the distance ($s_1$):
$$s = ut + \frac{1}{2}at^2$$
$$s_1 = (0)(12) + \frac{1}{2}(1.5)(12^2)$$
$$s_1 = 0.5 \times 1.5 \times 144 = 108 \text{ m}$$

calculator.description: Calculate Phase 1 distance

steps.title: Phase 2: Constant speed distance
steps.content:
For the next $8$ seconds, the cyclist maintains the speed reached at the end of Phase 1 ($v = 18 \text{ m/s}$). Since acceleration is zero, we use the basic distance formula:

$$s_2 = v \times t$$
$$s_2 = 18 \times 8 = 144 \text{ m}$$

calculator.description: Calculate Phase 2 distance

steps.title: Phase 3: Deceleration distance
steps.content:
Finally, the cyclist decelerates from $u = 18 \text{ m/s}$ to a stop ($v = 0$) in $6$ seconds. To use the equation specified in the markscheme ($s = ut + \frac{1}{2}at^2$), we first find the deceleration ($a$).

Calculate acceleration ($a$):
$$a = \frac{v - u}{t} = \frac{0 - 18}{6} = -3 \text{ m/s}^2$$

Calculate distance ($s_3$):
$$s_3 = ut + \frac{1}{2}at^2$$
$$s_3 = 18(6) + \frac{1}{2}(-3)(6^2)$$
$$s_3 = 108 + (-54) = 54 \text{ m}$$

This calculation earns the first method mark (**M1**) and accuracy mark (**A1**).

calculator.description: Calculate Phase 3 distance

highlights.text: Calculate distance for deceleration phase using $s = ut + \frac{1}{2}at^2$

steps.title: Calculate total distance
steps.content:
Add the distances calculated for all three phases to find the total distance covered.

$$s_{\text{total}} = s_1 + s_2 + s_3$$
$$s_{\text{total}} = 108 + 144 + 54 = 306 \text{ m}$$

This final sum earns the last accuracy mark (**A1**).

calculator.description: Sum all distances

steps.title: Method Strategy
steps.content:
To solve this using the **Velocity-Time Graph Area** approach, recall that the total distance covered by an object corresponds to the **area under its velocity-time graph**.

We need to:
1. Find the maximum velocity (the height of our graph).
2. Calculate the area of each section of the motion (acceleration, constant speed, deceleration).
3. Sum these areas to find the total distance.

This concept is part of **Topic A.1 Kinematics**.

steps.title: Calculate maximum velocity
steps.content:
First, we determine the height of the graph by finding the final velocity after the acceleration phase. The cyclist starts from rest ($u=0$) and accelerates at $1.5 \text{ m/s}^2$ for 12 seconds.

Using the kinematic equation from the **Mechanics** section of the data booklet:

$$v = u + at$$

Substituting the values:

$$v = 0 + (1.5)(12)$$

$$v = 18 \text{ m/s}$$

calculator.description: Calculate maximum velocity

steps.title: Visualizing the graph
steps.content:
Imagine the velocity-time graph. It has three distinct shapes based on the motion:

1.  **Triangle**: From $t=0$ to $t=12$, velocity rises linearly from 0 to 18 m/s.
2.  **Rectangle**: For the next 8 seconds ($t=12$ to $t=20$), velocity stays constant at 18 m/s.
3.  **Triangle**: For the final 6 seconds, velocity drops linearly from 18 m/s to 0.

We will calculate the area of each shape.

steps.title: Area 1: Acceleration
steps.content:
The first section is a triangle with base 12 s and height 18 m/s.

$$\text{Area}_1 = \frac{1}{2} \times \text{base} \times \text{height}$$

$$\text{Area}_1 = \frac{1}{2} \times 12 \times 18 = 108 \text{ m}$$

calculator.description: Calculate area of the first triangle

steps.title: Area 2: Constant speed
steps.content:
The second section is a rectangle with base 8 s and height 18 m/s.

$$\text{Area}_2 = \text{base} \times \text{height}$$

$$\text{Area}_2 = 8 \times 18 = 144 \text{ m}$$

calculator.description: Calculate area of the rectangle

steps.title: Area 3: Deceleration
steps.content:
The final section is a triangle with base 6 s and height 18 m/s (starting at 18 and ending at 0).

$$\text{Area}_3 = \frac{1}{2} \times \text{base} \times \text{height}$$

$$\text{Area}_3 = \frac{1}{2} \times 6 \times 18 = 54 \text{ m}$$

calculator.description: Calculate area of the second triangle

steps.title: Calculate total distance
steps.content:
Add the areas of all three sections to find the total distance traveled.

$$\text{Total Distance} = 108 + 144 + 54 = 306 \text{ m}$$

calculator.description: Sum the areas

Which of the following expressions represents the speed of the particle after it has travelled the total distance of $$2s$$?

questions.specification: A drone is flown in the following path: It starts at point A and flies 8 km due north to point B. From point B, it turns and flies 6 km due east to point C. Finally, the drone changes direction and flies 10 km due south to point D.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic A - Space, time and motion > A.1 Kinematics > A.1.1 Describing motion quantitatively and qualitatively
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.1.1 Describing motion quantitatively and qualitatively
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: All parts focus on interpreting a multi-leg journey in terms of distance vs displacement, displacement magnitude and direction (vector), and relating these to average speed and average velocity—core quantitative/qualitative description of motion.

parts.content: Calculate the total distance the drone flies.
parts.markscheme:
- Calculate the sum of the distances: $8 + 6 + 10 = 24$ km <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Define distance as a scalar
steps.content:
In IB Physics, it is important to distinguish between **distance** and **displacement**. Distance is a scalar quantity, meaning it only has magnitude (size) and no direction. 

This concept is part of **Topic A.1: Kinematics**. To find the total distance, we simply sum the lengths of every segment the drone traveled, regardless of the direction.

steps.title: Identify the path segments
steps.content:
The drone's journey consists of three distinct segments. We need to identify the magnitude of each segment from the question:

*   Segment 1 ($d_1$): 8 km
*   Segment 2 ($d_2$): 6 km
*   Segment 3 ($d_3$): 10 km

steps.title: Sum the magnitudes
steps.content:
Using the **Scalar Summation of Path Segments** approach, we add the lengths of these segments together to find the total distance ($D$):

$$D = d_1 + d_2 + d_3$$

Substituting our values:

$$D = 8\text{ km} + 6\text{ km} + 10\text{ km}$$

gdc.instructions: In the calculation menu, type 8 + 6 + 10 and press EXE.

ti84.instructions: Enter 8 + 6 + 10 on the home screen and press ENTER.

calculator.description: Add the three distance segments to find the total sum.

steps.title: State the final distance
steps.content:
Calculating the sum gives us the total path length:

$$D = 24\text{ km}$$

In the IB markscheme, you receive 1 mark (A1) for the correct final value with units.

parts.content: Determine the magnitude of the drone’s displacement from point A to point D.
parts.markscheme:
- Calculate net north-south distance: $8 - 10 = -2$ km (2 km south) <marks>M1</marks>

- Use Pythagorean theorem to find displacement magnitude: $\text{displacement} = \sqrt{2^2 + 6^2} = \sqrt{4 + 36} = \sqrt{40} \approx 6.32$ km <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

parts.content: What is the drone’s net displacement direction relative to due east?
parts.markscheme:
- Identify opposite (2 km south) and adjacent (6 km east) sides <marks>M1</marks>

- Calculate angle: $\theta = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{2}{6}\right) \approx 18.4^\circ$ south of east <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

<note>Accept answers between 18.3° and 18.5° due to rounding</note>

steps.title: Sum North-South components
steps.content:
To find the net displacement direction using the component summation approach, we first look at the movement in the North-South (vertical) direction. 

The drone moves 8 km North and later 10 km South. Taking North as positive and South as negative:

$$\text{Net vertical displacement} = +8\text{ km} - 10\text{ km} = -2\text{ km}$$

This result of $-2\text{ km}$ tells us the drone ends up **2 km South** of its starting latitude.

steps.title: Sum East-West components
steps.content:
Next, we sum the components in the East-West (horizontal) direction. 

The drone flies 6 km due East. Since there are no other East or West movements, the total horizontal displacement is simply:

$$\text{Net horizontal displacement} = 6\text{ km East}$$

steps.title: Set up the angle
steps.content:
We now have a right-angled triangle where the adjacent side is the East component (6 km) and the opposite side is the South component (2 km). 

To find the direction relative to due East, we use the tangent function from basic trigonometry:

$$\tan(\theta) = \frac{\text{opposite}}{\text{adjacent}} = \frac{2}{6}$$

To solve for $\theta$, we take the inverse tangent:

$$\theta = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{2}{6}\right)$$ 

This angle represents how many degrees **South of East** the drone is located.

steps.title: Calculate the direction
steps.content: Now, use your calculator to find the numerical value of the angle. This step earns the final mark for accuracy.

gdc.instructions:
1. Check that the angle setting is in Degrees.
2. Use the inverse tangent function (usually atan or tan⁻¹).
3. Enter 2/6.

ti84.instructions:
1. Ensure the calculator is in Degree mode: Press [MODE], highlight 'DEGREE', and press [ENTER].
2. Press [2nd] [TAN] (to get tan⁻¹).
3. Type (2 / 6) and press [ENTER].

desmos.instructions:
1. Click the wrench icon (Settings) and select 'Degrees'.
2. Type 'arctan(2/6)' or use the keypad to find tan⁻¹ under the functions menu.

calculator.description: Calculate the inverse tangent of (2/6) to find the angle in degrees.

steps.title: Final answer
steps.content:
From the calculation:

$$\theta = \tan^{-1}(0.333...) \approx 18.4^\circ$$

Because the net vertical component is South and the net horizontal component is East, the direction is **$18.4^\circ$ South of East**.

steps.title: Set up the coordinate plane
steps.content:
To solve this using coordinate geometry, let's place the starting point **A at the origin $(0, 0)$**. 

We will define the axes as follows:
- The **$x$-axis** represents the East-West direction (East is positive).
- The **$y$-axis** represents the North-South direction (North is positive).

steps.title: Calculate coordinates of Point D
steps.content:
Now, let's track the drone's movements to find the coordinates of point D:
1. **A to B**: 8 km due North $\rightarrow (0, 8)$
2. **B to C**: 6 km due East $\rightarrow (0 + 6, 8) = (6, 8)$
3. **C to D**: 10 km due South $\rightarrow (6, 8 - 10) = (6, -2)$

Point D is located at $(6, -2)$.

steps.title: Identify displacement components
steps.content:
The net displacement vector from A to D is $(6, -2)$. This gives us the side lengths of a right-angled triangle representing the displacement:
- **Adjacent side** (along the East axis): $6\text{ km}$
- **Opposite side** (along the South axis): $2\text{ km}$

Since the $y$-coordinate is negative, the direction is **South of East**.

steps.title: Calculate the angle
steps.content:
To find the angle $\theta$ relative to due east, we use the tangent function:

$$\tan \theta = \frac{\text{Opposite}}{\text{Adjacent}} = \frac{2}{6}$$

$$\theta = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{2}{6}\right)$$ 

This calculation helps determine how many degrees the displacement vector is tilted away from the East axis toward the South.

steps.title: Final calculation with GDC
steps.content:
Using a calculator, we find:
$$\theta \approx 18.4^\circ$$

Thus, the drone's net displacement direction is **$18.4^\circ$ South of East**.

gdc.instructions:
1. Set angle units to Degrees in the settings menu.
2. Use the 'atan' or 'tan⁻¹' function.
3. Input 2/6.

ti84.instructions:
1. Ensure the calculator is in DEGREE mode (press [MODE], highlight 'DEGREE', and press [ENTER]).
2. Press [2nd] [TAN].
3. Enter 2 / 6 and press [ENTER].

desmos.instructions:
1. Click the wrench icon (Graph Settings) and select 'Degrees'.
2. Type 'arctan(2/6)' or use the functions menu to select tan⁻¹.

calculator.description: Calculate the inverse tangent to find the angle in degrees.

parts.content: If the drone had flown directly from point A to point D, what distance would it have travelled?
parts.markscheme:
- State or calculate magnitude of displacement from A to D: $\sqrt{2^2 + 6^2} = 6.32$ km <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

parts.content: Compare the total distance flown to the magnitude of the displacement and explain why they differ.
parts.markscheme:
- State values: Distance is 24 km and displacement is 6.32 km <marks>A1</marks>

- Explain difference: Distance measures the total length of the path traveled while displacement measures the direct straight-line change in position <marks>R1</marks>

2 marks total

<note>Award A1 for correctly stating both numerical values. Award R1 for a clear explanation of the difference.</note>

parts.content: If the drone took 1.5 hours to complete the entire journey, calculate its average speed and average velocity.
parts.markscheme:
- Calculate average speed: $\text{speed} = \frac{\text{total distance}}{\text{total time}} = \frac{24}{1.5} = 16$ km/h <marks>M1</marks>

- Correct answer for average speed: 16 km/h <marks>A1</marks>

- Calculate average velocity: $\text{velocity} = \frac{\text{displacement}}{\text{total time}} = \frac{6.32}{1.5} \approx 4.21$ km/h at $18.4^\circ$ south of east <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

<note>Both magnitude and direction required for the average velocity mark. Accept 4.22 km/h (using exact calculation) or 4.21 km/h (using rounded displacement).</note>

steps.title: Calculate total distance and speed
steps.content:
Average speed is a scalar quantity defined as the total distance traveled divided by the total time. First, we find the total distance by adding each leg of the journey:

$$\text{Total Distance} = 8\text{ km} + 6\text{ km} + 10\text{ km} = 24\text{ km}$$

Now, we use the formula for average speed:

$$\text{Average Speed} = \frac{\text{Total Distance}}{\text{Total Time}} = \frac{24\text{ km}}{1.5\text{ h}} = 16\text{ km h}^{-1}$$

steps.title: Sketch path for displacement
steps.content:
Average velocity is a vector quantity, so we need the **displacement** (the straight-line change in position from A to D). Let's visualize the path on a coordinate plane starting at $A(0,0)$:

1. **A to B**: 8 km North $\rightarrow (0, 8)$
2. **B to C**: 6 km East $\rightarrow (6, 8)$
3. **C to D**: 10 km South $\rightarrow (6, 8 - 10) = (6, -2)$

The final position $D$ is **6 km East** and **2 km South** of the starting point $A$. This forms a right-angled triangle where the displacement is the hypotenuse.

steps.title: Calculate displacement magnitude
steps.content:
Using the Pythagorean theorem for the right-angled triangle with sides $x = 6$ and $y = 2$:

$$\text{Displacement} (s) = \sqrt{6^2 + 2^2} = \sqrt{36 + 4} = \sqrt{40} \approx 6.32\text{ km}$$

This calculation uses the fundamental geometric relationship for vectors in 2D space.

gdc.instructions: Enter sqrt(6^2 + 2^2) into the main calculation screen.

desmos.instructions: Type sqrt(6^2 + 2^2) into an expression cell.

calculator.description: Calculate the magnitude of the displacement using the square root of the sum of squares.

steps.title: Determine average velocity
steps.content:
Average velocity requires both magnitude and direction:

**Magnitude:**
$$\text{Average Velocity} = \frac{\text{Displacement}}{\text{Time}} = \frac{6.32\text{ km}}{1.5\text{ h}} \approx 4.21\text{ km h}^{-1}$$

**Direction:**
Using trigonometry on our triangle (opposite side = 2, adjacent side = 6):
$$\tan(\theta) = \frac{2}{6} \implies \theta = \arctan\left(\frac{1}{3}\right) \approx 18.4^\circ$$

Since the drone ended south of the eastward line, the direction is **$18.4^\circ$ South of East**.

gdc.instructions: Calculate 6.3245 / 1.5 for magnitude. Use the arctan or tan⁻¹ function for the angle.

ti84.instructions: For velocity: 6.3245 / 1.5 [ENTER]. For angle: Ensure mode is in DEGREES, then press [2nd] [tan] (tan⁻¹), type 2/6, and press [ENTER].

desmos.instructions: Type 6.3245 / 1.5 for magnitude. Type arctan(2/6) and ensure the setting is in Degrees.

calculator.description: Calculate the magnitude of velocity and the angle of displacement.

steps.title: Calculate average speed
steps.content:
Average speed is a scalar quantity found by dividing the total distance by the total time. First, we find the total distance covered by adding the magnitudes of all segments of the journey:

$$\text{Total Distance} = 8\text{ km} + 6\text{ km} + 10\text{ km} = 24\text{ km}$$

Now, we calculate the average speed:

$$\text{Average Speed} = \frac{\text{Total Distance}}{\text{Total Time}} = \frac{24\text{ km}}{1.5\text{ h}} = 16\text{ km h}^{-1}$$

gdc.instructions: Sum the distances and divide by 1.5.

ti84.instructions: Enter 8 + 6 + 10 to get the distance, then divide by 1.5.

desmos.instructions: Divide the sum of distances by time.

calculator.description: Calculate total distance and then average speed.

steps.title: Analyze displacement components
steps.content:
Average velocity is a vector, so we need the net displacement. Using the component analysis approach, we break the movement into East-West ($x$) and North-South ($y$) components:

- **East-West component ($\Delta x$):** The drone flies $+6\text{ km}$ (taking East as positive).
- **North-South component ($\Delta y$):** The drone flies $+8\text{ km}$ North and $-10\text{ km}$ South, giving $\Delta y = +8 - 10 = -2\text{ km}$.

The net displacement vector points 6 km East and 2 km South.

steps.title: Calculate displacement vector
steps.content:
To find the magnitude ($s$) of the resultant displacement, we apply the Pythagorean theorem:

$$s = \sqrt{6^2 + (-2)^2} = \sqrt{36 + 4} = \sqrt{40} \approx 6.32\text{ km}$$

To find the direction ($\theta$), we calculate the angle below the East axis (South of East):

$$\theta = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{|\text{opposite}|}{|\text{adjacent}|}\right) = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{2}{6}\right) \approx 18.4^\circ$$

ti84.instructions: Use square root for magnitude. For angle, ensure the calculator is in Degree mode and use tan⁻¹(2/6).

calculator.description: Calculate magnitude and direction of displacement.

steps.title: Calculate average velocity
steps.content:
Average velocity is the displacement divided by the total time. The direction of the average velocity is identical to the direction of the displacement.

- **Magnitude:**
$$\text{Average Velocity} = \frac{\text{Displacement Magnitude}}{\text{Total Time}} = \frac{6.32... \text{ km}}{1.5\text{ h}} \approx 4.21\text{ km h}^{-1}$$

- **Direction:**
As calculated previously, the direction is **$18.4^\circ$ South of East**.

ti84.instructions: Divide the magnitude of displacement (approx 6.32) by 1.5.

calculator.description: Calculate the final velocity magnitude.

parts.content: Suppose the drone retraced its path from point D back to point A. What would the total distance and total displacement be for the round trip?
parts.markscheme:
- State total distance: $24 + 24 = 48$ km <marks>A1</marks>

- State total displacement: 0 km (since drone returns to starting point) <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Calculate outward journey distance
steps.content:
To find the total distance, we first sum up each segment of the outward journey from point A to point D.

- A to B: $8\text{ km}$
- B to C: $6\text{ km}$
- C to D: $10\text{ km}$

$$\text{Outward distance} = 8 + 6 + 10 = 24\text{ km}$$

steps.title: Find total round-trip distance
steps.content:
The question states the drone retraces its path back to point A. This means it travels the same $24\text{ km}$ distance on the way back.

$$\text{Total distance} = 24\text{ km (outward)} + 24\text{ km (return)} = 48\text{ km}$$

This earns you the first mark **(A1)**.

gdc.instructions: Calculate the total distance by doubling the path sum.

ti84.instructions: Enter the sum of the segments and multiply by 2.

calculator.description: Double the outward distance to find the total distance.

highlights.text: retraced its path from point D back to point A

steps.title: Determine total displacement
steps.content:
In IB Physics, **displacement** is defined as the change in position of an object. This is a fundamental concept covered in **Topic A.1: Kinematics**.

Since the drone returns to its original starting point (point A), its final position is identical to its initial position. Therefore, the overall change in position is zero.

$$\text{Total displacement} = 0\text{ km}$$

This earns you the second mark **(A1)**.

steps.title: Define the outbound vectors
steps.content:
To solve this using vector analysis, we first represent each leg of the drone's outbound journey as a displacement vector. Let's use standard directions where North and East are positive, and South and West are negative.

From the question, we have:
- Leg 1 ($A$ to $B$): 8 km North
- Leg 2 ($B$ to $C$): 6 km East
- Leg 3 ($C$ to $D$): 10 km South

We can write these as vectors:
$$\begin{aligned} \vec{s}_1 &= +8\hat{j} \text{ km} \\ \vec{s}_2 &= +6\hat{i} \text{ km} \\ \vec{s}_3 &= -10\hat{j} \text{ km} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Define the return vectors
steps.content:
The question states the drone **retraced its path** from point $D$ back to point $A$. This means it travels the exact same legs but in the opposite direction and reverse order:

- Leg 4 ($D$ to $C$): 10 km North
- Leg 5 ($C$ to $B$): 6 km West
- Leg 6 ($B$ to $A$): 8 km South

As vectors, these are:
$$\begin{aligned} \vec{s}_4 &= +10\hat{j} \text{ km} \\ \vec{s}_5 &= -6\hat{i} \text{ km} \\ \vec{s}_6 &= -8\hat{j} \text{ km} \end{aligned}$$

highlights.text: retraced its path from point D back to point A

steps.title: Calculate total distance
steps.content:
Distance is a scalar quantity, so we sum the magnitudes of all individual displacement vectors. In IB Physics, remember that distance is the total path length traveled, regardless of direction.

$$\text{Total Distance} = |\vec{s}_1| + |\vec{s}_2| + |\vec{s}_3| + |\vec{s}_4| + |\vec{s}_5| + |\vec{s}_6|$$
$$\text{Total Distance} = 8 + 6 + 10 + 10 + 6 + 8 = 48 \text{ km}$$

ti84.instructions: Enter the sum of the magnitudes: 8 + 6 + 10 + 10 + 6 + 8

calculator.description: Calculate the sum of all path segments.

steps.title: Calculate total displacement
steps.content:
Displacement is a vector quantity defined as the change in position. For a round trip that returns to the starting point ($A$), the net displacement must be zero. 

We can prove this by finding the vector sum of all legs:
$$\vec{s}_{net} = \vec{s}_1 + \vec{s}_2 + \vec{s}_3 + \vec{s}_4 + \vec{s}_5 + \vec{s}_6$$

Summing the $\hat{i}$ (East-West) and $\hat{j}$ (North-South) components separately:
$$\begin{aligned} \text{Horizontal: } & (+6\hat{i}) + (-6\hat{i}) = 0\hat{i} \\ \text{Vertical: } & (+8\hat{j}) + (-10\hat{j}) + (+10\hat{j}) + (-8\hat{j}) = 0\hat{j} \end{aligned}$$

$$\vec{s}_{net} = 0 \text{ km}$$

steps.title: Final answer and marks
steps.content:
To secure full marks on an IB exam, state your answers clearly with units:

1. **Total distance:** $48 \text{ km}$ (awarded for summing all segments correctly).
2. **Total displacement:** $0 \text{ km}$ (awarded for recognizing the return to the origin).

This confirms the drone has traveled a long path but ended up exactly where it started!

steps.title: Map coordinates for outward trip
steps.content:
To solve this using **Coordinate Mapping**, let's place point $A$ at the origin $(0, 0)$ of a Cartesian plane. We track the drone's position by moving along the $y$-axis (North/South) and the $x$-axis (East/West).

1.  **Point A**: $(0, 0)$
2.  **Point B**: 8 km North $\rightarrow (0, 8)$
3.  **Point C**: 6 km East $\rightarrow (6, 8)$
4.  **Point D**: 10 km South $\rightarrow (6, 8 - 10) = (6, -2)$

steps.title: Map coordinates for return trip
steps.content:
The question states the drone **retraces its path** from $D$ back to $A$. This means it visits the same points in reverse order:

1.  **D to C**: Move from $(6, -2)$ back to $(6, 8)$ (distance = 10 km)
2.  **C to B**: Move from $(6, 8)$ back to $(0, 8)$ (distance = 6 km)
3.  **B to A**: Move from $(0, 8)$ back to $(0, 0)$ (distance = 8 km)

highlights.text: retraced its path from point D back to point A

steps.title: Calculate total distance
steps.content:
Distance is a scalar quantity that tracks the total ground covered. It is the sum of the absolute changes in position for every segment of the journey.

- **Outward distance**: $8 + 6 + 10 = 24$ km
- **Return distance**: $10 + 6 + 8 = 24$ km

Total distance:
$$d_{\text{total}} = 24 \text{ km} + 24 \text{ km} = 48 \text{ km}$$

gdc.instructions: Sum the lengths of all segments.

desmos.instructions: Enter the sum of segments in a new expression line.

calculator.description: Calculate total distance for the round trip.

steps.title: Determine total displacement
steps.content:
Displacement is a vector quantity defined as the change in position. In coordinate mapping, we compare the final position to the initial position.

- **Initial Position (A)**: $(0, 0)$
- **Final Position (A)**: $(0, 0)$

$$\text{Displacement} = \text{Final Position} - \text{Initial Position} = (0, 0) - (0, 0) = 0 \text{ km}$$

steps.title: Final check and marks
steps.content:
In IB Physics (Topic A.1), it is vital to distinguish between distance (path-dependent) and displacement (only depends on start and end points).

- Total Distance: **48 km** (1 mark for correctly summing both legs)
- Total Displacement: **0 km** (1 mark for recognizing the return to the start point)

questions.specification:
Two small spheres, $P$ and $Q$, are launched horizontally from the top of a vertical wall of height $H$. The initial horizontal speeds of the spheres are provided in the following table.

| Sphere | Initial horizontal speed |
| :--- | :--- |
| $P$ | $v$ |
| $Q$ | $3v$ |

Air resistance is negligible. Which of the following correctly compares the time $t$ taken to reach the ground and the horizontal distance $x$ traveled from the wall for the two spheres?

![diagram](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ramen-ramen-ib-chemistry-new-dfd26af2-ad41-4915-a20d-9069a327f1cb-1771938518510/dc44c6f4-9a60-4671-98ff-f37bcaab61a1.jpeg)

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic A - Space, time and motion > A.1 Kinematics > A.1.3 Projectile motion
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.1.3 Projectile motion
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Horizontal launch from a height and comparison of time of flight and horizontal range with different initial horizontal speeds is classic projectile motion with independent horizontal and vertical components.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic A - Space, time and motion > A.1 Kinematics > A.1.2 Equations of motion
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.1.2 Equations of motion
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Solving for time to reach the ground uses kinematics equations for vertical motion under constant acceleration (suvat), though within the broader projectile-motion context.

options.markscheme: In projectile motion with negligible air resistance, horizontal and vertical components of motion are independent. Both spheres have an initial vertical velocity of zero and experience the same vertical acceleration $g$. Therefore, they fall the same vertical distance $H$ in the same time $t = \sqrt{\frac{2H}{g}}$. The horizontal distance (range) is given by $x = v_{horizontal} \times t$. Since the time is the same and $Q$ has three times the horizontal speed of $P$, it will travel three times the horizontal distance: $x_Q = 3v \times t = 3x_P$.

This option incorrectly assumes that time of flight is proportional to the horizontal velocity. The time taken to reach the ground depends only on the vertical height and the vertical acceleration, not the horizontal speed.

While it correctly identifies that the time of flight is identical, it incorrectly suggests that the horizontal range is the same. The range is directly proportional to the initial horizontal velocity when time is constant.
This choice incorrectly implies that a higher horizontal speed reduces the time of flight and results in the same range, which contradicts the principle of independent motion components.

questions.specification:
The graph shows the variation with time $t$ of the velocity $v$ of two particles, $P$ and $Q$, that start from the same position at $t = 0$ and move along the same straight line.

![graph](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ramen-ramen-ib-physics-new-e265cb4f-f960-47b4-b946-73f91935d7dd-1771929956947/0fb79fbc-f69b-49b7-a49a-8d8dc2ed4ee2.jpeg)

Particle $P$ moves with a constant velocity $u$. Particle $Q$ moves with a constant acceleration from rest, reaching velocity $u$ at time $T$.

What is the time taken for particle $Q$ to overtake particle $P$, and what is the velocity of $Q$ at that instant?

| | Time taken to overtake | Velocity of $Q$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| A | $T$ | $u$ |
| B | $T$ | $2u$ |
| C | $2T$ | $u$ |
| D | $2T$ | $2u$ |

parts.content: Calculate the maximum speed reached by the motorbike.
parts.markscheme:
- Substitute values into the kinematic equation: $v = u + at = 0 + 4.00 \cdot 6.00$ <marks>M1</marks>
- State the final answer with units: $24.0\ \text{m s}^{-1}$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Analyze the motion phase
steps.content: To find the maximum speed, we need to identify when the motorbike is moving fastest. The bike starts from rest and accelerates for the first $6.00$ seconds. After this, it maintains a constant speed before finally slowing down. Therefore, the maximum speed is reached at the very end of the first acceleration phase.

steps.title: Identify known SUVAT variables
steps.content:
For the first phase of motion, we can list our known variables based on the information provided in the question:

- Initial velocity ($u$) = $0\ \text{m s}^{-1}$ (since it starts from "rest")
- Acceleration ($a$) = $4.00\ \text{m s}^{-2}$
- Time ($t$) = $6.00\ \text{s}$

We need to find the final velocity ($v$) for this phase, which corresponds to the maximum speed.

steps.title: Apply the kinematic equation
steps.content:
We use the standard kinematic (SUVAT) equation found in the **Mechanics** section of your IB Physics Data Booklet:

$$v = u + at$$

Substituting our values into the equation:

$$v = 0 + (4.00 \times 6.00)$$

This substitution earns the first mark (**M1**) in the markscheme.

gdc.instructions: Multiply the acceleration by the time.

ti84.instructions: Enter the multiplication on the home screen.

desmos.instructions: Type the product into a new expression line.

calculator.description: Calculate the final velocity by multiplying acceleration and time.

steps.title: State final answer
steps.content:
Calculating the product gives us the maximum speed. Remember to include the correct units ($ \text{m s}^{-1}$) and appropriate significant figures as given in the question (3 s.f.).

$$v = 24.0\ \text{m s}^{-1}$$

This final value earns the second mark (**A1**).

steps.title: Visualize the motion phases
steps.content:
To solve this using a velocity-time ($v-t$) graph, we first identify the motion phases. The motorbike starts from rest, so the graph begins at $(0,0)$. 

1. **Phase 1 (0 to 6 s):** The motorbike accelerates uniformly. On a $v-t$ graph, this is a straight line with a constant positive gradient (acceleration).
2. **Phase 2 (6 to 16 s):** It maintains a constant speed. This is a horizontal line.
3. **Phase 3 (16 to 20 s):** It decelerates to rest. This is a straight line with a negative gradient.

The **maximum speed** occurs at the end of the first phase and is held throughout the second phase.

steps.title: Relate gradient to acceleration
steps.content:
In a velocity-time graph, the gradient represents the acceleration. Since the motorbike starts from rest ($u = 0$), the velocity at any time $t$ during the first phase is calculated by:

$$\text{velocity} = \text{gradient} \times \text{time}$$

From the data booklet, this corresponds to the kinematic equation:

$$v = u + at$$

Where:
- $u = 0\text{ m s}^{-1}$
- $a = 4.00\text{ m s}^{-2}$
- $t = 6.00\text{ s}$

steps.title: Calculate the maximum speed
steps.content:
Substitute the values into the equation to find the maximum speed ($v_{\text{max}}$):

$$\begin{aligned} v_{\text{max}} &= 0 + (4.00 \times 6.00) \\ v_{\text{max}} &= 24.0 \text{ m s}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

This is the peak value on our velocity-time graph.

gdc.instructions: Multiply the acceleration by the time elapsed.

ti84.instructions: Enter the multiplication on the main screen.

calculator.description: Calculate the final velocity using acceleration and time.

steps.title: Final Answer
steps.content:
The maximum speed reached by the motorbike is **$24.0\text{ m s}^{-1}$**. 

Make sure to include three significant figures in your answer, as the given values ($4.00$ and $6.00$) also have three.

parts.content: Determine the total distance travelled during the entire journey.
parts.markscheme:
- Calculate the distance for the first phase: $s_1 = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 4.00 \cdot 6.00^2 = 72.0\ \text{m}$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Calculate the distance for the second phase: $s_2 = 24.0 \cdot 10.0 = 240\ \text{m}$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Determine the total distance by summing all phases ($72.0 + 240 + 48.0$): $360\ \text{m}$ <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

steps.title: Analyze the journey phases
steps.content:
To find the total distance, we need to break the motion down into three distinct phases and calculate the displacement ($s$) for each using SUVAT equations from **Topic A.1: Kinematics**.

*   **Phase 1:** Uniform acceleration from rest.
*   **Phase 2:** Constant speed motion.
*   **Phase 3:** Uniform deceleration to rest.

steps.title: Calculate Phase 1 distance
steps.content:
The motorbike starts from rest ($u = 0\ \text{m s}^{-1}$) and accelerates at $4.00\ \text{m s}^{-2}$ for $6.00\ \text{s}$. We use the displacement formula:

$$s_1 = ut + \frac{1}{2}at^2$$

Substituting the values:
$$s_1 = (0)(6.00) + \frac{1}{2}(4.00)(6.00)^2 = 72.0\ \text{m}$$

We also need the final velocity ($v$) of this phase, as it will be the constant speed for Phase 2:
$$v = u + at = 0 + (4.00)(6.00) = 24.0\ \text{m s}^{-1}$$

calculator.description: Calculate distance for Phase 1

steps.title: Calculate Phase 2 distance
steps.content:
During the second phase, the motorbike maintains a constant speed of $24.0\ \text{m s}^{-1}$ for $10.0\ \text{s}$. Since acceleration is zero, the distance is simply:

$$s_2 = v \times t = 24.0 \times 10.0 = 240\ \text{m}$$

calculator.description: Calculate distance for Phase 2

steps.title: Calculate Phase 3 distance
steps.content:
In the final phase, the bike decelerates from $24.0\ \text{m s}^{-1}$ to rest ($v = 0$) in $4.00\ \text{s}$. We can use the average velocity formula for displacement:

$$s_3 = \frac{u+v}{2}t$$

Substituting the values:
$$s_3 = \frac{24.0 + 0}{2} \times 4.00 = 12.0 \times 4.00 = 48.0\ \text{m}$$

calculator.description: Calculate distance for Phase 3

steps.title: Determine total distance
steps.content:
Finally, sum the distances from all three phases to find the total distance traveled:

$$s_{\text{total}} = s_1 + s_2 + s_3$$
$$s_{\text{total}} = 72.0 + 240 + 48.0 = 360\ \text{m}$$

calculator.description: Sum the distances

steps.title: Find the maximum velocity
steps.content:
To use a velocity-time graph, we first need to know the highest velocity reached. The motorbike starts from rest ($u = 0$) and accelerates at $4.00\ \text{m s}^{-2}$ for $6.00\ \text{s}$. 

Using the kinematic equation from the data booklet:
$$\text{v} = \text{u} + \text{at}$$

$$\text{v} = 0 + (4.00 \times 6.00) = 24.0\ \text{m s}^{-1}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the final velocity at the end of the first phase.

steps.title: Analyze the motion phases
steps.content:
A velocity-time graph for this motion would have three distinct parts:
1. **Phase 1 (0 to 6s):** A triangle representing the acceleration from $0$ to $24.0\ \text{m s}^{-1}$.
2. **Phase 2 (6 to 16s):** A rectangle representing the $10.0\ \text{s}$ of constant speed at $24.0\ \text{m s}^{-1}$.
3. **Phase 3 (16 to 20s):** A triangle representing the deceleration from $24.0\ \text{m s}^{-1}$ back to $0$ in $4.00\ \text{s}$.

The total distance travelled is equal to the **area under the velocity-time graph**.

steps.title: Calculate distance (Phase 1)
steps.content:
For the first phase, we calculate the area of the triangle with base $6.00\ \text{s}$ and height $24.0\ \text{m s}^{-1}$:

$$\text{Area}_1 = \frac{1}{2} \times \text{base} \times \text{height}$$
$$\text{s}_1 = 0.5 \times 6.00 \times 24.0 = 72.0\ \text{m}$$

calculator.description: Verify the distance for the first phase.

steps.title: Calculate distance (Phase 2)
steps.content:
For the second phase, we calculate the area of the rectangle with base $10.0\ \text{s}$ and height $24.0\ \text{m s}^{-1}$:

$$\text{Area}_2 = \text{base} \times \text{height}$$
$$\text{s}_2 = 10.0 \times 24.0 = 240\ \text{m}$$

calculator.description: Verify the distance for the constant speed phase.

steps.title: Calculate distance (Phase 3)
steps.content:
For the final phase, we calculate the area of the triangle with base $4.00\ \text{s}$ and height $24.0\ \text{m s}^{-1}$:

$$\text{Area}_3 = \frac{1}{2} \times \text{base} \times \text{height}$$
$$\text{s}_3 = 0.5 \times 4.00 \times 24.0 = 48.0\ \text{m}$$

calculator.description: Verify the distance for the deceleration phase.

steps.title: Sum the total distance
steps.content:
Finally, we sum the distances from all three phases to find the total distance travelled:

$$\text{Total Distance} = \text{s}_1 + \text{s}_2 + \text{s}_3$$
$$\text{Total Distance} = 72.0 + 240 + 48.0 = 360\ \text{m}$$

calculator.description: Sum the distances for the total journey.

parts.content: Sketch a speed–time graph for the full motion of the motorbike. Label axes and all key values.
parts.markscheme:
- Sketch a graph with three distinct segments: linear increase from rest, constant speed, and linear decrease to rest <marks>A1</marks>
- Label axes with units (speed / $\text{m s}^{-1}$ and time / $\text{s}$) and mark all key values ($6.00$, $16.0$, $20.0$ on the time axis and $24.0$ on the speed axis) <marks>A1</marks>

![Speed–time graph](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/plots/62118abb-b182-40ed-8517-3308fde78cda.png)

2 marks total

steps.title: Calculate the peak speed
steps.content:
To plot the graph, we first need to find the maximum speed reached at the end of the first phase. The motorbike starts from rest ($u = 0$) and accelerates uniformly.

Using the kinematic equation from the Data Booklet:

$$v = u + at$$

Substituting the given values:

$$v = 0 + (4.00 \times 6.00) = 24.0\text{ m s}^{-1}$$

steps.title: Determine cumulative timestamps
steps.content:
Next, we calculate the specific time coordinates for the end of each phase to determine where the graph changes shape:

1.  **End of Phase 1:** $t = 6.00\text{ s}$
2.  **End of Phase 2:** The motorbike travels at constant speed for $10.0\text{ s}$. 
    $$t = 6.00 + 10.0 = 16.0\text{ s}$$
3.  **End of Phase 3:** The motorbike decelerates to rest in $4.00\text{ s}$.
    $$t = 16.0 + 4.00 = 20.0\text{ s}$$

highlights.text: maintains a constant speed for $10.0\text{ s}$
decelerates uniformly to rest in $4.00\text{ s}$

steps.title: Define coordinate points
steps.content:
Based on our calculations, we now have four key coordinates $(t, v)$ to plot on the speed-time axes:

-   Start: $(0, 0)$
-   Peak Speed: $(6.00, 24.0)$
-   End of Constant Speed: $(16.0, 24.0)$
-   Final Rest: $(20.0, 0)$

In a speed-time graph, uniform acceleration/deceleration is represented by straight, sloped lines, while constant speed is represented by a horizontal line.

gdc.instructions: Create a list for time: {0, 6, 16, 20} and a list for speed: {0, 24, 24, 0}. Use the 'Scatter Plot' or 'Line Plot' function in the Statistics menu to display the points connected by lines.

ti84.instructions: Go to [STAT] > [EDIT]. In L1, enter the time values {0, 6, 16, 20}. In L2, enter the speed values {0, 24, 24, 0}. Go to [2nd] [STAT PLOT], turn Plot1 ON, select the connected line plot icon. Press [ZOOM] [9] to see the graph.

desmos.instructions: Plot the following points and click the 'line' icon to connect them: (0,0), (6,24), (16,24), and (20,0).

calculator.description: Visualize the motion by plotting the key coordinates as a piecewise function.

steps.title: Sketch and label graph
steps.content:
Finally, sketch the graph ensuring you receive full marks for presentation:

1.  **Axes:** Draw the y-axis as **Speed / $\text{m s}^{-1}$** and the x-axis as **Time / $\text{s}$**.
2.  **Segments:** 
    -   A straight line from $(0,0)$ to $(6.00, 24.0)$.
    -   A horizontal line from $(6.00, 24.0)$ to $(16.0, 24.0)$.
    -   A straight line from $(16.0, 24.0)$ down to $(20.0, 0)$.
3.  **Labels:** Ensure $24.0$ is marked on the y-axis, and $6.00$, $16.0$, and $20.0$ are marked on the x-axis.

highlights.text: Label axes with units (speed / $\text{m s}^{-1}$ and time / $\text{s}$)

parts.content: Discuss how changes in acceleration affect the shape of the speed–time graph and what it reveals about the motorbike's motion.
parts.markscheme:
- State that the gradient of the speed–time graph represents the acceleration of the motorbike <marks>R1</marks>
- Explain that a constant positive gradient shows uniform acceleration, a zero gradient indicates constant speed, and a negative gradient indicates uniform deceleration <marks>R1</marks>

2 marks total

questions.specification:
A small ball is projected with an initial velocity $u$ at an angle to the horizontal, as shown in the diagram below. The dashed line represents the parabolic trajectory of the ball in a vacuum.

![Projected ball diagram](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ramen-ramen-ib-physics-new-e56fefcc-24da-4844-9015-10eca928e176-1771929956947/5c67f2a0-ad86-4ea2-8126-92456576b650.jpeg)

In the presence of air resistance, the ball experiences a drag force proportional to its speed. Which of the following graphs best represents the variation with time $t$ of the horizontal component of the velocity, $v_x$, for the motion of the ball? 

The dotted line in each graph represents the case where air resistance is negligible.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic A - Space, time and motion > A.1 Kinematics > A.1.4 Fluid resistance (qualitative only)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.1.4 Fluid resistance (qualitative only)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Air resistance/drag force proportional to speed and its effect on velocity components is explicitly central; question asks for vx vs t with drag.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic A - Space, time and motion > A.1 Kinematics > A.1.3 Projectile motion
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.1.3 Projectile motion
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The context is projectile motion and comparing vacuum (parabolic) vs motion with air resistance, focusing on the horizontal component during a projectile’s flight.

questions.specification:
A student investigates impulse and its effect on a ball during collisions under different scenarios. The following data was recorded:

$$
\begin{array}{|l|l|l|l|l|}
\hline
\text{Collision Type} & \text{Mass of Ball} / \text{kg} & \text{Initial Velocity } (u) / \text{m s}^{-1} & \text{Final Velocity } (v) / \text{m s}^{-1} & \text{Time of Contact } (\Delta t) / \text{s} \\
\hline
\text{A} & 0.50 & 5.0 & 2.0 & 0.10 \\
\hline
\text{B} & 0.50 & 4.0 & -3.0 & 0.05 \\
\hline
\text{C} & 1.00 & -6.0 & 2.0 & 0.20 \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic A - Space, time and motion > A.2 Forces and momentum > A.2.2 Linear momentum and impulse
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.2.2 Linear momentum and impulse
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Most parts require using impulse as change in momentum (I = m(v−u)) and average force from impulse over contact time (F_avg = Δp/Δt), plus interpreting force direction from sign of Δv and discussing increasing contact time to reduce force.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic A - Space, time and motion > A.2 Forces and momentum > A.2.3 Collisions and explosions
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.2.3 Collisions and explosions
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The scenarios are explicitly collisions with given initial/final velocities (including reversal of direction), requiring qualitative comparison between different collision outcomes and their momentum changes.

parts.content: For collision type A, calculate the impulse experienced by the ball.

steps.title: Identify the given data
steps.content:
To solve this using the change in momentum approach, we first need to extract the specific values for **Collision Type A** from the provided table.

*   Mass of the ball ($m$): $0.50 \text{ kg}$
*   Initial velocity ($u$): $5.0 \text{ m s}^{-1}$
*   Final velocity ($v$): $2.0 \text{ m s}^{-1}$

steps.title: Apply Impulse-Momentum Theorem
steps.content:
In IB Physics **Topic A.2 Forces and momentum**, we learn that impulse ($J$) is equal to the change in momentum ($\Delta p$). We can express this using the following relationship:

$$J = \Delta p = m(v - u)$$

While the data booklet provides the definition for momentum ($p=mv$), you are expected to know that impulse is the difference between final and initial momentum.

steps.title: Calculate the impulse
steps.content:
Now, we substitute the known values into our equation and solve:

$$\begin{aligned} J &= 0.50 \times (2.0 - 5.0) \\ J &= 0.50 \times (-3.0) \\ J &= -1.5 \text{ N s} \end{aligned}$$

The negative sign indicates that the impulse (and therefore the force) was applied in the opposite direction to the ball's initial motion.

gdc.instructions: Enter the expression directly into the calculation screen.

ti84.instructions: Enter the expression directly into the main screen.

desmos.instructions: Type the expression into a new cell.

calculator.description: Calculate the impulse using the mass and change in velocity.

steps.title: Final answer and marks
steps.content:
The final impulse is **$-1.5 \text{ N s}$**.

According to the markscheme, you earn:
*   **1 mark** for substituting the correct values ($0.50$, $2.0$, and $5.0$) into the impulse formula.
*   **1 mark** for the correct numerical answer with units.

steps.title: Identify given values
steps.content:
First, let's list the data provided for **Collision Type A** from the table so we have everything ready for our calculations:

*   Mass ($m$) = $0.50 \text{ kg}$
*   Initial Velocity ($u$) = $5.0 \text{ m s}^{-1}$
*   Final Velocity ($v$) = $2.0 \text{ m s}^{-1}$
*   Time of Contact ($\Delta t$) = $0.10 \text{ s}$

steps.title: Calculate average acceleration
steps.content:
To find the average force later, we first need to determine the acceleration ($a$) of the ball during the collision. We use the kinematic equation for constant acceleration from the data booklet:

$$v = u + at$$

Rearranging to solve for $a$:

$$a = \frac{v - u}{\Delta t}$$

Substituting our values:

$$a = \frac{2.0 \text{ m s}^{-1} - 5.0 \text{ m s}^{-1}}{0.10 \text{ s}} = -30 \text{ m s}^{-2}$$

gdc.instructions: In the main calculation screen, enter (2.0 - 5.0) / 0.10.

calculator.description: Calculate the average acceleration by dividing the change in velocity by time.

steps.title: Determine average net force
steps.content:
Now that we have the acceleration, we can find the average net force ($F$) acting on the ball using Newton's Second Law:

$$F = ma$$

$$F = 0.50 \text{ kg} \times (-30 \text{ m s}^{-2}) = -15 \text{ N}$$

calculator.description: Multiply mass by acceleration to find the force.

steps.title: Calculate the impulse
steps.content:
Finally, we calculate the impulse ($J$) using the relationship between average force and time from the data booklet:

$$J = F \Delta t$$

$$J = -15 \text{ N} \times 0.10 \text{ s} = -1.5 \text{ N s}$$

calculator.description: Multiply force by the contact time to find the impulse.

steps.title: State final answer
steps.content:
The impulse experienced by the ball is **$-1.5 \text{ N s}$**. 

The negative sign indicates that the impulse (and the force) was applied in the opposite direction to the ball's initial motion. According to the markscheme, you earn 1 mark for the correct substitution of values and 1 mark for the final correct result with units.

parts.content: Determine the average force acting on the ball during collision type B.
parts.markscheme:
For collision type B:

$$
\begin{aligned}
J &= m(v-u) \\
J &= 0.50 \times (-3.0 - 4.0) = 0.50 \times (-7.0) = -3.5 \text{ N s}
\end{aligned}
$$
<marks>2 marks</marks>

Average force:

$$
F = \frac{J}{\Delta t} = \frac{-3.5}{0.05} = -70 \text{ N}
$$
<marks>1 mark</marks>

Force $= -70 \text{ N}$ (negative indicates force acted to reverse the motion).

parts.content: Explain why the impulse for collision type C is greater than the impulse for collision type A, even though both involve changes in velocity.
parts.markscheme:
Impulse is greater for collision type C because the ball has a larger mass ($1.00 \text{ kg}$) and a larger total change in velocity ($\Delta v = 2.0 - (-6.0) = 8.0 \text{ m s}^{-1}$) compared to collision type A ($\Delta v = 2.0 - 5.0 = -3.0 \text{ m s}^{-1}$).

*Award <marks>1 mark max</marks> for the fully justified answer.*

parts.content: Compare the directions of forces in collision types A and B. Use data to explain how the direction of force depends on the final and initial velocities.
parts.markscheme:
- For collision type A:
The initial velocity ($u = 5.0 \text{ m s}^{-1}$) is positive, and the final velocity ($v = 2.0 \text{ m s}^{-1}$) is also positive, indicating the force acted to slow the ball down.

- For collision type B:
The initial velocity ($u = 4.0 \text{ m s}^{-1}$) is positive, but the final velocity ($v = -3.0 \text{ m s}^{-1}$) is negative. This shows that the force acted to reverse the ball's direction of motion.

*Award <marks>1 mark max</marks> for each collision type*. <marks>2 marks in total</marks>

steps.title: Relate force to velocity change
steps.content:
To determine the direction of the force, we look at how the velocity changes. According to Newton's Second Law, the force acts in the same direction as the change in velocity (acceleration). 

$$\Delta v = v - u$$

If the force acts in the opposite direction to the initial motion, it will first slow the object down (reducing the magnitude) and, if strong enough, eventually reverse its direction (changing the sign).

steps.title: Analyze Collision Type A
steps.content:
In Collision A, the initial velocity is $u = 5.0\text{ m s}^{-1}$ and the final velocity is $v = 2.0\text{ m s}^{-1}$. 

Since both values are positive, the direction of motion did not change. However, because the magnitude decreased from $5.0$ to $2.0$, we can conclude that the force acted in the opposite direction to the motion to slow the ball down.

ti84.instructions: Subtract the initial velocity from the final velocity.

calculator.description: Calculate the change in velocity for Collision A.

steps.title: Analyze Collision Type B
steps.content:
In Collision B, the initial velocity is $u = 4.0\text{ m s}^{-1}$ and the final velocity is $v = -3.0\text{ m s}^{-1}$.

Notice that the sign changes from positive to negative. This indicates that the force was not only opposite to the initial motion but was applied long enough (or was strong enough) to completely reverse the ball's direction of motion.

calculator.description: Calculate the change in velocity for Collision B.

steps.title: Compare force directions
steps.content:
By comparing the two, we see that in both cases the force acts in the negative direction (opposite to the initial positive velocity). 

- In **Collision A**, the force only slows the ball (same sign, lower magnitude).
- In **Collision B**, the force reverses the ball (change in sign).

steps.title: Identify the relevant formula
steps.content:
To determine the direction of the force, we use the relationship between impulse and change in momentum. According to the **Topic A.2: Forces and momentum** section of your syllabus, the average force $F$ is equal to the rate of change of momentum:

$$F = \frac{\Delta p}{\Delta t} = \frac{m(v - u)}{\Delta t}$$

Since the time of contact $\Delta t$ and mass $m$ are always positive, the **direction** of the force is determined by the sign of the change in velocity $(v - u)$.

steps.title: Calculate force for Collision A
steps.content:
For Collision A, we have $u = 5.0\text{ m s}^{-1}$ and $v = 2.0\text{ m s}^{-1}$. Let's calculate the change in momentum $\Delta p$:

$$\Delta p_A = 0.50(2.0 - 5.0) = -1.5\text{ kg m s}^{-1}$$

The force is:
$$F_A = \frac{-1.5}{0.10} = -15\text{ N}$$

Since the force is negative and the initial velocity was positive, the force acted in the **opposite direction** to the motion, simply slowing the ball down.

calculator.description: Calculate the force for collision A.

steps.title: Calculate force for Collision B
steps.content:
For Collision B, we have $u = 4.0\text{ m s}^{-1}$ and $v = -3.0\text{ m s}^{-1}$. Let's calculate the change in momentum $\Delta p$:

$$\Delta p_B = 0.50(-3.0 - 4.0) = -3.5\text{ kg m s}^{-1}$$

The force is:
$$F_B = \frac{-3.5}{0.05} = -70\text{ N}$$

Again, the force is negative. However, because the final velocity $v$ is now negative, this indicates the force was strong enough to **reverse** the ball's direction of motion.

calculator.description: Calculate the force for collision B.

steps.title: Compare the directions
steps.content:
In both cases, the force acts in the negative direction (opposite to the initial motion). 

1. In **Collision A**, the initial and final velocities have the same sign (positive), meaning the force only **slowed down** the ball.
2. In **Collision B**, the velocities have opposite signs, showing the force was sufficient to **change the direction** of motion.

parts.content: Suggest one practical application where impulse plays a critical role in minimizing force, and explain how the design achieves this.
parts.markscheme:
- Practical application: Airbags in cars
- Explanation: Airbags increase the time of contact during a collision, which reduces the average force experienced by the passengers. By increasing $\Delta t$, the impulse remains constant while force is minimized.

Award <marks>1 mark max</marks> for any outlined application and <marks>1 mark max</marks> for its explanation.

steps.title: Identify a practical application
steps.content: A very common and effective practical application is the use of **airbags in cars**. Other examples include crumple zones, safety mats in gymnastics, or even the soft padding in a helmet. These are all designed using the same physics principles covered in **Topic A.2: Forces and momentum**.

steps.title: Define the momentum change
steps.content:
In a collision, a passenger has a certain mass and is traveling at a specific velocity. To stop safely, their momentum must change from its initial value to zero. This change in momentum is called the **impulse**.

From the data booklet, we know:

$$p = mv$$

Since the mass $m$ and the change in velocity $\Delta v$ are determined by the circumstances of the crash, the total change in momentum (impulse $\Delta p$) is a fixed value for that specific event.

steps.title: Apply the Impulse-Momentum relationship
steps.content:
The relationship between the average force $F$ acting on the passenger and the time $\Delta t$ of the impact is given by the impulse formula:

$$J = F \Delta t$$

We can rearrange this to solve for the average force experienced by the passenger:

$$F = \frac{\Delta p}{\Delta t}$$

steps.title: Explain the design mechanism
steps.content:
The design of an airbag works by being compressible and soft. When a passenger hits the airbag, it takes much longer for them to come to a complete stop compared to hitting a hard surface like a dashboard.

By increasing the **time of contact** ($\Delta t$), and knowing that the change in momentum ($\Delta p$) is constant, the resulting **average force** ($F$) must decrease.

$$\uparrow \Delta t \implies \downarrow F$$

steps.title: Final Summary
steps.content:
To earn full marks on this question:
1.  **Identify the application**: Airbags in cars.
2.  **Explain the physics**: The airbag increases the time of the impact ($\Delta t$). Because $F = \frac{\Delta p}{\Delta t}$, increasing the time for a fixed change in momentum results in a lower average force, which reduces the risk of injury.

steps.title: Identify a practical application
steps.content: A common practical application used to minimize force during a collision is the use of **airbags** in cars (or crumple zones). This design focuses on increasing the distance over which the collision occurs.

steps.title: Relate kinetic energy to work
steps.content:
In a collision, a moving object has a specific amount of kinetic energy ($E_k$) that must be reduced to zero to bring the object to a stop. According to the work-energy theorem, the work done ($W$) on the object must equal its change in kinetic energy.

From the **Mechanics** section of the data booklet:

$$E_{k} = \frac{1}{2}mv^{2}$$

steps.title: Apply the work formula
steps.content:
Work is defined as the product of the average force ($F$) and the displacement ($s$) over which that force acts. To stop the object, the work required is fixed by the initial kinetic energy.

From the **Mechanics** section of the data booklet:

$$W = Fs\cos\theta$$

In a head-on stopping scenario, we consider the magnitude of the work done to dissipate energy over a stopping distance $s$:

$$W = Fs$$

steps.title: Explain the force reduction
steps.content:
By rearranging the work formula for force, we get:

$$F = \frac{W}{s}$$

Airbags are designed to be compressible, which significantly **increases the stopping distance** ($s$) of the passenger compared to hitting a hard surface like a dashboard. Since the work ($W$) required to stop the passenger is constant for a given speed, increasing the distance ($s$) results in a lower average force ($F$) acting on the passenger, thereby reducing the risk of injury.

steps.title: Identify a Practical Application
steps.content:
A common practical application for minimizing force using impulse is the use of **airbags** in cars (or crumple zones, or safety mats in gyms).

This application is covered in **Topic A.2: Forces and momentum**. When a person is involved in a collision, their change in momentum—and therefore the required impulse—is fixed by their initial speed and their mass.

steps.title: Relate Impulse to Graphs
steps.content:
In physics, impulse is defined as the change in momentum. Graphically, if we plot Force ($F$) against time ($t$), the **impulse** is represented by the **area under the Force-time graph**.

From the data booklet, we use the relationship:

$$J = F \Delta t$$

Where $J$ is the impulse, $F$ is the average force, and $\Delta t$ is the time of contact.

steps.title: Analyze the Graphical Area
steps.content:
Imagine two scenarios for a passenger stopping during a crash:
1.  **Without an airbag:** The person hits the hard dashboard. The stop is almost instantaneous (small $\Delta t$). To keep the area (impulse) constant, the "height" of the graph (the force) must be very large.
2.  **With an airbag:** The person sinks into the bag, which increases the time of contact (large $\Delta t$). 

Because the total area under the curve must remain the same (the impulse needed to stop the person is constant), spreading the area over a **longer time interval** automatically results in a **lower peak and average force**.

steps.title: Final Conclusion
steps.content:
By increasing the time of contact $\Delta t$, the airbag ensures that the force $F$ experienced by the passenger is reduced to a safer level. 

**Final Answer Summary:**
- **Application:** Airbags in cars.
- **Explanation:** They increase the time of contact ($\Delta t$). Since Impulse ($J = \text{Area under } F\text{-}t \text{ graph}$) is constant for the collision, increasing the time base of the graph reduces the average and peak force experienced.

questions.specification: An orca whale breaches vertically out of water with a speed of $7.0 \, \mathrm{m\,s^{-1}}$. Neglecting air resistance, how long does the whale remain above the water level before splashing back down?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic A - Space, time and motion > A.1 Kinematics > A.1.2 Equations of motion
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.1.2 Equations of motion
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Requires using constant-acceleration kinematics (SUVAT) under gravity to find time of flight given an initial vertical speed and return to the same height.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic A - Space, time and motion > A.1 Kinematics > A.1.3 Projectile motion
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.1.3 Projectile motion
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: This is effectively a 1D vertical projectile-motion situation (launch upward, then fall back) with no air resistance.

options.markscheme: This is the time to rise to maximum height only: $t=\frac{u}{g}=\frac{7.0}{9.81}=0.713\ \mathrm{s}$, but the whale is above the water for both ascent and descent.

Use symmetry of vertical motion (no air resistance). Time to rise to $v=0$ is $t_{\text{up}}=\frac{u}{g}=\frac{7.0}{9.81}=0.713\ \mathrm{s}$. Total time above water is $t=2t_{\text{up}}=\frac{2u}{g}=\frac{14.0}{9.81}=1.43\ \mathrm{s}\approx1.4\ \mathrm{s}$ (2 s.f.).
This corresponds to incorrectly using $t=\frac{4u}{g}$ (doubling the already round-trip time) or an equivalent factor-of-two error.

This corresponds to incorrectly using $t=\frac{u}{g^2}$ or another dimensional/formula error; the time should be of order $\frac{u}{g}\sim0.7\ \mathrm{s}$ for one way, $\sim1.4\ \mathrm{s}$ for the full time above the surface.
